<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Bourdon Tube Pressure Sensors — Advanced Forms (Spiral & Helical)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A Bourdon tube is an elastic, curved, closed-end metallic tube of non-circular (typically oval or flat-elliptical) cross-section. When internal pressure is applied, the tube tends to straighten due to the differential stress distribution around its curved cross-section, producing a tip displacement proportional to the applied pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **C-type (C-Bourdon)** is the simplest, but it gives only small tip travel (~2–4 mm). To **amplify this displacement** without needing complex linkages, the tube is wound into **spiral** or **helical** shapes, giving 5× to 30× greater deflection at the free end.

### 1.1.1 Spiral Bourdon Tube
- The tube is wound into a **flat spiral** (like a watch mainspring) consisting of 2.5 to 20 turns, all in a **single plane**.
- The free end of the spiral is connected to a **pointer/linkage** that rotates about the central axis.
- **Pressure range:** typically 0.6 bar to 700 bar.
- **Advantages over C-type:** Higher deflection, lower hysteresis, smoother pointer motion.

### 1.1.2 Helical Bourdon Tube
- The tube is wound into a **helix** (like a corkscrew), with multiple turns around a central shaft.
- The free end moves **axially** (along the axis of the helix) when pressure is applied, NOT rotationally.
- **Pressure range:** 0.6 bar to 7000 bar (very high pressure).
- **Advantages:** Very high pressure capability, linear output, and the rotating pointer is replaced by a linear motion that drives a rack-and-pinion or LVDT.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a party horn that you unroll. When you blow into it (increase pressure), the curled-up tube tries to straighten itself. A Bourdon tube works on the **exact same physical principle**, but the straightening is *measured* as a pressure signal.

---

## 1.2 Diaphragm & Capsule Type Pressure Sensors

**Formal Definition:**
A **diaphragm** is a thin circular metallic or non-metallic plate (disc) clamped at its circumference. When pressure is applied across its surface, it deflects proportionally. The deflection magnitude depends on pressure, diameter, thickness, and material properties (Young's modulus, Poisson's ratio).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Capsule** is formed by welding **two diaphragms back-to-back at their edges**, sealing a reference volume between them. Pressure applied to the *outside* of the capsule causes the diaphragms to deflect *toward* each other (or away), giving **double the sensitivity** of a single diaphragm.

### Types of Diaphragms
1. **Flat Metallic Diaphragm** — used in capsule-type sensors and differential pressure (DP) cells.
2. **Corrugated Diaphragm** — has concentric corrugations to:
   - Increase linear range of deflection
   - Reduce hysteresis
   - Increase stiffness control
3. **Capsule (Aneroid)** — two diaphragms welded rim-to-rim; common in barometers and low-pressure gauges.

### Working Principle (Intuitive)
Think of a **drum head (membrane)**: press on one side, the centre bulges out the other. The amount of bulge = function of how hard you pressed. We just need to **measure** the bulge precisely.

---

## 1.3 Motion Balance vs. Force Balance Pressure Sensors

These are **electronic secondary transducers** that convert mechanical motion (from a Bourdon/diaphragm primary element) into a **standardized electrical/pneumatic output signal** (e.g., 4–20 mA or 3–15 psi).

### 1.3.1 Motion Balance System
- The input pressure produces a **small motion** of a sensing element (bellows, diaphragm).
- This motion is detected by a **position sensor** (LVDT, potentiometer, nozzle-flapper, capacitance probe).
- The detected motion is amplified and used to **null-balance** the system using a feedback actuator.
- The feedback force *opposes* the input pressure-induced force.
- **Example:** Electronic pressure transmitter using a force-balance principle with LVDT as motion detector.

### 1.3.2 Force Balance System
- The input pressure exerts a **force** on a bellows/diaphragm.
- This force is balanced against an **electromagnetic or pneumatic feedback force** generated by a coil or nozzle-baffle system.
- The output (current/pressure) is **directly proportional** to the input pressure.
- **Key Difference:** Force-balance uses force-equilibrium feedback (high accuracy), while motion-balance uses displacement-feedback (simpler, moderate accuracy).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** 
> - **Motion-balance** = like a person pushing a door open, but the door *moves* a little before the counterweight engages.
> - **Force-balance** = like a person pushing against a wall — the wall *never moves* because the counterforce exactly equals the push. This is more accurate but more complex.

---

## 1.4 Differential Pressure Elements — Dry Type

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
A **Differential Pressure (DP) Transmitter** measures the difference between two pressures (High-Pressure side HP and Low-Pressure side LP) and converts it into a standard signal. The **"dry" type** means the sensor has **no liquid fill (silicone oil)** protecting the sensing diaphragm — the process pressure acts **directly** on the metallic diaphragm.

### 1.4.1 Construction of Dry-Type DP Cell
- **Two process isolator diaphragms** (one on HP side, one on LP side).
- A **central sensing diaphragm** in between, with strain gauges bonded on it.
- **No oil fill** — the HP and LP pressures act on opposite sides of the central diaphragm.
- Strain gauges form a **Wheatstone bridge** that becomes unbalanced proportional to ΔP.

### 1.4.2 Working Principle
1. Process HP and LP pressures enter through isolator diaphragms.
2. Differential pressure deflects the central diaphragm.
3. Strain gauges change resistance → bridge produces millivolt output.
4. Output is amplified, linearized, and converted to 4–20 mA.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Dry"?** Older DP cells used **oil-filled** chambers to transmit pressure hydrostatically. Oil can leak, freeze, or contaminate process fluids. **Dry cells eliminate these issues**, making them the modern industry standard (e.g., Rosemount 1151, Honeywell ST 3000).

| Parameter | Oil-Filled DP Cell | Dry-Type DP Cell |
|---|---|---|
| Fill fluid | Silicone oil | None (air/gas) |
| Maintenance | High (oil leak risk) | Low |
| Temperature range | Limited | Wider |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Modern usage | Legacy | Standard |
| Overpressure protection | Better | Moderate |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing **Dry-type DP cell** with **Differential Pressure Switch** (a binary device). A DP transmitter gives **continuous** 4–20 mA output proportional to ΔP, while a DP switch gives **ON/OFF** contact closure at a set threshold.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operating Principle of Bourdon Tube — The Physics

A Bourdon tube, when pressurized, tends to **straighten**. The reason lies in the **non-circular cross-section** (oval or flat). 

### Step-by-Step Logic of the "Why":

1. **Initial State:** The tube is curved, cross-section is oval. The outer fibers are in tension; inner fibers are in compression (just like a bent beam).
2. **Internal Pressure Applied:** Pressure acts **normal to the inner wall** at every point. Because the cross-section is non-circular, the **resultant moment** of these pressure forces **tends to open/uncurl** the tube.
3. **Resultant Deflection:** The tip moves outward (uncoils), and the cross-section tends to become **more circular** (reducing the strain energy).
4. **Equilibrium:** Deflection stops when the elastic restoring moment of the tube equals the pressure-induced moment.
5. **Output:** Tip displacement ∝ Applied pressure (P), within elastic limit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not circular cross-section?** A perfectly circular cross-section has *uniform* hoop stress — there is **no net bending moment** to straighten the tube. The **non-circularity** is the key to the Bourdon effect.

---

## 2.2 Bourdon Tube Sensitivity — Why Spiral and Helical?

For a **C-type Bourdon**, tip deflection δ_C is given (simplified) by:

$$
\delta_C \;\propto\; \frac{P \cdot R^2 \cdot a^2}{E \cdot h^3}
$$

Where:
- $P$ = applied pressure
- $R$ = radius of curvature of the tube centerline
- $a$ = minor semi-axis of the oval cross-section
- $E$ = Young's modulus
- $h$ = wall thickness

For a **helical/spiral tube with N turns**, the total tip displacement is approximately **N times** the per-turn contribution (because all turns contribute additively):

$$
\delta_{\text{spiral}} \approx N \cdot \delta_{\text{per turn}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why a **10-turn spiral** can give **30 mm to 50 mm** tip travel, compared to just **2–4 mm** for a C-type — eliminating the need for complex amplification linkages.

---

## 2.3 Diaphragm Deflection — Mathematical Foundation

For a **flat circular diaphragm** of radius $a$, thickness $h$, clamped at the edge, under uniform pressure $P$:

**Maximum Centre Deflection:**

$$
y_{\max} \;=\; \frac{3 P \, a^4 \, (1 - \nu^2)}{16 \, E \, h^3}
$$

**Maximum Stress (at the edge, where diaphragm meets the clamp):**

$$
\sigma_{\max} \;=\; \frac{3 P \, a^2}{4 h^2}
$$

Where:
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio
- $E$ = Young's modulus

### Key Observations:
- Deflection is **inversely proportional to $h^3$** → halving thickness gives **8× more** deflection.
- Deflection is **proportional to $a^4$** → larger diaphragm = much more sensitive.
- Stress is **inversely proportional to $h^2$** → thin diaphragms can rupture under overpressure.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **corrugated diaphragms** are used: the corrugations increase flexibility (more deflection) without making the diaphragm dangerously thin.

---

## 2.4 Capsule Element Analysis

For a **capsule** (two diaphragms welded rim-to-rim), the deflection is **double** that of a single diaphragm (since both diaphragms deflect in the same direction under external pressure):

$$
y_{\text{capsule}} \;\approx\; 2 \cdot y_{\text{single diaphragm}}
$$

Capsules are stacked in series for **higher sensitivity** — used in **altimeters, barometers, and low-pressure gauges** (down to **millibar ranges**).

---

## 2.5 Motion Balance vs. Force Balance — Detailed Comparison

| Feature | Motion Balance | Force Balance |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating principle** | Detect motion, feedback force to reduce motion | Detect force imbalance, feedback force to nullify |
| **Feedback element** | Servo motor, pneumatic relay | Electromagnetic coil, pneumatic nozzle-baffle |
| **Detection element** | LVDT, strain gauge, capacitive probe | Same as motion balance |
| **Output signal** | Proportional to displacement (≈ pressure) | Proportional to feedback force (≈ pressure) |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate (±0.5% to ±1%) | High (±0.1% to ±0.25%) |
| **Dynamic response** | Faster (less mass in feedback) | Slower (more inertia) |
| **Complexity** | Lower | Higher |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Typical use** | Industrial transmitters, indicators | Precision lab-grade transmitters, aerospace |

### 2.5.1 Force Balance Working (Pneumatic Type) — Step-by-Step:
1. Input pressure P₁ enters a **bellows** or diaphragm.
2. This produces a force $F_1 = P_1 \cdot A$ on a **balance beam/lever**.
3. The beam deflects slightly, increasing the **nozzle back-pressure**.
4. The **pneumatic relay (flapper-nozzle)** amplifies this back-pressure.
5. The amplified output pressure (3–15 psi) is fed back to a **bellows on the other end of the beam**.
6. Feedback force $F_2 = P_o \cdot A_f$ balances $F_1$, so beam deflection is **near zero**.
7. At equilibrium: $P_1 \cdot A_1 = P_o \cdot A_f$ → $P_o \propto P_1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why call it "force balance"?** Because the system operates at **near-zero deflection** of the beam. The output is derived from a **force equality**, not a displacement.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y_{\max} = \dfrac{3 P a^4 (1 - \nu^2)}{16 E h^3}$ | Centre deflection of flat circular diaphragm | m |
| 2 | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{3 P a^2}{4 h^2}$ | Maximum stress in clamped diaphragm | Pa |
| 3 | $\delta_{\text{spiral}} \approx N \cdot \delta_{\text{C-type}}$ | Total deflection of spiral Bourdon | m |
| 4 | $y_{\text{capsule}} \approx 2 \cdot y_{\text{diaphragm}}$ | Deflection of capsule element | m |
| 5 | $F_1 = P_1 \cdot A_1$ | Input force on balance beam | N |
| 6 | $F_2 = P_o \cdot A_f$ | Feedback force from output bellows | N |
| 7 | $P_o = P_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_1}{A_f}$ | Force-balance output pressure | Pa |
| 8 | $\Delta R = G \cdot \varepsilon \cdot R$ | Strain gauge resistance change | Ω |
| 9 | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Wheatstone bridge output (quarter-bridge) | V |
| 10 | $\Delta P = P_{\text{HP}} - P_{\text{LP}}$ | Differential pressure definition | Pa |

> [!WARNING]
> Do not confuse **diaphragm deflection $y$** with **Bourdon tip displacement $\delta$**. They use different geometric parameters. Also remember that $\nu$ (Poisson's ratio) appears only in diaphragm equations, not in simple Bourdon formulas.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Sensor Type | Typical Application |
|---|---|
| Spiral Bourdon | Industrial pressure gauges (steam, hydraulic), 0.6–700 bar |
| Helical Bourdon | High-pressure gauges, test benches, dead-weight testers |
| Diaphragm | Low-pressure gauges, vacuum, cleanroom monitoring |
| Capsule | Barometers, altimeters, weather instruments |
| Motion-balance transmitter | Process industries (level, flow via DP) |
| Force-balance transmitter | Precision flow, aerospace, lab instrumentation |
| Dry-type DP cell | Level measurement, flow (orifice plate), filter monitoring |

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Diaphragm Centre Deflection (Plate Theory)

### Problem Setup
A circular diaphragm of radius $a$, thickness $h$, made of material with Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$, is rigidly clamped at its edge and subjected to uniform pressure $P$ on one side.

### Step 1: Governing Equation for Plate Deflection

For a thin isotropic plate (Kirchhoff-Love plate theory), the governing differential equation is:

$$
D \left( \frac{d^4 y}{dr^4} + \frac{2}{r} \frac{d^3 y}{dr^3} - \frac{1}{r^2} \frac{d^2 y}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r^3} \frac{dy}{dr} \right) = P
$$

where $D$ is the **flexural rigidity** of the plate:

$$
D \;=\; \frac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}
$$

### Step 2: Solve by Integration

For axisymmetric loading, integrate the plate equation twice. The general solution of the resulting inhomogeneous ODE is:

$$
y(r) \;=\; \frac{P r^4}{64 D} + C_1 \ln(r) + C_2 r^2 \ln(r) + C_3 r^2 + C_4
$$

### Step 3: Apply Boundary Conditions

For a **clamped** circular plate:
- **B.C. 1:** Deflection is zero at the rim: $y(a) = 0$
- **B.C. 2:** Slope is zero at the rim: $\left. \dfrac{dy}{dr} \right|_{r=a} = 0$
- **B.C. 3:** Deflection is finite (no singularity) at the center: $y(0) \neq \infty$ → $C_1 = 0$, $C_2 = 0$
- **B.C. 4:** Slope is zero at the center (axisymmetric): $\left. \dfrac{dy}{dr} \right|_{r=0} = 0$ → $C_2 = 0$

Applying B.C. 3 and B.C. 4 to the simplified equation:

$$
y(r) \;=\; \frac{P r^4}{64 D} + C_3 r^2 + C_4
$$

### Step 4: Apply Clamped-Edge Conditions

Setting $y(a) = 0$:

$$
0 \;=\; \frac{P a^4}{64 D} + C_3 a^2 + C_4 \quad \cdots \text{(i)}
$$

Setting $\left. \dfrac{dy}{dr} \right|_{r=a} = 0$:

$$
\frac{dy}{dr} \;=\; \frac{P r^3}{16 D} + 2 C_3 r
$$

$$
0 \;=\; \frac{P a^3}{16 D} + 2 C_3 a \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_3 = -\frac{P a^2}{32 D} \quad \cdots \text{(ii)}
$$

Substitute $C_3$ into (i):

$$
C_4 \;=\; \frac{P a^4}{64 D} - \frac{P a^4}{32 D} \;=\; -\frac{P a^4}{64 D}
$$

### Step 5: Final Deflection Profile

$$
y(r) \;=\; \frac{P}{64 D} \left( a^4 - 2 a^2 r^2 + r^4 \right) \;=\; \frac{P (a^2 - r^2)^2}{64 D}
$$

### Step 6: Maximum Deflection at Centre ($r = 0$)

$$
y_{\max} \;=\; y(0) \;=\; \frac{P a^4}{64 D}
$$

Substitute back $D = \dfrac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$:

$$
y_{\max} \;=\; \frac{P a^4}{64} \cdot \frac{12(1-\nu^2)}{E h^3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,y_{\max} \;=\; \frac{3 P a^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E h^3}\,}
$$

### Step 7: Maximum Bending Stress (at the clamped edge)

The bending moment per unit length in a circular plate is given by:

$$
M_r(r) \;=\; -D \left( \frac{d^2 y}{dr^2} + \frac{\nu}{r} \frac{dy}{dr} \right)
$$

Differentiating the deflection profile:

$$
\frac{dy}{dr} = \frac{P}{64 D} \cdot \left( -4 a^2 r + 4 r^3 \right) = \frac{P \, r\,(r^2 - a^2)}{16 D}
$$

$$
\frac{d^2 y}{dr^2} = \frac{P}{16 D} \cdot (3 r^2 - a^2)
$$

At $r = a$:

$$
\left. \frac{d^2 y}{dr^2} \right|_{r=a} = \frac{P}{16 D} \cdot (2 a^2) = \frac{P a^2}{8 D}
$$

$$
\left. \frac{dy}{dr} \right|_{r=a} = 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
M_r(a) = -D \cdot \frac{P a^2}{8 D} = -\frac{P a^2}{8}
$$

Maximum bending stress (using $\sigma = \dfrac{6 M}{h^2}$ for surface fibre of plate):

$$
\sigma_{\max} = \frac{6 \cdot P a^2 / 8}{h^2} = \frac{3 P a^2}{4 h^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\sigma_{\max} \;=\; \frac{3 P a^2}{4 h^2}\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Force-Balance Transmitter Output Equation

### Setup
A pneumatic force-balance transmitter has an **input bellows** of area $A_1$ and a **feedback bellows** of area $A_f$. Input pressure $P_1$ enters $A_1$. Output pressure $P_o$ is fed back to $A_f$. The system reaches equilibrium when beam deflection is zero.

### Step 1: Force on Input Bellows
$$
F_{\text{in}} = P_1 \cdot A_1
$$

### Step 2: Force on Feedback Bellows
$$
F_{\text{fb}} = P_o \cdot A_f
$$

### Step 3: Equilibrium (Beam Deflection → 0)
At equilibrium, the moments about the pivot are balanced. With a 1:1 lever ratio for simplicity:

$$
P_1 \cdot A_1 \cdot L_1 \;=\; P_o \cdot A_f \cdot L_f
$$

Assuming $L_1 = L_f$ (symmetric beam):

$$
P_1 \cdot A_1 = P_o \cdot A_f
$$

### Step 4: Solve for Output Pressure
$$
P_o = \frac{A_1}{A_f} \cdot P_1
$$

### Step 5: Sensitivity (Gain)
$$
K = \frac{dP_o}{dP_1} = \frac{A_1}{A_f}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $A_1 < A_f$, then $K < 1$ → output is a *fraction* of input (range suppression). If $A_1 > A_f$, the output is *amplified*. By choosing $A_1 / A_f$, the span of the transmitter can be tuned.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Diaphragm Sensor

**Problem:** A circular diaphragm of radius $a = 25$ mm, thickness $h = 0.5$ mm is made of stainless steel ($E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$). Find:
(a) Centre deflection for $P = 100$ kPa.
(b) Maximum bending stress.
(c) Verify the deflection is within safe range if $\sigma_{\text{yield}} = 250$ MPa.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Using $y_{\max} = \dfrac{3 P a^4 (1 - \nu^2)}{16 E h^3}$

$$
1 - \nu^2 = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91
$$

$$
y_{\max} = \frac{3 \times (100 \times 10^3) \times (0.025)^4 \times 0.91}{16 \times (200 \times 10^9) \times (0.0005)^3}
$$

$$
= \frac{3 \times 10^5 \times 3.906 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.91}{16 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.0665 \times 10^{-1}}{4.0 \times 10^{2}} = 2.666 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,y_{\max} \approx 0.267 \text{ mm}\,}
$$

**Part (b):** Using $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{3 P a^2}{4 h^2}$

$$
\sigma_{\max} = \frac{3 \times 10^5 \times (0.025)^2}{4 \times (0.0005)^2}
$$

$$
= \frac{3 \times 10^5 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

$$
= \frac{187.5}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.875 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\sigma_{\max} = 187.5 \text{ MPa}\,}
$$

**Part (c):** Safety factor = $\dfrac{250}{187.5} = 1.33$

The diaphragm operates with a **factor of safety of 1.33**, which is acceptable for low-cycle industrial use but **not for fatigue-critical** applications.

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a spiral and a helical Bourdon tube. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **spiral Bourdon tube** is wound into a flat spiral in a single plane with multiple turns, and its free end rotates about a central axis to drive a pointer. It is used in **industrial pressure gauges** for medium ranges (0.6–700 bar).

A **helical Bourdon tube** is wound into a corkscrew-shaped helix with multiple turns, and its free end translates **axially** along the helix axis. It is used in **high-pressure measurement** (up to 7000 bar) and test benches.

**Key distinction:** Spiral = rotational tip motion (one plane). Helical = axial tip motion (3D).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a capsule-type pressure sensor? How does it differ from a single diaphragm?**

**Model Answer:**

A **capsule** is formed by welding two diaphragms rim-to-rim, sealing a reference volume (often vacuum or low-pressure gas) inside. When external pressure is applied, both diaphragms deflect in the same direction, giving **twice the deflection** of a single diaphragm of the same material and dimensions.

**Differences from single diaphragm:**

| Feature | Single Diaphragm | Capsule |
|---|---|---|
| Number of plates | 1 | 2 |
| Deflection magnitude | y | 2y |
| Sensitivity | Lower | Higher |
| Reference pressure | Atmospheric on one side | Sealed gas/vacuum inside |
| Use | Standard gauges | Low-pressure, barometers |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the centre deflection and maximum bending stress of a flat circular diaphragm clamped at its edges and subjected to uniform pressure. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flat circular stainless-steel diaphragm has a radius of 20 mm and a thickness of 0.4 mm. If the applied pressure is 200 kPa, calculate the centre deflection and maximum stress. Take $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Derivation:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Diaphragm is thin: $h \ll a$ (Kirchhoff-Love plate theory valid).
2. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.
3. Deflection is small compared to thickness: $y \ll h$ (linear theory).
4. Clamping is perfectly rigid at the rim.
5. Pressure is uniformly distributed.

**Governing Plate Equation:**

$$
D \nabla^4 y = P
$$

with flexural rigidity $D = \dfrac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$.

**For axisymmetric loading, the general solution after integration and applying boundary conditions** (deflection = 0 and slope = 0 at the clamped edge $r = a$; finite and symmetric at center) **gives:**

$$
y(r) = \frac{P (a^2 - r^2)^2}{64 D}
$$

**Maximum deflection at centre ($r = 0$):**

$$
\boxed{\,y_{\max} = \frac{P a^4}{64 D} = \frac{3 P a^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E h^3}\,}
$$

**Maximum bending stress at the clamped edge:**

$$
\boxed{\,\sigma_{\max} = \frac{3 P a^2}{4 h^2}\,}
$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $a = 20$ mm $= 0.020$ m, $h = 0.4$ mm $= 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $P = 200$ kPa $= 2 \times 10^5$ Pa, $E = 2 \times 10^{11}$ Pa, $\nu = 0.3$.

**Step 1: Compute $1 - \nu^2$**
$$
1 - \nu^2 = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91
$$

**Step 2: Centre Deflection**
$$
y_{\max} = \frac{3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times (0.020)^4 \times 0.91}{16 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times (4 \times 10^{-4})^3}
$$

Numerator: $3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.91 = 8.736 \times 10^{-2}$

Denominator: $16 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 6.4 \times 10^{-11} = 204.8$

$$
y_{\max} = \frac{8.736 \times 10^{-2}}{204.8} = 4.266 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,y_{\max} \approx 0.427 \text{ mm}\,}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum Bending Stress**
$$
\sigma_{\max} = \frac{3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times (0.020)^2}{4 \times (4 \times 10^{-4})^2}
$$

Numerator: $3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 240$

Denominator: $4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-7} = 6.4 \times 10^{-7}$

$$
\sigma_{\max} = \frac{240}{6.4 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.75 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\sigma_{\max} = 375 \text{ MPa}\,}
$$

**Step 4: Safety Check** — If $\sigma_{\text{yield}} = 250$ MPa, the diaphragm would **fail** (stress exceeds yield). This indicates the design must be revised (e.g., increase $h$ to 0.5 mm to reduce stress).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a force-balance pneumatic pressure transmitter with a neat block diagram. Derive the relationship between input and output pressure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare motion-balance and force-balance pressure transmitters in detail. Which is more accurate and why? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Force-Balance Pneumatic Pressure Transmitter:**

A force-balance transmitter operates on the principle of **force equilibrium** — the output pressure is generated such that the feedback force exactly balances the input force, keeping the beam deflection near zero.

**Working:**

1. Input pressure $P_1$ enters the input bellows of area $A_1$, producing force $F_1 = P_1 \cdot A_1$ on the balance beam.
2. The beam deflects slightly (micro-deflection), reducing the gap between nozzle and flapper.
3. Nozzle back-pressure rises.
4. The pneumatic relay amplifies this back-pressure to produce output pressure $P_o$ (3–15 psi).
5. Output $P_o$ is fed back to the feedback bellows of area $A_f$ on the opposite end of the beam.
6. Feedback force $F_2 = P_o \cdot A_f$ counteracts $F_1$.
7. At equilibrium, $F_1 = F_2$ → beam returns to near-zero deflection.

**Derivation:**

At equilibrium:

$$
P_1 \cdot A_1 = P_o \cdot A_f
$$

Solving for $P_o$:

$$
\boxed{\,P_o = \frac{A_1}{A_f} \cdot P_1\,}
$$

The ratio $A_1 / A_f$ is the **gain** of the transmitter. By adjusting $A_1$ or $A_f$, the span can be tuned.

**(b) Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Motion Balance | Force Balance |
|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | Detect motion, use feedback to reduce motion | Detect force imbalance, use feedback to nullify force |
| Beam deflection at steady state | Small but non-zero | Essentially zero |
| Detection element | LVDT, strain gauge, flapper-nozzle | Strain gauge, LVDT |
| Feedback actuator | Electromagnetic coil, pneumatic relay | Electromagnetic coil, pneumatic relay |
| Output signal | Proportional to motion (≈ pressure) | Proportional to feedback force (≈ pressure) |
| Linearity | Good (depends on motion sensor) | Excellent (null-balance is inherently linear) |
| Accuracy | ±0.5 to ±1% FS | ±0.1 to ±0.25% FS |
| Hysteresis | Moderate | Very low |
| Dynamic response | Faster (lower feedback mass) | Slower (higher feedback mass) |
| Cost and complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Typical applications | Process transmitters (general) | Precision flow, aerospace, lab-grade measurement |

**More Accurate:** **Force-balance** transmitters are more accurate because they operate at **null deflection** — minimizing non-linearity, hysteresis, and friction effects inherent in moving mechanical components. The output depends only on the ratio of bellows areas, which can be manufactured to high precision.

**Limitation:** Force-balance systems have **slower dynamic response** due to the larger feedback mass, and they require **higher-quality components**, making them more expensive.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall in KTU Valuation:** When asked to "derive the relationship," many students write only the equation $P_o = P_1 \cdot (A_1/A_f)$ without justifying **how the feedback loop maintains equilibrium**. Examiners deduct up to 3 marks if you skip the "beam-deflects → flapper moves → relay amplifies → feedback bellows counteracts" sequence. **Always describe the physical process in steps before writing the math.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Second Common Pitfall:** Confusing **dry-type DP cell** with **diaphragm-type DP cell with oil fill**. In KTU questions, "dry" specifically means **no silicone oil between the isolator diaphragms and the sensing diaphragm**. The strain gauges sit on the **sensing diaphragm itself** and are directly exposed to the differential pressure. Writing "oil is used to transmit pressure" for a *dry-type* cell will fetch **zero marks** for that part.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before you walk into the exam, ensure you can confidently answer the following:**

### Bourdon Tubes
- ☐ State the working principle: non-circular cross-section → unbalanced bending moment → tube straightens.
- ☐ Differentiate C-type, spiral, and helical Bourdon tubes by **tip motion** (linear / rotational / axial).
- ☐ Recall that **N-turn spiral** gives approximately **N times** the C-type deflection.
- ☐ Know the typical pressure ranges (spiral: ≤700 bar; helical: ≤7000 bar).
- ☐ Mention materials: phosphor bronze, beryllium copper, stainless steel.

### Diaphragms and Capsules
- ☐ Recall the **two key equations**:
  - $y_{\max} = \dfrac{3 P a^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E h^3}$
  - $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{3 P a^2}{4 h^2}$
- ☐ State the four assumptions of Kirchhoff-Love plate theory.
- ☐ Define a **corrugated diaphragm** and its purpose (linearity + safety).
- ☐ Define a **capsule** and recall $y_{\text{capsule}} = 2 \cdot y_{\text{diaphragm}}$.
- ☐ Know applications: barometer (capsule), low-pressure gauge (diaphragm), pressure switch (corrugated).

### Motion vs. Force Balance
- ☐ Define motion-balance: detects small motion, feedback reduces motion.
- ☐ Define force-balance: detects force imbalance, feedback nullifies force.
- ☐ Draw the block diagram of a **pneumatic force-balance transmitter**.
- ☐ Derive $P_o = (A_1 / A_f) \cdot P_1$ from the force equilibrium.
- ☐ Identify which is more accurate (**force balance**) and why (null-deflection operation).
- ☐ Identify which has faster dynamic response (**motion balance**).

### Dry-Type DP Cells
- ☐ Define DP transmitter: measures $P_{HP} - P_{LP}$.
- ☐ State what "dry" means: **no silicone oil fill** between isolator and sensing diaphragms.
- ☐ Identify sensing element: **strain gauge bridge on central diaphragm**.
- ☐ List advantages over oil-filled: no leak, no contamination, wider temperature range, lower cost.
- ☐ List applications: level (tank), flow (orifice plate), filter ΔP, density measurement.

### KTU-Favorite Questions
- ☐ "Derive diaphragm centre deflection" — must show **all boundary conditions** explicitly.
- ☐ "Compare motion and force balance" — table format is best.
- ☐ "Explain dry-type DP cell with block diagram" — emphasize **no oil** and **direct strain gauge exposure**.
- ☐ "Differentiate spiral and helical Bourdon" — emphasize **plane of motion** (rotational vs. axial).

---

**END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — Pressure Sensors (Bourdon, Diaphragm, Motion/Force Balance, Dry-Type DP)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: force balance and dry
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT865 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** force balance and dry

# Force Balance Principle and Dry-Type Instrumentation Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 — Pressure Measurement):**
> The **Force Balance Principle** and **Dry-Type / Wet-Type classification** form the backbone of modern industrial pressure transmitters. Every high-accuracy pneumatic, hydraulic, and electronic smart transmitter (Rosemount / Honeywell / Yokogawa) is built on the force-balance philosophy.

### 1.1 What is the Force Balance Principle?

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 terminology):**
> *Force balance* is a closed-loop, null-deflection measurement technique in which the unknown input measurand force (produced by the primary sensing element) is continuously counter-balanced by an equivalent, automatically generated feedback force generated by a pneumatic, hydraulic, or electromagnetic actuator. The system always operates at, or extremely near, the **null deflection** position of the sensing element.

In simple words: the input force is *always* matched (balanced) by an equal and opposite feedback force, so the moving parts never actually move appreciably. The output is proportional to the magnitude of the feedback force required to restore equilibrium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** The sensing element does not deflect significantly — its role is reduced to a force comparator, not a displacement indicator. This eliminates the non-linearities, hysteresis, and friction errors of conventional deflection-type gauges.

**Real-World Analogy — "The Spring and the Hand":**
Imagine pushing down on a bathroom weighing scale with your hand. In a *deflection-type* scale, the spring deflects and the dial reads your weight. In a *force-balance* scale, the moment you push, a tiny motor instantly extends a counter-spring upward with exactly the same force your hand applies. The platform barely moves. The current drawn by that motor is the exact reading of your weight. This is precisely how a Rosemount 3051 pressure transmitter works — the measured pressure pushes a diaphragm, and a magnet coil immediately pushes back with equal force.

### 1.2 What is "Dry-Type" in Pressure Measurement?

**Formal Definition:**
> A **dry-type** pressure instrument is one in which the pressure-sensing element is in **direct contact** with the process fluid, with **no intermediate manometric (filling) liquid** used to transmit pressure to the gauge mechanism.

**Counter-definition — Wet-Type / Filled System:**
> A **wet-type** (also called *liquid-filled* or *inverted-bell filled*) instrument uses a manometric fluid (mercury, oil, silicone, glycol-water mixture) to isolate the process and/or transmit pressure from the sensing point to the indicating point.

**Intuitive Analogy — "Bare Finger vs. Rubber-Gloved Finger":**
- **Dry-type:** You press your bare finger directly on a hot surface — you feel it instantly. *(Direct contact, fast response, no isolation, no corrosion protection.)*
- **Wet-type:** You press a gloved finger (with gel inside) — the gel distributes the pressure, protects your finger, and smooths the reading. *(Isolated, damped, safer for corrosive media, slower response.)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this classification matters in industry:** Process fluids are often *hot, corrosive, viscous, slurry-laden, or explosive*. You cannot let them touch a delicate Bourdon tube or strain gauge. Hence the *dry vs. wet* choice is a critical engineering design decision dictated by **media compatibility, safety, damping, and maintenance** requirements.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Standard Value (SI) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Gravitational acceleration $g$ | **9.80665 m/s²** |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | **101.325 kPa** (= 1.01325 bar) |
| Mercury density (25 °C) | **13,534 kg/m³** |
| Process oil density (typical silicone) | **~ 950 kg/m³** |
| Standard process temperature | **25 °C / 298.15 K** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Force Balance Equation — Derivation of the Operating Principle

Consider the canonical **pneumatic force-balance pressure transmitter** (Foxboro 13A / Moore 77 series, still cited in the KTU 2024 syllabus as the pedagogical reference).

### Step 1 — Identify the Four Forces on the Beam/Flapper

The pivoted beam carries a flapper-nozzle assembly. Four forces act on it:

1. **Input measuring force** $F_i$ — generated by the differential pressure capsule (bellows or diaphragm).
2. **Feedback bellows force** $F_f$ — pneumatic pressure (3–15 psi) acting on the feedback bellows.
3. **Range weight force** $F_r$ — a fixed zero-adjustment weight.
4. **Moment balance condition** — torque about the pivot = 0.

### Step 2 — Apply Moment Equilibrium About the Flapper Pivot

Let the input bellows be at distance $a$ from the pivot and the feedback bellows be at distance $b$ from the pivot.

$$\sum \tau = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_i \cdot a = F_f \cdot b$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Null-Balance Condition:** Because the beam never deflects measurably, the flapper position relative to the nozzle is restored to the same point in every cycle. The nozzle back-pressure is therefore a *pure function* of the feedback pressure, not of beam displacement.

### Step 3 — Express the Output in Terms of the Measurand

Solving for the feedback (output) pressure $P_o$:

$$P_o = \left(\frac{A_i}{A_f}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{a}{b}\right) \cdot P_i + \text{zero offset}$$

where:
- $A_i$ = effective area of the input sensing element
- $A_f$ = effective area of the feedback bellows
- $P_i$ = input differential pressure (the measurand)
- $a/b$ = beam (lever) ratio — sets the **span** of the transmitter

**Key Engineering Insight:** The **span** is set mechanically by the lever ratio, and the **zero** is set by a spring/baffle adjustment. This mechanical trim-ability is one of the biggest reasons force-balance transmitters dominated process control from 1940–1985.

### Step 2.4 — The Pneumatic Amplifier (Nozzle–Flapper + Relay)

The nozzle–flapper stage converts the tiny flapper motion ($\mu$m level) into a large pneumatic signal (3–15 psi). A typical relay has a gain of 100–200. This makes the **loop gain** enormous, ensuring the null condition is held with extreme precision.

### 2.2 Dry-Type vs. Wet-Type — Decision Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Marks are often awarded for *justifying* a dry vs. wet choice given a process scenario. Memorize the trade-offs.

| Parameter | Dry-Type | Wet-Type (Filled System) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Process contact** | Direct | Through filling fluid |
| **Corrosion resistance** | Low — limited to sensor material | High — fill fluid shields the gauge |
| **Response time** | Fast (no fluid lag) | Slower (viscous damping) |
| **Damping of pulsations** | Poor — needs external snubber | Excellent — inherent viscous damping |
| **Maintenance** | Difficult (sensor clogs/fouls) | Easy (only fill fluid touched) |
| **Over-range protection** | Poor | Good (fluid cushions the element) |
| **Temperature error** | Depends on sensing element only | Compensated by fill-fluid choice |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Typical uses** | Clean gases, steam (with siphon), dry air | Slurries, acids, viscous liquids, hot oils |
| **Example instrument** | Capsule, Bourdon (dry), strain gauge | Inverted bell, manometer, filled Bourdon |

### 2.3 The Bourdon Tube — A Foundational Dry-Type Element

The C-shaped Bourdon tube is the *quintessential dry-type* pressure element. When internal pressure increases, the closed end tries to straighten; the tip motion is coupled via a linkage to a pointer or, in modern transmitters, to a force-balance beam.

$$P = \frac{E \cdot t^2 \cdot (1 - \nu^2)}{1.5 \cdot D^2 \cdot (1 + \nu)^2} \cdot \left(\frac{\delta}{r}\right)$$

where $E$ = Young's modulus, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio, $t$ = wall thickness, $D$ = major axis, $r$ = minor axis, $\delta$ = tip deflection. (Cited for awareness — full derivation not in KTU scope.)

### 2.4 Filled (Wet-Type) Thermal System — Why "Dry" is the Contrast

In a *filled thermal system* (used in temperature measurement, often taught alongside pressure in Module 2):
- The **bulb** contains a liquid, gas, or vapor.
- The **capillary** transmits pressure to the Bourdon or bellows.
- The system is "wet" because a manometric fluid fills the closed volume.

A *mercury-filled* system is wet; a *gas-filled* system (e.g., nitrogen) is technically dry in the capillary but the system is still classified as *filled* because of the sealed gas column. KTU examiners often test this nuance.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use |
| :-: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $F_i \cdot a = F_f \cdot b$ | Moment balance on force-balance beam |
| 2 | $P_o = \dfrac{A_i \, a}{A_f \, b} P_i$ | Output pressure of pneumatic transmitter |
| 3 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic (wet) pressure at depth $h$ |
| 4 | $\Delta P = (\rho_{fill} - \rho_{proc}) g \, h$ | Differential pressure in inverted-bell system |
| 5 | $\delta = K \cdot P \cdot \dfrac{r^2}{t^3}$ | Bourdon tip deflection (proportional form) |
| 6 | $E_{loop} = G_{nozzle} \cdot G_{relay} \cdot G_{feedback}$ | Closed-loop gain of force-balance loop |
| 7 | $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ | Standard atmospheric pressure |
| 8 | $\text{Span} \propto a/b$ | Lever-ratio sets the transmitter span |
| 9 | $\tau = \dfrac{\mu L}{A \cdot \Delta P}$ | Time constant of a filled (wet) system |
| 10 | $F_b = P_{back} \cdot A_{nozzle} - P_{atm} \cdot A_{nozzle}$ | Net force on feedback bellows |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Quick-Fire Rule:**
> *If the exam question says "the flapper never moves" — it's a force-balance, and the **moment equation** is the answer key.*
> *If the question says "mercury column" or "silicon oil" — it's a wet/filled system, and $P = \rho g h$ is your formula.*

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Transfer Function of a Pneumatic Force-Balance Transmitter

This derivation is the **single most expected numerical/derivation problem** in KTU's Industrial Instrumentation Module 2. We will proceed line by line.

### Step 0 — System Description (Text Setup)

A horizontal beam is pivoted at its centre. On the left arm:
- An **input bellows** of effective area $A_i$ at distance $a$ from the pivot senses the differential pressure $P_i$.
- A **range spring** (zero adjust) provides an upward force $F_z$ at distance $a$.

On the right arm:
- A **feedback bellows** of area $A_f$ at distance $b$ from the pivot receives the output pneumatic pressure $P_o$.
- A **flapper** extends from the right end of the beam, modulating the nozzle back-pressure $P_b$.

The **nozzle–flapper–relay** assembly acts as a high-gain pneumatic amplifier: any minute motion of the flapper toward the nozzle raises the nozzle back-pressure $P_b$, which (after amplification by the relay) raises the feedback bellows pressure $P_o$, which (via the beam) pushes the flapper *back* away from the nozzle — completing the negative feedback loop.

### Step 1 — Force Equation on the Input Side

The downward force exerted by the input pressure on the beam:

$$F_i = P_i \cdot A_i \quad \text{[N]}$$

This force acts at the moment arm $a$ from the pivot, producing a clockwise torque $\tau_{cw}$ (taking clockwise as positive by convention):

$$\tau_{cw} = F_i \cdot a = P_i \cdot A_i \cdot a$$

### Step 2 — Force Equation on the Feedback Side

The upward force exerted by the feedback bellows on the beam (since the feedback bellows acts to oppose the input motion, it pushes the beam back up):

$$F_f = P_o \cdot A_f \quad \text{[N]}$$

This force acts at moment arm $b$, producing a counter-clockwise torque $\tau_{ccw}$:

$$\tau_{ccw} = F_f \cdot b = P_o \cdot A_f \cdot b$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Null (Force-Balance) Condition

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Assumption (Null-Balance):** Because the high-gain pneumatic relay drives the system to equilibrium within microseconds, the beam deflection is negligibly small. The spring forces from the beam's own stiffness therefore vanish. We impose pure moment equilibrium:

$$\sum \tau = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_i \cdot A_i \cdot a = P_o \cdot A_f \cdot b$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Output Pressure $P_o$

Rearranging:

$$P_o = \left(\frac{A_i}{A_f}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{a}{b}\right) \cdot P_i$$

Defining the **span constant** $K_{span} = \dfrac{A_i \cdot a}{A_f \cdot b}$:

$$\boxed{P_o = K_{span} \cdot P_i}$$

**Interpretation:** The output pneumatic pressure $P_o$ is **linearly proportional** to the input differential pressure $P_i$. The proportionality constant $K_{span}$ is set by mechanical geometry (areas and lever arms) — this is the engineering *elegance* of force balance.

### Step 5 — Add the Zero Suppression (Range Spring)

In real transmitters, a range spring pre-loads the beam so that the lowest input pressure (e.g., 0 kPa) produces the lowest output (3 psi). The modified equation:

$$P_o = 3 \text{ psi} + K_{span} \cdot (P_i - P_{i,\min})$$

For a standard 3–15 psi output over a 0–100 kPa input range:

$$K_{span} = \frac{15 - 3}{100 - 0} = 0.12 \text{ psi/kPa}$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A pneumatic force-balance transmitter has $A_i = 5 \text{ cm}^2$, $A_f = 2 \text{ cm}^2$, lever arm ratio $a/b = 0.8$. The input range is 0–80 kPa. Find the output pneumatic pressure when $P_i = 50$ kPa.

**Solution:**

Step (i) — Compute the span constant:

$$K_{span} = \frac{A_i \cdot a}{A_f \cdot b} = \frac{5}{2} \cdot 0.8 = 2.0$$

Step (ii) — Compute the output (ignoring offset for simplicity):

$$P_o = 2.0 \times 50 = 100 \text{ kPa}$$

Step (iii) — Note: 100 kPa ≈ 14.5 psi, which is within the standard 3–15 psi range. ✓

**Verification by units:** $[K_{span}]$ is dimensionless (area ratio × dimensionless lever ratio), so $P_o$ has units of pressure. ✔

## 3.2 Full Derivation: Differential Pressure in a Wet-Type (Mercury Manometer) System

Consider a U-tube manometer with mercury ($\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}534 \text{ kg/m}^3$) connected across an orifice plate in a water pipeline ($\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$).

### Step 1 — Hydrostatic Balance

At the lower mercury interface (left leg), the pressure equals the pressure at the same level in the right leg:

$$P_1 + \rho_w g h_1 = P_2 + \rho_w g h_2 + \rho_{Hg} g h_{Hg}$$

### Step 2 — Differential Pressure

Rearranging and noting that the height difference of mercury $h = h_{Hg}$ is what the manometer reads:

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot h$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation

For $h = 0.25$ m, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$\Delta P = (13534 - 1000) \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.25$$

$$\Delta P = 12534 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.25$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P \approx 30{,}746 \text{ Pa} \approx 30.75 \text{ kPa}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Force-Balance vs. Deflection Type

| Property | Deflection Type | Force-Balance Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sensing element motion | Large, measurable | Nearly zero (null) |
| Hysteresis | Significant | Negligible |
| Linearity | Depends on element | Inherently linear |
| Accuracy | 0.5–1% FS | 0.1–0.25% FS |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Loop gain | Open | High closed-loop gain |
| Calibration drift | Moderate | Very low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Pneumatic Force-Balance Transmitter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PIP[Process Pressure Pi] --> SENSE[Input Bellows Area Ai]
    SENSE --> BEAM[Pivoted Beam with Flapper]
    ZEROSPR[Zero Range Spring] --> BEAM
    BEAM --> NOZZLE[Nozzle Flapper Detector]
    NOZZLE --> RELAY[Pneumatic Relay Amplifier]
    RELAY --> OUTP[Output Pressure Po 3 to 15 psi]
    OUTP --> FEEDBK[Feedback Bellows Area Af]
    FEEDBK --> BEAM
    OUTP --> CTRL[Controller or Recorder]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The signal flows from the process → input bellows → beam/flapper → nozzle → relay (amplifier) → output. The output is fed back to a feedback bellows on the beam, closing the negative-feedback loop. This is the textbook Moore 77 / Foxboro 13A architecture.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Dry vs. Wet-Type Pressure Measurement Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Process Conditions] --> B{Is process fluid corrosive or hot?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Wet Type Filled System}
    B -- No --> D{Is pulsation severe?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Wet Type for Damping]
    D -- No --> F{Need fast response and low cost?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Dry Type Direct Contact]
    F -- No --> H{Continuous online transmission needed?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Force Balance Transmitter Dry or Filled]
    H -- No --> J[Local Dry Type Indicator Gauge]
    C --> K[Inverted Bell or Manometer]
    E --> K
    G --> L[Bourdon Tube or Capsule or Strain Gauge]
    J --> L
    I --> M[Pneumatic or Electronic Smart Transmitter]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Modern Smart (Electronic) Force-Balance Transmitter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PROC[Process Pipe] --> ISO[Isolating Diaphragm Silicone Oil Fill]
    ISO --> CAPS[Center Sensing Diaphragm]
    CAPS --> DPSENSOR[Differential Capacitive or Piezoresistive Sensor]
    DPSENSOR --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> CPU[Microprocessor with Linearization and Temperature Compensation]
    CPU --> DAC[Digital to Analog Converter]
    DAC --> HART[HART Modem 4 to 20 mA plus Digital]
    HART --> CTRL[Control Room DCS or PLC]
    CPU --> FBK[Digital Feedback to Sensor Coil Force Balance]
    FBK --> DPSENSOR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** Even in a modern *electronic* smart transmitter (e.g., Rosemount 3051), the **force-balance philosophy** persists — the sensor's minute capacitance/resistance change is *nulled* by an electromagnetic feedback coil. The current to that coil is the output. The principle has not changed in 70 years; only the implementation (electromagnetic vs. pneumatic) has evolved.

## 4.4 Functional Flow — Filled (Wet-Type) Thermal System in Pressure Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROC[Process Vessel] --> BULB[Bulb Filled with Liquid Gas or Vapor]
    BULB --> CAP[Capillary Tube with Filling Fluid]
    CAP --> BEL[Receiver Element Bellows or Bourdon]
    BEL --> IND[Indicator or Transmitter Output]
    BEL -. damped feedback .-> CAP
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 — Define Force Balance Principle.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper Q2(a)]**

> **Model Answer:**
> The force balance principle is a closed-loop measurement technique in which the input measurand force is continuously counter-balanced by an automatically generated, equal and opposite feedback force, so that the sensing element remains at the null-deflection position. The output is derived from the magnitude of the feedback force required to maintain equilibrium. It is widely used in pneumatic, hydraulic, and electronic transmitters for its inherent linearity, low hysteresis, and high accuracy. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for null condition, 1 for advantage.)*

### Question 2 — Differentiate between Dry-Type and Wet-Type pressure measurement with one example each.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Q1(b)]**

> **Model Answer:**
> In a **dry-type** pressure instrument, the pressure-sensing element is in **direct contact** with the process fluid. Example: a standard C-type Bourdon tube gauge used on an air line.
> In a **wet-type** (filled) instrument, an intermediate manometric fluid (mercury, oil, or silicone) is used to transmit the pressure to the sensing element, providing isolation and damping. Example: a mercury U-tube manometer across an orifice plate in a water line.
> Dry-type gives faster response and lower cost; wet-type gives better corrosion resistance and pulsation damping. *(3 marks: 1 for dry def+example, 1 for wet def+example, 1 for trade-off.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each)

### Part B – Question A (Internal Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Q11(a)(b)]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a pneumatic force-balance pressure transmitter. Derive the governing equation relating output pneumatic pressure to input differential pressure.**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A pneumatic force-balance pressure transmitter consists of:
1. An **input pressure bellows** of effective area $A_i$ that senses the differential pressure $P_i$.
2. A **pivoted beam** with a flapper at one end and a feedback bellows at the other.
3. A **nozzle–flapper** assembly that converts beam motion into a pneumatic signal.
4. A **pneumatic relay** (typically a 1:1 or higher gain relay) that amplifies the nozzle back-pressure into a robust 3–15 psi output.
5. A **feedback bellows** of area $A_f$ that senses the output pressure and applies a counter-force to the beam.
6. A **range spring** for zero adjustment.

**Working (2 marks):**
The input pressure pushes the input bellows, which deflects the beam, moving the flapper closer to the nozzle. Nozzle back-pressure rises, the relay amplifies this, and the output pneumatic pressure increases. The rising output pressure simultaneously acts on the feedback bellows, which pushes the beam back, restoring the flapper to its original (null) position. The output pressure at which equilibrium occurs is the transmitter's output.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
Applying moment equilibrium about the pivot:

$$P_i \cdot A_i \cdot a = P_o \cdot A_f \cdot b$$

$$\therefore \quad P_o = \left(\frac{A_i \, a}{A_f \, b}\right) P_i$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A pneumatic force-balance transmitter has $A_i = 8 \text{ cm}^2$, $A_f = 3 \text{ cm}^2$, and lever arm ratio $a/b = 0.75$. The input range is 0–120 kPa and the output range is 3–15 psi. Find: (i) the output pressure when input is 80 kPa, (ii) the input pressure when the output reads 9 psi.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the span constant:**

$$K_{span} = \frac{A_i \cdot a}{A_f \cdot b} = \frac{8}{3} \cdot 0.75 = 2.0$$

**Step 2 — Establish the input–output mapping in standard units.**
The output spans 12 psi (15 − 3) over 120 kPa input, giving a slope of:

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{12 \text{ psi}}{120 \text{ kPa}} = 0.1 \text{ psi/kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Part (i):** Output when $P_i = 80$ kPa:

$$P_o = 3 \text{ psi} + (0.1 \text{ psi/kPa}) \times 80 \text{ kPa} = 3 + 8 = \boxed{11 \text{ psi}}$$

**Step 4 — Part (ii):** Input when $P_o = 9$ psi:

$$9 = 3 + 0.1 \cdot P_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.1 P_i = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{P_i = 60 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Verification:** Both values fall within the 0–120 kPa / 3–15 psi envelope. ✔

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 3 psi zero offset** is the #1 reason students lose 2 marks on numerical force-balance questions. Always anchor: *0% input → 3 psi output.*
> 2. **Unit mixing** between kPa and psi. Use one system consistently. 1 psi ≈ 6.895 kPa.
> 3. **Not stating the null assumption** explicitly in the derivation. Examiners give at least 1 mark for the statement "Since the beam operates at null, $\sum \tau = 0$."

---

### Part B – Question B (Internal Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q12(a)(b)]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the construction and working of a filled (wet-type) system used for pressure measurement. Discuss the role of the filling fluid and list four commonly used filling fluids with their typical applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A filled (wet-type) pressure measurement system consists of:
1. A **process connection** with a **siphon tube** (for steam) or **chemical seal** (for corrosive media).
2. An **isolating diaphragm** that separates the process fluid from the filling fluid.
3. A **capillary tube** filled with the manometric fluid, transmitting pressure from the process tap to the gauge.
4. A **receiver element** (Bourdon tube, bellows, or capsule) inside the gauge that deflects under the transmitted pressure.
5. A **linkage and pointer** (or transmitter sensor) to convert the receiver deflection into a reading.

**Working (2 marks):**
The process pressure acts on the isolating diaphragm, which transfers the pressure to the incompressible filling fluid in the capillary. The filling fluid transmits this pressure to the receiver element (Bourdon/bellows) inside the gauge, which deflects proportionally. The filling fluid also provides pulsation damping and protects the receiver from corrosive, hot, or viscous process media.

**Filling Fluids (2 marks):**

| Fluid | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Mercury** | High-pressure, high-temperature, reference manometers |
| **Silicone oil** | Food, pharma, general chemical processes |
| **Glycerin / Glycol mixture** | Low-temperature, food-grade |
| **Inert gas (Nitrogen)** | Clean, high-purity, low-temperature differential systems |
| **Halocarbon oil** | Oxygen service (non-reactive, non-flammable) |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A wet-type differential manometer uses mercury and is connected across an orifice in a horizontal water pipe. The manometer reads $h = 0.18$ m. If the flow is reversed, what is the new manometer reading? Take $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Original $\Delta P$:**

$$\Delta P_1 = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot h$$

$$\Delta P_1 = (13600 - 1000) \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.18$$

$$\Delta P_1 = 12600 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.18 = 22{,}247 \text{ Pa} \approx 22.25 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Concept of Reversed Flow:**
When flow direction is reversed, the **magnitude** of the differential pressure remains the same (since the orifice creates the same $V^2$ loss). The **sign** of $\Delta P$ flips, but since the manometer reads the **absolute** height difference (a scalar), the reading $h$ remains the same:

$$\boxed{h_{reversed} = 0.18 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3 — Why it doesn't change:**
The orifice plate's pressure drop depends on the *square of the velocity*, which is independent of flow direction. The manometer is a passive device that only measures the magnitude of differential pressure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Some students incorrectly compute $\Delta P$ using $\rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$ alone, **ignoring the displaced water column**. This overestimates $\Delta P$ by 7% and costs 2 marks.
> 2. Others forget that the manometer reading is direction-agnostic (only depends on $|\Delta P|$).
> 3. Not writing units (Pa or kPa) in the final answer — ½ mark deducted.
> 4. Confusing *flow reversal* (magnitude same) with *flow stoppage* (manometer reads zero).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2: Force Balance & Dry-Type**

- **Force Balance Principle** = input force continuously nulled by equal & opposite feedback force; sensing element never deflects measurably.
- **Master Equation:** $P_i \cdot A_i \cdot a = P_o \cdot A_f \cdot b \Rightarrow P_o = \left(\dfrac{A_i \, a}{A_f \, b}\right) P_i$
- **Span** is set by the **lever arm ratio $a/b$**; **Zero** is set by the **range spring**.
- **Pneumatic standard output** is **3–15 psi** (0.2–1.0 bar); **Electronic standard** is **4–20 mA**.
- **Nozzle–Flapper + Relay** = the high-gain amplifier that enforces the null condition; relay gain typically 100–200.
- **Dry-Type** = sensing element **directly contacts** process fluid. *Fast, low-cost, but vulnerable to corrosion and pulsation.*
- **Wet-Type (Filled)** = an **intermediate manometric fluid** isolates the receiver. *Slow, expensive, but corrosion-resistant and self-damping.*
- **Dry-Type examples:** Bourdon tube, capsule, strain gauge, piezoelectric sensor.
- **Wet-Type examples:** Mercury U-tube manometer, inverted-bell, filled Bourdon, silicone-filled chemical seal.
- **Hydrostatic equation for filled systems:** $P = \rho g h$ (or $\Delta P = (\rho_f - \rho_p) g h$ for differential).
- **Bourdon tip deflection** scales as $\delta \propto P \cdot r^2 / t^3$ — *thinner walls* and *larger minor axis* give more deflection.
- **Modern smart transmitters** (Rosemount 3051, Yokogawa EJX) still use force-balance philosophy — only the feedback actuator changed from pneumatic to **electromagnetic**.
- **Why force balance won:** Inherent linearity, no hysteresis, no friction error, accuracy of 0.1% FS, and ease of zero/span adjustment.
- **Why dry vs. wet matters:** Process safety, media compatibility, and maintenance economics — *all exam scenarios test this.*
- **Common exam trap:** "Dry" does **not** mean "no fluid involved" — it means the *sensing element* is not isolated by a *manometric fill*. Nitrogen-filled capillaries in temperature systems are still "filled" systems.
- **Numerical constants to memorize:** $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$, $1 \text{ psi} = 6.895 \text{ kPa}$.
- **Past paper frequency:** Force-balance derivation appears in nearly **every KTU ESE** for this course — practice the moment-equation derivation at least three times.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: motion balance-Electronic pressure sensors: strain gauge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT865 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** motion balance-Electronic pressure sensors: strain gauge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Motion Balance (in the context of Pressure Measurement)

### Formal Definition
**Motion Balance** is a closed-loop negative feedback principle used in industrial transmitters where the small displacement (motion) of the primary sensing element (e.g., a diaphragm) is detected by a position/motion sensor, electronically amplified, and fed back as a current or voltage to a force-producing actuator (magnet/coil or pneumatic relay) that generates a balancing force to restore the sensing element to its original null position. The output signal is proportional to the input measurand (pressure).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, motion balance is classified as a **Closed-Loop / Servo-Type Transmitter** architecture, where the feedback signal is derived from *motion* rather than *force*. This distinguishes it from the classical "force balance" pneumatic transmitters (Foxboro, etc.).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a bathroom weighing scale (analog type). When you stand on it:
1. The top plate (diaphragm) **moves down** slightly.
2. A **pointer (motion sensor)** attached to it moves with the plate.
3. The pointer is connected through gears to a **spring-loaded restoring mechanism** that pulls the plate back up.
4. The amount of spring stretch needed to restore the plate equals your weight.

In a **Motion Balance Transmitter**, the mechanical gears-and-spring are replaced by an **LVDT or capacitive position sensor** (to detect motion) and an **electromagnetic coil** (to apply restoring force). The current needed to restore the plate is the output, which is now a standard 4–20 mA signal.

---

## 1.2 Electronic Pressure Sensors – Strain Gauge

### Formal Definition
A **Strain Gauge** is a passive resistive transducer whose electrical resistance changes in a known, reproducible manner when subjected to mechanical strain (ε = ΔL/L). When bonded to an elastic pressure-sensing element (diaphragm, capsule, or Bourdon tube), it converts applied pressure into a measurable change in resistance, which is then conditioned using a **Wheatstone bridge** to produce an output voltage proportional to pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric for strain gauges is the Gauge Factor (GF)**. For metallic foil gauges, **GF ≈ 2.0**; for semiconductor (piezoresistive) gauges, **GF ≈ 100 to 200**. The unit of strain is dimensionless (often expressed as µ-strain, i.e., 10⁻⁶).

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **stretchy rubber band with a thin wire wrapped around it**. If you stretch the rubber band:
- The wire becomes **longer** (resistance ↑)
- The wire becomes **thinner** (resistance ↑)
- The total resistance **increases** measurably.

A strain gauge works on the *same* principle, but the rubber band is replaced by a **flexible metal foil or semiconductor strip** firmly glued (bonded) to the surface whose strain you want to measure. The change in resistance is tiny (often parts per million), so a **Wheatstone bridge** is used to detect it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The strain gauge is the most widely used **electronic pressure sensor** in industrial process control because of its compact size, good accuracy (typically 0.1 % to 0.5 % of full scale), high frequency response, and compatibility with standard 4–20 mA two-wire transmitter electronics.

---

## 1.3 Why Combine Motion Balance with Electronic Pressure Sensors?

In modern smart transmitters (e.g., Honeywell ST 3000, Rosemount 3051, Yokogawa EJA), **strain gauges** are mounted on a **single-crystal silicon resonator/diaphragm** that operates in a **motion balance** feedback loop. The strain gauge detects the deflection (motion) due to applied pressure; the electronics servo the deflection back to null by driving a current through a coil. The servo current is the 4–20 mA output. This combination yields:
- High accuracy (0.075 % of span)
- Excellent long-term stability
- Strong immunity to overpressure and vibration
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Motion Balance Transmitter – Working Logic

The motion balance pressure transmitter operates in a precise, step-by-step closed-loop sequence:

1. **Primary Element (Diaphragm):** Input pressure *P* acts on a metallic or silicon diaphragm and produces a small deflection *x* (typically 0.001 to 0.01 mm).
2. **Motion/Position Sensor:** A capacitive plate, LVDT, or strain gauge detects the deflection *x* and produces an electrical signal *e₁* proportional to *x*.
3. **Amplifier:** The signal *e₁* is amplified to *e₂ = A·e₁*, where *A* is the amplifier gain (typically 10⁴ to 10⁶).
4. **Feedback Actuator:** The amplified signal drives a current *I* through a force coil placed in a permanent magnetic field. The Lorentz force *F_b = k·I* acts on the diaphragm to oppose the original deflection.
5. **Null Balance Condition:** At equilibrium, the restoring force *F_b* equals the input pressure force *F_in*; the diaphragm returns to its null (zero) position.
6. **Output:** The output current *I* (or voltage) is directly proportional to the input pressure *P* and is standardized to **4–20 mA** (or 1–5 V).

> [!NOTE]
> Because the diaphragm never actually moves significantly from its null position, **hysteresis, creep, and non-linearity are virtually eliminated**. This is the chief advantage of closed-loop motion balance over open-loop sensors.

---

## 2.2 Strain Gauge – Operating Principle

A strain gauge consists of a **thin resistive foil or wire grid** arranged in a back-and-forth (serpentine) pattern to maximize the length of the resistive element in the direction of strain. The gauge is bonded to the substrate using a special adhesive (cyanoacrylate or epoxy).

When the substrate is stressed:
- The foil experiences the **same strain ε** as the surface it is bonded to.
- The resistance of the foil changes according to the gauge factor.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity / Equation** | **Symbol & Form** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resistance of a conductor | $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ | Ω (ohm) | ρ = resistivity; L = length; A = cross-section |
| 2 | Strain (longitudinal) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ | dimensionless (m/m) | Often expressed in µε |
| 3 | Gauge Factor (definition) | $G_F = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L} = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | dimensionless | Key parameter of strain gauge |
| 4 | Gauge Factor (analytical form) | $G_F = (1 + 2\nu) + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$ | dimensionless | ν = Poisson's ratio; 2nd term is piezoresistive effect |
| 5 | Approx. GF for metals | $G_F \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 2.0$ | dimensionless | For constantan, nichrome, Karma alloy |
| 6 | GF for semiconductors | $G_F \approx 100 \text{ to } 200$ | dimensionless | Silicon, germanium (piezoresistive dominant) |
| 7 | Bridge output (single active arm) | $V_o \approx V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{4R}$ | V (volt) | For $\Delta R \ll R$, bridge initially balanced |
| 8 | Bridge output (full-bridge, 2 active tension, 2 compression) | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | V | Maximum sensitivity, 4× single arm |
| 9 | Bridge output (half-bridge, 2 active) | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{2R}$ | V | Two gauges, one in tension, one in compression |
| 10 | Pressure-induced strain (thin diaphragm) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{3 P a^{2}}{8 h^{2} E}(1 - \nu^{2})$ | dimensionless | a = diaphragm radius; h = thickness; E = Young's modulus |
| 11 | Diaphragm centre deflection | $x = \dfrac{3 P a^{4}}{16 E h^{3}}(1 - \nu^{2})$ | m | For clamped circular plate |
| 12 | Motion balance output current | $I_{out} = \dfrac{P \cdot A_{d}}{k_{coil}}$ | A (ampere) | $A_d$ = diaphragm area; $k_{coil}$ = coil force constant |
| 13 | 4–20 mA mapping | $I = 4 \text{ mA} + \dfrac{P - P_{min}}{P_{max} - P_{min}} \times 16 \text{ mA}$ | mA | Standard industrial current loop |
| 14 | Sensitivity (S) of pressure sensor | $S = \dfrac{\partial V_o}{\partial P}$ | V/Pa | Determines smallest detectable pressure change |
| 15 | Temperature-induced apparent strain | $\varepsilon_{apparent} = \alpha_s - \alpha_g$ | µε/°C | α_s, α_g = thermal expansion coefficients |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU valuation, students frequently confuse $\Delta R / R$ (which is dimensionless) with $\Delta R$ (which has units of Ω). Always carry units clearly in your derivations.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Application of Motion-Balance + Strain Gauge Pressure Sensor** |
|---|---|
| Oil & Gas | Wellhead pressure monitoring, downhole gauges, pipeline pressure |
| Power Plants | Boiler drum pressure, feedwater pump discharge pressure |
| Process Industries | Reactor vessel pressure, distillation column overhead |
| Aerospace | Altitude sensing (Pitot-static), hydraulic system pressure |
| Automotive | Manifold absolute pressure (MAP), brake-line pressure |
| Hydraulic Systems | Load sensing, proportional valve control |
| Medical | Blood pressure transducers, ventilators, dialysis machines |

The combination of **motion balance electronics + silicon strain gauge** is the *de-facto* industry standard in 21st-century smart transmitters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Gauge Factor

### Starting Point
The fundamental resistance of a uniform conductor is:

$$R = \frac{\rho \, L}{A}$$

where
- $\rho$ = resistivity of the material [Ω·m]
- $L$ = length of the conductor [m]
- $A$ = cross-sectional area [m²]

### Step 1 – Take Natural Logarithm
Taking the natural logarithm of both sides converts multiplication into addition, which simplifies differentiation:

$$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$

### Step 2 – Differentiate
Differentiating both sides (assuming small, incremental changes):

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

### Step 3 – Apply Poisson's Ratio Relation
For a uniform cylindrical conductor (or foil) under longitudinal strain, the cross-section contracts laterally. Poisson's ratio $\nu$ relates lateral strain to longitudinal strain:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot \frac{dr}{r} = -2 \nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

The negative sign indicates that as length increases, area decreases. Substituting:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \left(-2 \nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L}\right)$$

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} + 2 \nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu) \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

### Step 4 – Define Longitudinal Strain
By definition, longitudinal strain is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{dL}{L}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu) \cdot \varepsilon$$

### Step 5 – Define Gauge Factor
The gauge factor is the ratio of fractional resistance change to applied strain:

$$G_F \;\equiv\; \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

Comparing the equation from Step 4:

$$G_F = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

### Step 6 – Simplify for Metallic vs. Semiconductor Gauges
- **Metallic gauges** (constantan, Karma alloy, nichrome): the piezoresistive term $\Delta \rho / \rho$ is negligible. With $\nu \approx 0.3$ for most metals:
$$G_F \approx 1 + 2(0.3) = 1.6 \approx 2.0$$

- **Semiconductor gauges** (silicon, germanium): the piezoresistive term dominates, giving:
$$G_F \approx 100 \text{ to } 200$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **KTU 2024 ESE frequently-tested derivation** and is expected to be reproduced from first principles during examination. Memorize the intermediate steps; marks are awarded for the *transitions*, not just the final result.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Wheatstone Bridge Output (Quarter-Bridge with One Active Gauge)

### Circuit Description
A standard Wheatstone bridge has four arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$, an excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ across the bridge, and an output voltage $V_o$ measured across the mid-points.

By voltage division:

$$V_o = V_{ex}\left[\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}\right]$$

### Balanced Condition
When the bridge is balanced, $V_o = 0$, which requires:

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_4}{R_3} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad R_1 R_3 = R_2 R_4$$

This is the classical **balance equation of the Wheatstone bridge**.

### Quarter-Bridge with One Active Gauge
Let $R_1 = R + \Delta R$ (the active strain gauge), and $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$ (all fixed). Substituting:

$$V_o = V_{ex}\left[\frac{R + \Delta R}{R + \Delta R + R} - \frac{R}{R + R}\right]$$

$$V_o = V_{ex}\left[\frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2}\right]$$

Taking the common denominator $2(2R + \Delta R)$:

$$V_o = V_{ex}\left[\frac{2(R + \Delta R) - (2R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)}\right]$$

$$V_o = V_{ex}\left[\frac{2R + 2\Delta R - 2R - \Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}\right]$$

$$V_o = V_{ex}\left[\frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}\right]$$

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R + 2\Delta R}$$

### Approximation for Small $\Delta R$
For strain gauges, $\Delta R \ll R$ (typically $\Delta R / R < 10^{-3}$). Neglecting the $2\Delta R$ term in the denominator:

$$\boxed{\,V_o \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}\,}$$

This is the **standard quarter-bridge output equation** for a single active gauge.

> [!NOTE]
> The approximation is justified because for $G_F = 2$ and $\varepsilon = 1000 \; \mu\varepsilon$, we have $\Delta R / R = 0.002$, and the neglected $2\Delta R$ contributes only $0.1\%$ error — well within engineering tolerance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Bridge Output for Full-Bridge Configuration

For a **full bridge** with two gauges in tension (resistance increases by $+\Delta R$) and two gauges in compression (resistance decreases by $-\Delta R$), the bridge arrangement is:

- $R_1 = R + \Delta R$ (tension)
- $R_2 = R - \Delta R$ (compression)
- $R_3 = R + \Delta R$ (tension)
- $R_4 = R - \Delta R$ (compression)

Substituting into the general bridge equation and simplifying (using the same voltage-division approach):

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

This gives **four times the sensitivity** of the quarter-bridge configuration, plus **automatic temperature compensation** because gauges experiencing the same temperature in opposite arms produce zero net output.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Motion-Balance Output Current

Consider a circular diaphragm of effective area $A_d$ subjected to differential pressure $\Delta P$.

### Force Balance at Null Position
The input force on the diaphragm is:

$$F_{in} = \Delta P \cdot A_d$$

The electromagnetic restoring force generated by the feedback coil in a permanent magnetic field of flux density $B$ is:

$$F_{b} = B \cdot I_{out} \cdot L_{coil}$$

where $L_{coil}$ is the total length of the conductor in the coil. At null (steady state), the diaphragm is restored to its original position, so:

$$F_{in} = F_{b}$$

$$\Delta P \cdot A_d = B \cdot I_{out} \cdot L_{coil}$$

Solving for output current:

$$I_{out} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot A_d}{B \cdot L_{coil}}$$

Defining the coil force constant $k_c = B \cdot L_{coil}$:

$$\boxed{\,I_{out} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot A_d}{k_c}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the output current is **directly proportional to input pressure** and **independent of diaphragm stiffness, Young's modulus, or diaphragm thickness** — because the diaphragm never actually deflects! This is the central elegance of motion-balance design.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Configuration** | **Number of Active Gauges** | **Sensitivity $V_o / V_{ex}$** | **Temperature Compensation** | **KTU Exam Weightage** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quarter-bridge (1 active) | 1 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{4R}$ | Requires dummy gauge | High |
| Half-bridge (2 active, adjacent arms) | 2 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{2R}$ | Partial | Medium |
| Half-bridge (2 active, opposite arms) | 2 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{2R}$ (with sign) | Good | Medium |
| Full-bridge (4 active) | 4 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Excellent | Very High |
| Poisson configuration (1 active, 1 Poisson dummy) | 1 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{2R}(1+\nu)$ | Good | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Motion-Balance Pressure Transmitter

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the closed-loop signal flow inside a modern motion-balance pressure transmitter. Each block represents a physical/functional stage in the system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Pressure P] --> B[Diaphragm Capsule Primary Element]
    B --> C[Small Deflection x of Sensing Diaphragm]
    C --> D[Motion Detector LVDT or Capacitive Pickoff]
    D --> E[AC Carrier Amplifier and Demodulator]
    E --> F[High Gain DC Amplifier Gain A]
    F --> G[Output Stage Current Controller]
    G --> H[Output 4 to 20 mA Current Loop]
    G --> I[Feedback Coil in Permanent Magnetic Field]
    I --> J[Lorentz Restoring Force Fb]
    J --> B
    H --> K[Receiver Indicator Recorder or DCS]
```

### Functional Explanation of the Block Flow
1. **Input Pressure P** acts on the diaphragm and produces a small mechanical deflection.
2. The **Motion Detector** (LVDT/Capacitive pickoff) senses this deflection and converts it into an AC voltage proportional to displacement.
3. The **AC Amplifier/Demodulator** rectifies and filters the signal into a clean DC voltage.
4. The **High-Gain DC Amplifier** boosts the signal by a factor of $10^4$ to $10^6$ to drive the output stage.
5. The **Output Stage** converts the voltage into a regulated **4–20 mA** current loop.
6. A portion of this current is sent through the **Feedback Coil**, which sits in a strong magnetic field. The coil generates a Lorentz force that pulls the diaphragm back to its null position.
7. The **Receiver** (DCS / PLC / Indicator) terminates the loop and converts 4–20 mA back into engineering units (e.g., kPa, bar, psi).

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Bonded Strain-Gauge Pressure Sensor

The figure below illustrates the complete signal chain for a strain-gauge-based electronic pressure sensor, from the physical input to the conditioned output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Pressure P] --> B[Force Summation on Diaphragm]
    B --> C[Diaphragm Deflection and Surface Strain epsilon]
    C --> D[Four Bonded Strain Gauges in Full Bridge]
    D --> E[Excitation Voltage Vex Regulated DC Supply]
    D --> F[Wheatstone Bridge Output Vo]
    F --> G[Instrumentation Amplifier INA High CMRR]
    G --> H[Offset and Gain Calibration Trimming Network]
    H --> I[Low Pass Filter for Noise Suppression]
    I --> J[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    J --> K[Microcontroller Linearization and Temperature Compensation]
    K --> L[Output 4 to 20 mA or Digital HART]
```

### Functional Explanation
- The **diaphragm** deflects by a few micrometres under pressure.
- **Four strain gauges** (two axial, two transverse) bonded on the diaphragm surface change resistance with strain.
