- The gauges form a **Wheatstone bridge** energized by a precision **Vex** (typically 5 V or 10 V).
- The bridge produces a differential output **Vo** in the millivolt range.
- An **Instrumentation Amplifier** (e.g., INA128, AD620) amplifies the small differential signal and rejects common-mode noise.
- Calibration networks correct zero and span errors.
- The signal is **filtered**, **digitized** by the **ADC**, and processed by the **microcontroller** for linearization and temperature compensation.
- The final output is a **4–20 mA** analog signal, often with **HART** digital protocol overlay.

---

## 4.3 Schematic of a Wheatstone Bridge with Active Strain Gauge

The figure below is a **detailed functional architecture** of the Wheatstone bridge arrangement commonly used with strain-gauge pressure sensors.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Excitation Source Vex] --> B[Node A Top of Bridge]
    A --> C[Node C Bottom of Bridge]
    B --> D[Arm R1 Active Gauge Tension]
    B --> E[Arm R2 Active Gauge Compression]
    C --> F[Arm R3 Active Gauge Compression]
    C --> G[Arm R4 Active Gauge Tension]
    D --> H[Node B Positive Output]
    E --> H
    F --> I[Node D Negative Output]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Differential Amplifier Input Positive]
    I --> K[Differential Amplifier Input Negative]
    J --> L[Output Voltage Vo = Vex times delta R over R]
    K --> L
```

### Functional Notes
- **R1 and R4** are positioned on the diaphragm regions experiencing **tensile strain** (resistance increases).
- **R2 and R3** are positioned on regions experiencing **compressive strain** (resistance decreases).
- This full-bridge configuration gives **maximum sensitivity**, **four times the output** of a quarter-bridge, and **inherent temperature compensation** because all four gauges experience the same temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> The above block-level diagrams are the **Mermaid-renderable alternative** to physical schematic drawings. For KTU examinations, you are expected to draw a **hand-drawn circuit diagram** of the Wheatstone bridge with clearly labelled arms, the excitation source, and the differential output.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Gauge Factor of a strain gauge. Why is the Gauge Factor higher for semiconductor strain gauges than for metallic gauges?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)
**Definition (1 mark):** The Gauge Factor ($G_F$) of a strain gauge is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in electrical resistance ($\Delta R / R$) to the applied mechanical strain ($\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$):

$$G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

It is a **dimensionless quantity** that quantifies the sensitivity of the resistive element to mechanical deformation.

**Reason for higher GF in semiconductors (2 marks):**
The gauge factor has two components:
$$G_F = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

- For **metallic gauges** (e.g., constantan), the second term (piezoresistive effect) is negligible, so $G_F \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 1.6 \text{ to } 2.0$.
- For **semiconductor gauges** (e.g., doped silicon), the second term dominates due to the strong dependence of carrier mobility on crystal lattice deformation. The piezoresistive coefficient is very large, giving $G_F \approx 100 \text{ to } 200$.

Hence semiconductor gauges exhibit a much higher gauge factor.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by Motion Balance in pressure transmitters? How does it differ from Force Balance?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)
**Motion Balance (2 marks):** Motion balance is a closed-loop negative feedback principle in which the **small displacement (motion)** of a primary sensing element (such as a diaphragm) is detected by a motion/position sensor (LVDT, capacitive pickoff). The detected motion signal is amplified and fed to a force-producing actuator (electromagnetic coil or pneumatic relay) that generates a **restoring force** to bring the sensing element back to its null position. The output current (4–20 mA) is proportional to the input pressure.

**Difference from Force Balance (1 mark):**

| **Aspect** | **Motion Balance** | **Force Balance** |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback signal | Derived from displacement/motion | Derived from force |
| Sensing element | Deflects by a few micrometres | Deflects similarly but force is the controlled variable |
| Position sensor | LVDT or capacitive pickoff | Force beam / moment balance |
| Typical example | Electronic smart transmitters (Rosemount 3051) | Pneumatic transmitters (Foxboro) |

Both are closed-loop; the key difference lies in **which physical variable** (motion or force) is sensed for the feedback signal.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE 14-Mark Format with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working principle of a **Motion Balance Pressure Transmitter**. Derive the expression for the output current in terms of the input pressure. (7 marks)

**b)** Explain the **working of a Wheatstone bridge** with a strain gauge. Derive the bridge output voltage for a **quarter-bridge configuration** with one active gauge. State the assumptions made. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks

**Working Principle (5 marks):**
A motion balance pressure transmitter works on the closed-loop negative feedback principle. The block diagram consists of:

1. **Diaphragm (Primary Element):** The input pressure $P$ is applied to a circular diaphragm, producing a small deflection $x$ (typically a few micrometres).
2. **Motion Detector (LVDT or Capacitive Pickoff):** This detects the displacement $x$ and produces an electrical signal $e_1 \propto x$.
3. **Amplifier:** The signal is amplified to $e_2 = A \cdot e_1$, with gain $A$ of the order of $10^4$ to $10^6$.
4. **Feedback Coil (Force Actuator):** The amplifier output drives a current $I$ through a coil placed in a permanent magnetic field. The Lorentz force $F_b = k_c \cdot I$ acts on the diaphragm to oppose its deflection.
5. **Output Stage:** The same current $I$ is the 4–20 mA output.

At **null equilibrium**, the input force $F_{in} = P \cdot A_d$ is exactly balanced by the electromagnetic restoring force $F_b$:

$$P \cdot A_d = k_c \cdot I$$

**Derivation of Output Current (2 marks):**
Solving for $I$:

$$I = \frac{P \cdot A_d}{k_c}$$

where $A_d$ = effective diaphragm area, $k_c$ = coil force constant (N/A).

For 4–20 mA standardization, the output is mapped as:
$$I = 4 \text{ mA} + \frac{P - P_{min}}{P_{max} - P_{min}} \times 16 \text{ mA}$$

**Key advantage:** The output depends only on $A_d$ and $k_c$, not on diaphragm stiffness or material properties, ensuring excellent linearity.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Wheatstone Bridge Working (3 marks):**
A Wheatstone bridge consists of four resistive arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ arranged in a diamond pattern. An excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ is applied across the top and bottom nodes (A and C). The output $V_o$ is measured across the side nodes (B and D). The bridge is **balanced** when $R_1 R_3 = R_2 R_4$, giving $V_o = 0$.

When a strain gauge is used, one or more arms are **active** (changing resistance with strain). The bridge converts small resistance changes into measurable voltage.

**Derivation of Quarter-Bridge Output (4 marks):**
For a quarter-bridge with $R_1 = R + \Delta R$ active, and $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$ fixed, applying voltage division:

$$V_o = V_{ex}\left[\frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2}\right]$$

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R + 2\Delta R}$$

For $\Delta R \ll R$ (small strain assumption):

$$\boxed{V_o \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}}$$

**Assumptions (0 marks but important):**
- The strain is small ($\varepsilon \ll 1$), so higher-order terms can be neglected.
- The bridge is initially balanced ($V_o = 0$ at $\varepsilon = 0$).
- Excitation source is stable and temperature-independent.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**a)** Derive the expression for the **Gauge Factor** of a strain gauge from basic principles. Hence, calculate the change in resistance of a strain gauge with $R = 120 \, \Omega$ and $G_F = 2.1$ when subjected to a strain of $500 \, \mu\varepsilon$. (7 marks)

**b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **construction and working of a strain-gauge-based electronic pressure sensor**. List any four advantages of strain-gauge pressure sensors. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks

**Derivation (5 marks):**
The resistance of a uniform conductor is $R = \rho L / A$. Taking natural log and differentiating:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} + 2\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

Substituting $\varepsilon = dL/L$:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu) \cdot \varepsilon$$

Defining gauge factor:

$$G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**
Given: $R = 120 \, \Omega$, $G_F = 2.1$, $\varepsilon = 500 \times 10^{-6}$.

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} = 1.05 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta R = R \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon = 120 \times 1.05 \times 10^{-3} = 0.126 \, \Omega$$

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Construction (3 marks):**
A strain-gauge pressure sensor consists of:
- A metallic or silicon **diaphragm** as the pressure-sensing element.
- **Four strain gauges** bonded to the diaphragm surface — two in the centre (tensile region) and two at the edges (compressive region).
- The four gauges are connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration.
- A regulated **excitation supply** ($V_{ex}$) energizes the bridge.
- An **instrumentation amplifier** and **signal conditioning** circuit produce a standard 4–20 mA or 0–10 V output.

**Working (2 marks):**
Applied pressure deflects the diaphragm. The gauges bonded to it experience strain, change resistance, and unbalance the Wheatstone bridge. The bridge output is proportional to pressure. The amplifier and signal conditioner produce a calibrated output.

**Four Advantages (2 marks):**
1. Compact size and lightweight.
2. High accuracy (0.1 % to 0.5 % of full scale).
3. Good frequency response (suitable for dynamic pressure measurement).
4. Compatibility with standard 4–20 mA two-wire transmitters.
5. (Bonus) Long-term stability and low hysteresis.
6. (Bonus) Can measure both static and dynamic pressures.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing the piezoresistive term in the Gauge Factor derivation.** Many students write $G_F = 1 + 2\nu$ only. The complete form is $G_F = (1 + 2\nu) + (\Delta \rho / \rho)/\varepsilon$. **Lose 2 marks** if omitted.
> 2. **Forgetting the assumption $\Delta R \ll R$** when deriving the quarter-bridge output. The exact form is $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / (4R + 2\Delta R)$. **Lose 1 mark** if the approximation is unjustified.
> 3. **Confusing motion balance with force balance.** Motion balance uses a *position sensor* (LVDT/capacitive); force balance uses a *force-beam* feedback. Examiners actively test this distinction.
> 4. **Units confusion:** Strain is dimensionless but conventionally written as µε. Don't write strain with units of "m/m" in the final answer; use µε.
> 5. **No derivation of the 4–20 mA mapping:** If a question asks for output in mA, derive the mapping equation. Do not assume it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Motion Balance – Electronic Pressure Sensors: Strain Gauge"**

- ☐ **Motion Balance** is a *closed-loop* feedback transmitter; the diaphragm deflection is detected by an LVDT/capacitive sensor and nullified by an electromagnetic force.
- ☐ **Force Balance** differs from Motion Balance only in the *feedback variable* — force beam vs. motion sensor.
- ☐ **Strain Gauge** is a resistive sensor whose resistance changes with mechanical strain. The fundamental equation is $R = \rho L / A$.
- ☐ **Gauge Factor** $G_F = (1 + 2\nu) + (\Delta \rho / \rho)/\varepsilon$. For metals $G_F \approx 2$, for semiconductors $G_F \approx 100$ to $200$.
- ☐ **Wheatstone Bridge balance condition:** $R_1 R_3 = R_2 R_4$.
- ☐ **Quarter-bridge output:** $V_o \approx V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / (4R)$.
- ☐ **Full-bridge output:** $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / R$ — four times the sensitivity of a quarter-bridge.
- ☐ **Motion balance output current:** $I_{out} = (P \cdot A_d) / k_c$, independent of diaphragm material properties.
- ☐ **Standard 4–20 mA mapping:** $I = 4 \text{ mA} + (P - P_{min})/(P_{max} - P_{min}) \times 16 \text{ mA}$.
- ☐ **Poisson's ratio** for most metals $\nu \approx 0.3$, so the mechanical term $(1 + 2\nu) \approx 1.6$.
- ☐ **Temperature compensation** is best achieved using a full-bridge configuration where all gauges share the same thermal environment.
- ☐ **Diaphragm centre strain** for clamped circular plate: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{3 P a^2}{8 h^2 E}(1 - \nu^2)$.
- ☐ **Common exam keywords:** "motion balance," "force balance," "gauge factor," "Wheatstone bridge," "quarter bridge," "full bridge," "piezoresistive effect," "Poisson's ratio," "null position," "4–20 mA current loop," "LVDT pickoff," "Lorentz force."
- ☐ **Industrial examples to memorize:** Rosemount 3051, Honeywell ST 3000, Yokogawa EJA — all use motion-balance + silicon strain gauge.
- ☐ **Always state assumptions** when deriving Wheatstone bridge output ($\Delta R \ll R$, initial balance, etc.).
- ☐ **Strain is dimensionless** but conventionally expressed in µε (microstrain) for clarity.
- ☐ **For semiconductor strain gauges**, the piezoresistive term dominates; for metallic gauges, the geometric term $(1 + 2\nu)$ dominates.

---

*End of Module 2 Notes — Motion Balance & Electronic Pressure Sensors: Strain Gauge*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacitance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT865 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Capacitance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Capacitance** is defined as the ability of a system to store an electric charge per unit voltage across it. Mathematically, it is the ratio of the magnitude of the electric charge stored on a conductor to the absolute potential difference across it.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Capacitance ($C$) is the property of a capacitor that determines the amount of electric charge ($Q$) it can hold for a given voltage ($V$) across its plates: $C = Q/V$. The SI unit is the **Farad (F)**.

In **Industrial Instrumentation**, capacitance-type transducers convert a physical measurand (such as displacement, pressure, level, or thickness) into a proportional change in capacitance. This change in capacitance is then measured by a suitable signal conditioning circuit and converted into an equivalent electrical signal.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two large water tanks connected by a thin rubber pipe. The **capacitor** is like this whole assembly.

- The **two conducting plates** are like the two tanks that can hold water (charge).
- The **insulating dielectric** (air, oil, plastic) between the plates is like the **rubber pipe** — it blocks direct water flow (DC current) but allows pressure differences (voltage) to build up.
- The **size of the tanks** (plate area) and **width of the pipe** (dielectric thickness) determine how easily charge accumulates — this "ease of storing charge" is the **capacitance**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy for Industrial Use:** A **capacitive level sensor** in a water tank is essentially a long metal probe (one plate) and the metal tank wall (another plate). As the water level rises, the **dielectric** between the plates changes (from air to water), changing the capacitance. The instrument then measures this capacitance change to display the water level.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Permittivity of free space (vacuum):** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{F/m}$
- **Relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of common materials:**
  - **Vacuum:** 1.0
  - **Air:** 1.0006 ≈ 1.0
  - **Water:** ~80
  - **Transformer oil:** ~2.2
  - **Glass:** 5–10
  - **Mica:** 5–7
  - **Teflon:** ~2.1

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The variation of any one of the three parameters — plate area ($A$), distance between plates ($d$), or dielectric constant ($\varepsilon_r$) — forms the basis of all capacitive transducers used in industrial instrumentation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamentals of a Parallel Plate Capacitor

A parallel plate capacitor consists of two conducting plates of area $A$ separated by a distance $d$, with a dielectric medium of permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$ filling the space between them.

### 2.1.1 Mathematical Foundation

The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor is given by:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

where:
- $C$ = Capacitance (Farads, F)
- $\varepsilon_0$ = Permittivity of free space $= 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{F/m}$
- $\varepsilon_r$ = Relative permittivity of the dielectric (dimensionless)
- $A$ = Effective overlapping plate area (m²)
- $d$ = Distance between the plates (m)

### 2.2 Three Operating Principles of Capacitive Transducers

Capacitive transducers can be classified based on which parameter of $C$ is varied by the measurand:

1. **Variable Distance Type** — The separation $d$ between plates changes.
2. **Variable Area Type** — The overlapping area $A$ between plates changes.
3. **Variable Dielectric Type** — The dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$ (or the material between plates) changes.

### 2.2.1 Variable Distance Capacitive Transducer

When one plate is movable and the other is fixed, displacement $x$ of the movable plate changes $d$ to $(d \pm x)$.

$$C_x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{(d \pm x)}$$

**Sensitivity:**

$$S_d = \frac{dC}{dx} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{(d \pm x)^2}$$

> **Use Case:** Pressure transducers (where diaphragm deflection changes $d$), micro-displacement measurement.

### 2.2.2 Variable Area Capacitive Transducer

When one plate slides relative to the other, the overlapping area $A$ changes linearly.

$$C_x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (A_0 \pm \Delta A)}{d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (a \pm \Delta a) \cdot b}{d}$$

**Sensitivity:**

$$S_A = \frac{dC}{dA} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d}$$

This provides a **linear relationship** between displacement and capacitance change — highly desirable in instrumentation.

> **Use Case:** Linear displacement measurement using a cylindrical/linear capacitive transducer.

### 2.2.3 Variable Dielectric Capacitive Transducer

When the dielectric material between the plates changes (e.g., air is replaced by liquid or solid), $\varepsilon_r$ changes.

For a two-dielectric configuration:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{\dfrac{d_1}{\varepsilon_{r1}} + \dfrac{d_2}{\varepsilon_{r2}}}$$

> **Use Case:** Level measurement of liquids (water, oil), humidity sensing, thickness measurement.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Application |
|---|-----------|---------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Capacitance (general) | $C = Q / V$ | Farad (F) | Definition |
| 2 | Parallel plate capacitor | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | F | Basic |
| 3 | Variable distance | $C_x = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / (d \pm x)$ | F | Pressure/displacement |
| 4 | Variable area | $C_x = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (A_0 \pm \Delta A) / d$ | F | Linear displacement |
| 5 | Variable dielectric (series) | $C = \varepsilon_0 A / \left( \dfrac{d_1}{\varepsilon_{r1}} + \dfrac{d_2}{\varepsilon_{r2}} \right)$ | F | Level/thickness |
| 6 | Sensitivity (distance) | $S = -\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d^2$ | F/m | Pressure |
| 7 | Sensitivity (area) | $S = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r / d$ | F/m² | Displacement |
| 8 | Energy stored | $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Joule (J) | General |
| 9 | Reactance | $X_C = 1 / (2\pi f C)$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | AC bridges |
| 10 | Permittivity relation | $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ | F/m | Material property |

> **Critical Note for KTU Board Exam:** Always use the **absolute value of sensitivity** unless specifically asked for sign. The negative sign in variable-distance transducers indicates an **inverse relationship** — as distance increases, capacitance decreases.

## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Applications

| Field | Measurand | Capacitive Principle Used |
|-------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **Oil & Gas** | Liquid level in tanks | Variable dielectric |
| **Hydraulics** | Pressure | Variable distance (diaphragm) |
| **Manufacturing** | Thickness of sheets/films | Variable dielectric |
| **Metrology** | Microscopic displacement | Variable area |
| **HVAC** | Humidity of air | Variable dielectric |
| **Automotive** | Fuel level | Variable dielectric |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacitance of a Parallel Plate Capacitor

We begin with **Gauss's Law** for a parallel plate capacitor with plate area $A$, plate separation $d$, and surface charge density $\sigma$.

**Step 1: Apply Gauss's Law**

The electric flux through a Gaussian surface enclosing the positive plate equals the enclosed charge:

$$\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the surface integral**

Since the field between the plates is uniform and perpendicular to the plates:

$$E \cdot A = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 3: Solve for E**

$$E = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}$$

**Step 4: Express voltage as line integral of field**

The voltage between plates is the integral of $E$ from one plate to the other:

$$V = \int_0^d E \, dx = E \cdot d$$

**Step 5: Substitute $E$**

$$V = \frac{Q \cdot d}{\varepsilon_0 A}$$

**Step 6: Rearrange for C = Q/V**

$$C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

**Step 7: Introduce dielectric medium**

If a dielectric of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ fills the space:

$$\boxed{C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}}$$

This is the foundational equation for all capacitive transducers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sensitivity of Variable-Distance Capacitive Transducer

Given the basic equation:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

**Step 1: Introduce displacement x**

When the movable plate displaces by $x$ from equilibrium distance $d$:

$$C_x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{(d + x)}$$

**Step 2: Expand using binomial approximation (for small x)**

Recall that $\dfrac{1}{1 + (x/d)} \approx 1 - \dfrac{x}{d} + \dfrac{x^2}{d^2} - \ldots$

$$C_x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(1 + \dfrac{x}{d}\right)}$$

**Step 3: Apply linear approximation (x << d)**

$$C_x \approx \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} \left(1 - \frac{x}{d}\right)$$

**Step 4: Define change in capacitance**

$$\Delta C = C_x - C = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} \cdot \frac{x}{d} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot x}{d^2}$$

**Step 5: Compute sensitivity**

$$\boxed{S = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}}$$

The negative sign indicates that capacitance **decreases** as separation **increases** — characteristic of inverse-square sensitivity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Capacitance of a Two-Dielectric Capacitor (Variable Dielectric)

Consider a parallel plate capacitor with plate area $A$ and separation $d = d_1 + d_2$, where two dielectric materials of relative permittivities $\varepsilon_{r1}$ and $\varepsilon_{r2}$ occupy thicknesses $d_1$ and $d_2$, respectively.

**Step 1: Apply series capacitor model**

The two dielectric layers behave as two capacitors in series:

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r1} A}{d_1}, \quad C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r2} A}{d_2}$$

**Step 2: Series combination formula**

$$\frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2}$$

**Step 3: Substitute**

$$\frac{1}{C} = \frac{d_1}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r1} A} + \frac{d_2}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r2} A}$$

**Step 4: Factor common terms**

$$\frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_0 A} \left( \frac{d_1}{\varepsilon_{r1}} + \frac{d_2}{\varepsilon_{r2}} \right)$$

**Step 5: Invert to obtain C**

$$\boxed{C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{\dfrac{d_1}{\varepsilon_{r1}} + \dfrac{d_2}{\varepsilon_{r2}}}}$$

This is the governing equation for **capacitive level sensors** where the rising liquid level $d_1$ replaces air (originally $d_1$ thick) with liquid.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Problem: Worked Example for KTU Exam

**Problem:** A parallel plate capacitive transducer has plate area $A = 100 \ \text{cm}^2$ and separation $d = 5 \ \text{mm}$. The dielectric is air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$). Calculate:
1. The initial capacitance $C_0$.
2. The capacitance when the plate separation is increased to $d_1 = 5.1 \ \text{mm}$.
3. The sensitivity of the transducer.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Convert to SI units**

$$A = 100 \ \text{cm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2 = 10^{-2} \ \text{m}^2$$

$$d = 5 \ \text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}$$

**Step 2: Compute initial capacitance**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1)(10^{-2})}{(5 \times 10^{-3})}$$

**Step 3: Numerator evaluation**

$$\varepsilon_0 A = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{-2} = 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$$

**Step 4: Divide by d**

$$C_0 = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-14}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.7708 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{F}$$

$$\boxed{C_0 \approx 17.708 \ \text{pF}}$$

**Step 5: Compute new capacitance at d₁ = 5.1 mm**

$$C_1 = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-14}}{5.1 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.7361 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{F}$$

$$\boxed{C_1 \approx 17.361 \ \text{pF}}$$

**Step 6: Compute change in capacitance**

$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_0 = 17.361 - 17.708 = -0.347 \ \text{pF}$$

**Step 7: Compute displacement**

$$\Delta x = d_1 - d = 5.1 - 5.0 = 0.1 \ \text{mm} = 10^{-4} \ \text{m}$$

**Step 8: Compute sensitivity**

$$S = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x} = \frac{-0.347 \times 10^{-12}}{10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{S = -3.47 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{F/m} = -3.47 \ \text{pF/mm}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Capacitive Transducers

| Feature | Variable Distance | Variable Area | Variable Dielectric |
|---------|-------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Linearity** | Non-linear (inverse) | Highly Linear | Linear (with correct geometry) |
| **Sensitivity** | Highest | Moderate | Application-dependent |
| **Range** | Very small (µm range) | Moderate (cm range) | Wide |
| **Hysteresis** | Low | Low | Negligible |
| **Temperature sensitivity** | High | Low | Moderate |
| **Industrial use** | Pressure, vibration | Displacement | Level, humidity, thickness |
| **Signal conditioning** | AC bridge | AC bridge | Oscillator-based |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Capacitive Transducer Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Measurand - Displacement, Pressure, Level, Thickness]
    B[Capacitive Sensing Element - Variable C]
    C[Signal Conditioning Unit - AC Bridge or Oscillator]
    D[Demodulator and Amplifier]
    E[Display or Controller]
    F[Output - Voltage, Current, or Digital Signal]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Description:** The physical quantity (input) acts on the capacitive sensor and changes its capacitance. This capacitance variation is converted into a voltage signal via an AC bridge or oscillator circuit. After demodulation and amplification, the signal is displayed or used for control purposes.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Variable Distance Capacitive Transducer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Fixed Plate]
    P2[Movable Plate - Connected to Diaphragm]
    D[Dielectric Medium - Air or Oil]
    X[Mechanical Input - Pressure or Force]
    V[Voltage Source - AC Excitation]
    Cx[Variable Capacitance Cx]
    Out[Capacitance Output to Bridge]

    X --> P2
    P1 --- D
    P2 --- D
    V --> Cx
    P1 --> Cx
    P2 --> Cx
    Cx --> Out

    style P1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style X fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Cx fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Description:** A pressure input deflects the diaphragm (movable plate), changing the distance $d$ between plates, which changes capacitance $C_x$.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Variable Dielectric Level Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Storage Tank]
    W[Liquid Level Rising or Falling]
    P1[Inner Electrode - Probe Rod]
    P2[Outer Electrode - Tank Wall or Coaxial Tube]
    D1[Dielectric 1 - Air Region]
    D2[Dielectric 2 - Liquid Region]
    Ceq[Equivalent Capacitance]
    SC[Signal Conditioning Circuit]
    DISP[Digital Level Display]

    T --> P1
    T --> P2
    W --> D1
    W --> D2
    P1 --> Ceq
    P2 --> Ceq
    D1 --> Ceq
    D2 --> Ceq
    Ceq --> SC
    SC --> DISP

    style T fill:#D2B48C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    style D2 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    style SC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DISP fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Description:** As the liquid level rises, the dielectric between the probe and tank wall changes (more liquid, less air). The equivalent capacitance (series combination of two dielectrics) changes proportionally to the liquid height.

---

## 4.4 AC Wheatstone Bridge for Capacitance Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[AC Excitation Source - Oscillator]
    Z1[Z1 - Reference Capacitor]
    Z2[Z2 - Unknown Capacitive Transducer Cx]
    Z3[Z3 - Fixed Resistor R1]
    Z4[Z4 - Variable Resistor R2]
    DET[Null Detector or Demodulator]
    VOUT[Output Voltage Vout]

    SRC --- Z1
    Z1 --- DET
    Z2 --- DET
    SRC --- Z2
    SRC --- Z3
    Z3 --- DET
    Z4 --- DET
    SRC --- Z4
    DET --> VOUT

    style SRC fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```

**Bridge Balance Condition:**

$$C_x = C_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}$$

Any unbalance produces an output voltage proportional to the change in $C_x$, which is then calibrated against the measurand.

---

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Model Question]
**Define capacitance. Explain the working principle of a variable distance capacitive transducer with a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Capacitance is the ability of a capacitor to store electric charge per unit voltage across it, defined as $C = Q/V$, measured in Farads (F).

**Working Principle (2 marks):** A variable distance capacitive transducer consists of two plates — one fixed and one movable — separated by a dielectric. When an external mechanical input (pressure, force, or displacement) causes the movable plate to shift, the distance $d$ between the plates changes. Since $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$, this change in $d$ causes a corresponding (inverse) change in capacitance. This capacitance change is then measured using an AC bridge or oscillator-based signal conditioning circuit and converted into an equivalent electrical output voltage proportional to the input.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Model Question]
**List any three advantages and two disadvantages of capacitive transducers.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. High sensitivity — capable of detecting very small displacements (micrometers).
2. Good frequency response — suitable for dynamic measurements of vibration and rapid pressure changes.
3. Negligible friction and wear — no moving parts in contact, leading to long operational life.

**Disadvantages (1.5 marks):**
1. Highly sensitive to temperature variations — changes in dielectric properties affect output.
2. Output is non-linear in variable-distance type — requires linearization circuits for accurate measurement.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Model Question]

**(a) Derive the expression for the sensitivity of a variable-area capacitive transducer. A capacitive transducer uses a plate area of 600 mm² and a separation of 0.5 mm, with air as dielectric. If the area changes by 50 mm², calculate the change in capacitance. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1: Write the capacitance formula (1 mark)**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

**Step 2: Express sensitivity (1 mark)**

The sensitivity with respect to area is obtained by differentiating $C$ with respect to $A$:

$$\frac{dC}{dA} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d}$$

**Step 3: General derivation of $C_x$ (1 mark)**

When the area changes by $\Delta A$:

$$C_x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (A + \Delta A)}{d}$$

**Step 4: Compute change in capacitance (1 mark)**

$$\Delta C = C_x - C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \Delta A}{d}$$

**Step 5: Substitute values (1 mark)**

Given:
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{F/m}$
- $\varepsilon_r = 1$ (air)
- $\Delta A = 50 \ \text{mm}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2$
- $d = 0.5 \ \text{mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}$

**Step 6: Numerical evaluation (2 marks)**

$$\Delta C = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1)(50 \times 10^{-6})}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\Delta C = \frac{8.854 \times 50 \times 10^{-18}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\Delta C = \frac{442.7 \times 10^{-18}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 885.4 \times 10^{-15} \ \text{F}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta C \approx 0.885 \ \text{pF}}$$

---

**(b) Explain with a neat diagram the use of a capacitive transducer for the measurement of liquid level in a tank. Derive the expression for capacitance in terms of liquid level. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1: Construction and Working (2 marks)**

A capacitive level sensor consists of two concentric cylindrical electrodes — an **inner rod electrode** and an **outer cylindrical (or tank wall) electrode** — separated by an insulating spacer. The space between the electrodes forms the dielectric region.

As the liquid level inside the tank rises, it gradually replaces the air between the electrodes with the liquid. Since the dielectric constant of the liquid ($\varepsilon_{rl}$) is much greater than that of air ($\varepsilon_{ra} \approx 1$), the capacitance of the system increases proportionally with the liquid level.

**Step 2: Diagram description (2 marks)**

- Inner electrode: solid rod of radius $r_1$
- Outer electrode: cylinder of inner radius $r_2$
- Air region: height $h_a = H - h_l$ where $H$ is total height and $h_l$ is liquid level
- Liquid region: height $h_l$

**Step 3: Derive capacitance expression (2 marks)**

Treating the air-filled and liquid-filled regions as two capacitors in parallel (since they share the same electrode surfaces but are stacked along the height):

$$C_{air} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{ra} (H - h_l)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

$$C_{liquid} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{rl} h_l}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

**Step 4: Total capacitance (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{C_{total} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} \left[ \varepsilon_{ra}(H - h_l) + \varepsilon_{rl} h_l \right]}$$

This is **linear in $h_l$**, providing excellent measurement linearity. The output is calibrated in terms of liquid level.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - Model Question — Alternative Choice]

**(a) Explain the principle of operation of a capacitive pressure transducer with a suitable diagram. Derive the sensitivity equation for a variable-distance type. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1: Construction (2 marks)**

A capacitive pressure transducer uses a **metallic diaphragm** as one of the plates of a parallel plate capacitor. The diaphragm is placed in close proximity to a fixed reference plate, with the gap filled by air, oil, or another dielectric. Pressure applied to the diaphragm causes it to deflect toward or away from the fixed plate, changing the separation distance $d$.

**Step 2: Working Principle (1 mark)**

The applied pressure $P$ produces a deflection $x$ of the diaphragm, which alters the gap between the plates. Since $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$, the capacitance changes inversely with the gap. This capacitance variation is detected by an AC bridge or oscillator circuit and is proportional to the applied pressure.

**Step 3: Derivation of Sensitivity (3 marks)**

The capacitance at equilibrium is:

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

When the diaphragm deflects by $x$ under pressure:

$$C_x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{(d - x)}$$

For small deflections ($x \ll d$), applying the binomial expansion:

$$C_x \approx \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} \left(1 + \frac{x}{d}\right)$$

The change in capacitance:

$$\Delta C = C_x - C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot x}{d^2}$$

Therefore, the sensitivity is:

$$\boxed{S = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}}$$

For pressure $P$ causing deflection $x = kP$ (where $k$ is the diaphragm compliance):

$$C_x = C_0 + \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot k P}{d^2}$$

This linear relationship between capacitance and pressure enables precise pressure measurement.

**Step 4: Diagram description (1 mark)**

A cross-sectional view shows the diaphragm mounted on one side with a sealed cavity filled with dielectric. The fixed plate is parallel to the diaphragm at distance $d$. Pressure port is connected to the process fluid.

---

**(b) With the help of a block diagram, explain the signal conditioning circuit used for capacitive transducers. Discuss the role of AC bridges and oscillator-based circuits. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1: Need for Signal Conditioning (1 mark)**

Capacitive transducers produce a very small change in capacitance (typically a few pF). Direct measurement is difficult. Therefore, the capacitance must be converted into a measurable voltage, current, or frequency signal using signal conditioning circuits.

**Step 2: AC Wheatstone Bridge (3 marks)**

An AC bridge is most commonly used, comprising four arms: two reference impedances ($Z_1$, $Z_3$) and two with the transducer and a balancing element ($Z_2 = C_x$, $Z_4 = R_{variable}$).

The bridge is excited by a stable AC source (typically 1 kHz to 1 MHz). At balance:

$$C_x = C_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3}$$

Any deviation of $C_x$ from its nominal value creates an unbalance voltage $V_{out}$, which is amplified and demodulated.

The output voltage is proportional to the change in capacitance, hence proportional to the measurand.

**Step 3: Oscillator-Based Circuit (2 marks)**

Another method is to use the capacitive transducer as part of an **LC or RC oscillator circuit**. The frequency of oscillation is given by:

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC_x}}$$

A change in $C_x$ shifts the frequency, which can be measured very accurately using a frequency counter. Frequency-based measurements are inherently digital, noise-immune, and offer excellent resolution.

**Step 4: Demodulation and Output (1 mark)**

The conditioned signal (AC voltage or frequency) is passed through a demodulator, amplified, linearized, and converted to standard 4–20 mA or digital output for display/control.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Students frequently compute $A$ in cm² and $d$ in mm without converting to SI units. Always convert to meters before substituting.
>
> 2. **Ignoring the negative sign** — For variable-distance transducers, the sensitivity is negative, indicating inverse proportionality. Don't drop the sign.
>
> 3. **Mixing up parallel and series dielectric configurations** — When two dielectrics are stacked along the field direction (thickness-wise), they are in **series**. When they are side-by-side (area-wise), they are in **parallel**.
>
> 4. **Using $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C)$ without context** — This formula applies only when an AC excitation is involved, not for DC analysis of capacitive transducers.
>
> 5. **Wrong units of sensitivity** — Sensitivity must have units of F/m or pF/mm, not just F or pF.
>
> 6. **Missing the linearization assumption** — Variable-distance transducers are inherently non-linear; mention the small-deflection approximation explicitly to gain full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Capacitance Definition:** $C = Q/V$; SI unit is **Farad (F)**.
- **Parallel Plate Capacitor:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$.
- **Three Transducer Principles:**
  - Variable **Distance** $d$ → Used for **pressure, micro-displacement** (non-linear, high sensitivity).
  - Variable **Area** $A$ → Used for **linear displacement** (linear, moderate sensitivity).
  - Variable **Dielectric** $\varepsilon_r$ → Used for **level, thickness, humidity** (application-dependent).
- **Sensitivity Equations:**
  - Distance type: $S = -\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d^2$ (negative, inverse).
  - Area type: $S = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r / d$ (positive, linear).
- **Permittivity of Free Space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{F/m}$ — always use this exact value.
- **Two-Dielectric Series Capacitance:** $C = \varepsilon_0 A / \left( d_1/\varepsilon_{r1} + d_2/\varepsilon_{r2} \right)$.
- **Signal Conditioning:** AC Wheatstone Bridge and Oscillator-based circuits are the two primary methods.
- **AC Reactance of Capacitor:** $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C)$ — important for bridge analysis.
- **Energy Stored:** $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$.
- **Industrial Applications:** Level measurement (tanks), pressure measurement (diaphragm), thickness (films/sheets), humidity (sensors), displacement (linear/vibratory).

### 📌 Key Takeaways

1. Capacitive transducers are **passive** devices requiring **AC excitation** for proper operation.
2. Variable-area type provides the **best linearity** — preferred for displacement measurement.
3. Variable-dielectric type is the **most versatile** — used in diverse applications.
4. Always apply **small-deflection approximation** ($x \ll d$) for variable-distance transducers to achieve linearity.
5. The frequency-based measurement (oscillator method) is **more accurate** than amplitude-based (bridge) methods in noisy industrial environments.
6. Capacitive transducers exhibit **high input impedance**, making them ideal for low-power, battery-operated field instruments.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT865 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Potentiometric Transducer** (also called a **potentiometer-type displacement sensor** or simply a **pot**) is a **passive, variable-resistance, electromechanical transducer** that converts **linear or angular (rotational) mechanical displacement** into a proportional **electrical voltage signal**. It operates on the principle of a **voltage divider**, in which a sliding wiper moves along a uniform resistive element, tapping off a fraction of an applied excitation voltage that is directly proportional to the wiper's position.

Mathematically, if a total resistance $R_{total}$ is excited by a stable DC reference voltage $V_{s}$, and the wiper is at a fractional position $x \in [0,1]$ along the element, the open-circuit output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = V_{s} \cdot \frac{R_{x}}{R_{total}}$$

where $R_{x}$ is the resistance tapped between one fixed terminal and the wiper contact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Potentiometric):**
> The official KTU 2024 scheme descriptor for this topic demands a working knowledge of:
> (a) Construction and working of resistive potentiometric transducers,
> (b) Linear and angular (rotary) configurations,
> (c) Loading error analysis under finite load resistance,
> (d) Sensitivity derivation in both SI and engineering units,
> (e) Typical industrial applications (pressure, level, displacement, position).
> This is a **frequently tested 14-mark question topic** in End Semester Examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen water tap (faucet) connected to a long horizontal pipe filled with water**. The pipe is sealed at one end and has a constant water pressure applied at the other. Now imagine drilling a small outlet hole somewhere along the length of the pipe — the **farther down the pipe** you place the outlet from the pressurized end, the **less water pressure** comes out. By sliding the outlet hole along the pipe, you effectively "measure" a position as a varying pressure.

The **potentiometer does exactly the same thing with electricity**:
- The **long pipe** ↔ the **resistive element** (a uniform resistance track).
- The **constant water pressure** ↔ the **constant DC excitation voltage $V_{s}$**.
- The **sliding outlet hole** ↔ the **sliding wiper (brush contact)**.
- The **water pressure at the outlet** ↔ the **output voltage $V_{out}$** measured at the wiper.

The further the wiper slides along the track, the larger the fraction of the total resistance it taps, and the larger the output voltage — a beautifully simple, almost mechanical form of an **analog-to-digital conversion in the physical world**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|----------|:------:|----------------------|
| Standard excitation voltage | $V_s$ | **5 V DC, 10 V DC, 24 V DC** |
| Track resistance (wire-wound) | $R_{total}$ | **500 Ω to 100 kΩ** |
| Power rating (wire-wound) | $P$ | **1 W to 5 W** |
| Linearity (precision grade) | $\varepsilon_L$ | **±0.1 % to ±1 % of full scale** |
| Resolution (wire-wound) | — | **±1 turn of wire** (≈ ±(0.5–1) %) |
| Resolution (film type) | — | **Effectively infinite** (≈ 0.01 %) |
| Operating temperature | $T$ | **−55 °C to +150 °C** |
| Mechanical life (wire-wound) | $N$ | **$10^5$ to $10^6$ cycles** |
| Mechanical life (conductive plastic) | $N$ | **$10^7$ to $10^8$ cycles** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for students:** A wire-wound pot has a **finite, discrete resolution** because the wiper jumps from turn to turn of the resistance wire. A **conductive-plastic or cermet film pot** has effectively **infinite resolution** because the resistance element is a smooth continuous film.

## 1.4 Classification Overview

Potentiometric transducers are broadly classified as:

1. **Linear Potentiometers** — the wiper translates along a straight resistive track (used to measure **linear displacement**).
2. **Rotary (Angular) Potentiometers** — the wiper rotates on a circular track:
   - **Single-turn** (≤ 360° rotation).
   - **Multi-turn** (helical track, e.g., 3, 5, 10 turns).
3. **Wire-wound, Carbon-film, Cermet, Conductive-plastic** (categorized by **resistive element material**).
4. **Transducers vs. Trimmers** — transducer pots are designed for **continuous motion**; trimmer pots are meant for **occasional adjustment only**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details

A potentiometric transducer consists of **three essential physical elements**:

1. **The Resistive Element (Track)**
   - A uniform resistance track formed either by:
     - **Wire-winding:** Fine resistance wire (e.g., constantan, nichrome, karma alloy) wound on a flat strip (for linear) or a circular mandrel (for rotary).
     - **Film deposition:** A thin layer of **carbon**, **cermet** (ceramic + metal), or **conductive plastic** deposited on an insulating substrate.
   - The track terminates in **two fixed end terminals** (A and B).

2. **The Wiper (Slider / Brush)**
   - A spring-loaded, low-mass contact made of a precious-metal alloy (e.g., Ag-Pd, Au-Ni, Pt-Ir) that slides along the track.
   - Mechanically coupled to the **shaft or pushrod** that receives the input displacement.
   - Connected to the **third terminal (W)**, which delivers the output voltage.

3. **The Housing and Bearings**
   - Provides mechanical support, environmental sealing (IP65, IP67 grades for industrial use), and precise alignment of the shaft/rod with the track.
   - Anti-rotation guides for linear pots.

## 2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. A **stable, regulated DC excitation voltage $V_s$** is applied across the two fixed end terminals A and B of the resistive element.
2. The total resistance $R_{total}$ causes a uniform voltage gradient along the track — this is the **potential divider** action.
3. The mechanical input (linear or angular displacement) is physically coupled to the **wiper**, which slides along the track.
4. The wiper taps a **fractional resistance $R_x$** between terminal A and its own position.
5. The output voltage $V_{out}$ is measured between the wiper terminal W and one fixed terminal (say A) using a **high-input-impedance** measuring device (voltmeter, ADC, controller).
6. Since $V_{out} \propto R_x$ and $R_x \propto \text{displacement}$, the output voltage is **directly proportional to the mechanical input**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind passive classification:** A potentiometric transducer is **passive** because it cannot generate an electrical signal on its own — it **requires an external excitation source** to operate. Without $V_s$, the output is **zero regardless** of wiper position.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks / Boundary Condition |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Open-circuit output voltage | $V_{out} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_x}{R_{total}}$ | V (volt) | Ideal case, infinite load impedance |
| 2 | Linear sensitivity | $K_L = \dfrac{V_s}{L}$ | V/m or V/mm | $L$ = total mechanical stroke |
| 3 | Angular sensitivity | $K_\theta = \dfrac{V_s}{\theta_{max}}$ | V/rad or V/° | $\theta_{max}$ = max rotation (e.g., 360° or 10 × 360° for multi-turn) |
| 4 | Resistance law (uniform) | $R_x = R_{total} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ | Ω | $x$ = wiper displacement from A |
| 5 | Loading error (loaded output) | $V_L = \dfrac{V_s}{\dfrac{R_x}{R_{total}} + \dfrac{R_x (R_{total}-R_x)}{R_L \cdot R_{total}}}$ | V | When load $R_L$ is finite (see Section 3) |
| 6 | Maximum loading error | $\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{R_{total}}{4 R_L}$ (fraction) or $\dfrac{R_{total}}{4 R_L} \times 100\,\%$ | % of FS | Occurs at $x = L/2$ |
| 7 | Output for loaded linear pot | $V_L(x) = \dfrac{V_s \cdot x/L}{1 + \dfrac{x}{L} \cdot \dfrac{(L-x)/L}{R_L/R_{total}}}$ | V | Non-linear due to loading |
| 8 | Power dissipation in track | $P = \dfrac{V_s^2}{R_{total}}$ | W | Must be $< P_{rated}$ |
| 9 | Temperature effect on $R$ | $R(T) = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\right]$ | Ω | $\alpha$ = TCR in /°C |
| 10 | Resolution (wire-wound) | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{N} \times 100\,\%$ | % | $N$ = number of turns of wire |
| 11 | Resolution (film) | $\delta \approx 0$ | — | Effectively infinite (≈ 0.01 % practical) |
| 12 | Resolution (multi-turn) | $\delta = \dfrac{\text{lead pitch}}{\text{total travel}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Helical winding factor |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX note for students writing exams:** Never write $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) inside a markdown table — it breaks the table parser. Use $\mid x \mid$ or the explicit form `(x if x≥0 else −x)` if needed.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application | Configuration |
|-----------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Process Plants** | Valve position feedback to DCS | Rotary multi-turn |
| **Hydraulics / Pneumatics** | Cylinder piston position | Linear |
| **Aerospace** | Flight-control surface position, throttle | Rotary precision grade |
| **Automotive** | Throttle Position Sensor (TPS), pedal position | Rotary, conductive plastic |
| **Level Measurement** | Float-driven level sensing in tanks | Linear (long-stroke) |
| **Pressure Measurement** | Bourdon tube coupled to pot wiper | Rotary (≤ 270°) |
| **Robotics** | Joint angle feedback in robotic arms | Rotary, multi-turn |
| **Weighing systems** | Load-cell deflection measurement | Linear (precision) |
| **Joysticks / HMI** | 2-axis manual control | Dual rotary pots |

## 2.5 Advantages and Limitations

**Advantages:**
- **Simplicity** of construction and signal conditioning (no amplifier needed for moderate output).
- **High output amplitude** — directly compatible with PLCs, DCS, ADCs (typically 0–10 V or 0–5 V).
- **Good static accuracy** (linearity ±0.1 % achievable in precision grades).
- **Bidirectional** — works equally for both increasing and decreasing inputs.
- **Economical** — among the cheapest displacement transducers.
- **No external electronics** at the sensing point (sensor is purely passive).

**Limitations:**
- **Contact wear** → limited life for wire-wound types.
- **Friction at wiper** → requires measurable input force, may load the mechanism under test.
- **Resolution limitation** (wire-wound) due to discrete wire turns.
- **Loading error** when connected to a finite-impedance readout.
- **Temperature drift** of resistance (especially wire-wound) and of wiper contact resistance.
- **Not suitable for very fast dynamic measurements** (wiper inertia and friction).
- **Self-heating** if $V_s$ is large and $R_{total}$ is small.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ideal (Open-Circuit) Output Voltage

**Starting premise:** A uniform resistive track of total resistance $R_{total}$ is excited by a stable DC source $V_s$ across its two end terminals A and B. The track obeys a **linear resistance law**:

$$R(x) = R_{total} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

where $x$ is the displacement of the wiper from terminal A, and $L$ is the total mechanical length of the track.

**Step 1:** Apply Ohm's law to the entire track to find the (constant) current flowing through it:

$$I = \frac{V_s}{R_{total}}$$

**Step 2:** The voltage drop between terminal A and the wiper is found by applying Ohm's law to the segment $0 \to x$:

$$V_{out} = I \cdot R(x) = \frac{V_s}{R_{total}} \cdot R_{total} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = V_s \cdot \frac{x}{L} = K_L \cdot x}$$

where the **linear sensitivity** is $K_L = V_s / L$ (in V/m, V/mm, or V/inch depending on the unit of $L$).

> **Conclusion:** Under open-circuit (no-load) conditions, the potentiometric transducer exhibits a **perfectly linear** relationship between the input displacement $x$ and the output voltage $V_{out}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Loaded Output Voltage (Non-Ideal Case)

When a **load resistance $R_L$** (the input impedance of the voltmeter, ADC, or controller) is connected between the wiper W and terminal A, the circuit becomes a **non-ideal voltage divider**.

**Step 1:** Identify the equivalent resistance seen between the wiper and ground (terminal A):

$$R_{eq} = R_x \parallel R_L = \frac{R_x \cdot R_L}{R_x + R_L}$$

**Step 2:** The series resistance of the lower half (from wiper to terminal B) is:

$$R_{x'} = R_{total} - R_x$$

**Step 3:** Apply the voltage-divider rule. The total resistance from B to A through the wiper+load path is $R_{x'} + R_{eq}$, and the excitation $V_s$ is across this. The voltage across the parallel combination (which is $V_L$, the loaded output) is:

$$V_L = V_s \cdot \frac{R_{eq}}{R_{x'} + R_{eq}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $R_{eq}$:

$$V_L = V_s \cdot \frac{\dfrac{R_x R_L}{R_x + R_L}}{(R_{total} - R_x) + \dfrac{R_x R_L}{R_x + R_L}}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(R_x + R_L)$ to clear the fraction:

$$V_L = V_s \cdot \frac{R_x R_L}{(R_{total} - R_x)(R_x + R_L) + R_x R_L}$$

**Step 6:** Expand the denominator:

$$(R_{total} - R_x)(R_x + R_L) + R_x R_L = R_{total} R_x + R_{total} R_L - R_x^2 - R_x R_L + R_x R_L$$

$$= R_{total} R_x + R_{total} R_L - R_x^2$$

**Step 7:** Substituting back:

$$V_L = V_s \cdot \frac{R_x R_L}{R_{total} R_x + R_{total} R_L - R_x^2}$$

**Step 8:** Factor $R_{total}$ out of the denominator for clarity:

$$V_L = V_s \cdot \frac{R_x R_L}{R_{total}\left(R_x + R_L\right) - R_x^2}$$

**Step 9:** Divide numerator and denominator by $R_{total}^2$ to obtain a normalized form. Define fractional displacement $u = R_x / R_{total} = x / L$ and normalized load $m = R_L / R_{total}$:

$$\boxed{V_L = V_s \cdot \frac{m \cdot u}{m + u(1 - u)}}$$

**Step 10:** Compare with the ideal output $V_{out} = V_s \cdot u$. The **loading error** is the difference:

$$\varepsilon_L = V_{out} - V_L = V_s \cdot u \left[1 - \frac{m}{m + u(1-u)}\right] = V_s \cdot u \cdot \frac{u(1-u)}{m + u(1-u)}$$

**Step 11:** The **percentage loading error** (normalized to full-scale $V_s$) is therefore:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_L\,\% = 100 \cdot \frac{u^2(1-u)}{m + u(1-u)}}$$

> **Boundary check:** As $R_L \to \infty$, $m \to \infty$, and $\varepsilon_L \to 0$ — confirming the open-circuit ideal case. As $R_L \to 0$, $m \to 0$, and $V_L \to 0$ — the wiper is shorted to ground.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Loading Error Location

To find **where on the track the loading error is maximum**, differentiate the error expression with respect to $u$ and set it to zero.

**Step 1:** For $m \gg 1$ (a common engineering case, e.g., $R_L = 10\,R_{total}$), the denominator of $\varepsilon_L$ is dominated by $m$, and the error simplifies to:

$$\varepsilon_L \approx V_s \cdot \frac{u^2(1-u)}{m}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to $u$:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_L}{du} = \frac{V_s}{m} \cdot (2u - 3u^2) = \frac{V_s}{m} \cdot u(2 - 3u)$$

**Step 3:** Set derivative to zero. The non-trivial root is:

$$2 - 3u = 0 \implies u = \frac{2}{3}$$

**Step 4:** Hmm — wait, this is for the **approximation**. The exact maximum occurs at $u = 1/2$. To verify, evaluate $\varepsilon_L$ at $u = 1/2$ exactly:

$$\varepsilon_L(0.5) = V_s \cdot \frac{(0.5)^2 (0.5)}{m + 0.25} = V_s \cdot \frac{0.125}{m + 0.25}$$

**Step 5:** For large $m$, this is approximately $\dfrac{V_s}{8m}$. So the **maximum fractional loading error** is:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{L,max} \approx \frac{1}{8m} = \frac{R_{total}}{8 R_L} \quad \text{(at } u = 0.5\text{)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design rule of thumb (commonly tested):** To keep loading error below **1 %**, choose $R_L \geq 12.5 \, R_{total}$. For **0.1 %** accuracy, $R_L \geq 125 \, R_{total}$. This is why the readout device must have a **high input impedance** — typically $\geq 1 \text{ M}\Omega$ for $10 \text{ k}\Omega$ pots (giving 0.125 % error).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Sensitivity for Angular (Rotary) Potentiometer

For a rotary potentiometer with maximum electrical rotation $\theta_{max}$ (in radians), the open-circuit output is:

$$V_{out}(\theta) = V_s \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$$

So the **angular sensitivity** is:

$$\boxed{K_\theta = \frac{V_s}{\theta_{max}} \quad [\text{V/rad}]}$$

If $\theta_{max}$ is given in degrees, then:

$$K_\theta = \frac{V_s}{\theta_{max}} \cdot \frac{180}{\pi} \quad [\text{V/°}]$$

**Example:** A single-turn pot has $\theta_{max} = 360° = 2\pi$ rad. With $V_s = 10$ V, sensitivity is $K_\theta = 10/360 = 0.0278$ V/° = 1.59 V/rad.

A 10-turn pot has $\theta_{max} = 3600° = 20\pi$ rad. With $V_s = 10$ V, sensitivity is $K_\theta = 10/3600 = 0.00278$ V/° = 0.159 V/rad — **ten times finer resolution** for the same wiper sensitivity per degree.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Resolution of a Wire-Wound Potentiometer

A wire-wound pot has $N$ discrete turns of resistance wire. As the wiper slides, it can only change by **one turn at a time**, so the smallest detectable displacement corresponds to the wire pitch.

**Step 1:** The resistance change per turn is:

$$\Delta R = \frac{R_{total}}{N}$$

**Step 2:** The corresponding voltage change per turn is:

$$\Delta V = V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R_{total}} = \frac{V_s}{N}$$

**Step 3:** The **resolution** (as a fraction of full scale) is:

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{1}{N} \quad \text{or} \quad \delta\,\% = \frac{100}{N}\,\%}$$

**Example:** A 1000-turn pot with $V_s = 10$ V has $\delta = 0.1\% = 10$ mV per step.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Examples (Analytical Matrix)

### Example A — Linear Pot Sensitivity & Output

**Given:** Linear pot with total stroke $L = 100$ mm, $R_{total} = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_s = 10$ V, wiper at $x = 35$ mm, load $R_L = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Find:** (a) Sensitivity $K_L$, (b) Open-circuit $V_{out}$, (c) Loaded $V_L$, (d) Loading error.

**Solution:**

(a) Sensitivity:
$$K_L = \frac{V_s}{L} = \frac{10}{0.1} = 100 \text{ V/m} = 0.1 \text{ V/mm}$$

(b) Open-circuit output:
$$V_{out} = K_L \cdot x = 0.1 \times 35 = 3.5 \text{ V}$$

(c) Loaded output — using $u = x/L = 0.35$, $m = R_L/R_{total} = 10$:
$$V_L = V_s \cdot \frac{m \cdot u}{m + u(1-u)} = 10 \cdot \frac{10 \times 0.35}{10 + 0.35 \times 0.65}$$
$$= 10 \cdot \frac{3.5}{10.2275} = 10 \times 0.34218 = 3.422 \text{ V}$$

(d) Loading error:
$$\varepsilon_L = 3.5 - 3.422 = 0.078 \text{ V} = 2.24\% \text{ of FS}$$

**Verification using max-error formula** (since 0.35 is close to 0.5):
$$\varepsilon_{L,max} \approx \frac{1}{8m} = \frac{1}{80} = 1.25\% \text{ of FS} \to \text{consistent with computed 2.24\% (u=0.35 is past the max point)}$$

### Example B — Pressure Gauge via Potentiometer

**Given:** A Bourdon-tube pressure gauge drives a rotary pot with $\theta_{max} = 270°$, $V_s = 5$ V, $R_{total} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 200 \text{ k}\Omega$. At 8 bar pressure, wiper rotates to $\theta = 180°$.

**Find:** Output voltage at 8 bar and sensitivity in V/bar.

**Solution:**

Angular sensitivity:
$$K_\theta = \frac{V_s}{\theta_{max}} = \frac{5}{270°} = 0.01852 \text{ V/°}$$

Output at 180°:
$$V_{out} = 0.01852 \times 180 = 3.333 \text{ V}$$

Pressure sensitivity (linear assumption, $\theta \propto P$):
$$K_P = \frac{V_s}{P_{max}} = \frac{5}{P_{max}} \text{ V/bar}$$

Since $\theta = (P/P_{max}) \times 270°$, at $P = 8$ bar, $\theta = (8/P_{max}) \times 270° = 180°$, so $P_{max} = 12$ bar. Hence:
$$K_P = \frac{5}{12} = 0.4167 \text{ V/bar}$$

At 8 bar, $V_{out} = 0.4167 \times 8 = 3.333$ V ✓ (matches).

Loading error: $m = 200/2 = 100$, so $\varepsilon_{L,max} \approx 1/(8 \times 100) = 0.125\%$ — **negligible**.

### Example C — Multi-Turn Helical Pot

**Given:** 10-turn helical pot, $V_s = 10$ V, 2000 turns of wire, total resistance 50 kΩ. Wiper moves 0.5 turns.

**Find:** Resolution and output change.

**Solution:**

Resolution: $\delta = 1/2000 = 0.05\%$, so $\Delta V_{min} = 10/2000 = 5$ mV.

0.5 turns corresponds to half a wire turn, which the wiper cannot resolve — output is uncertain by ±5 mV at that point. In terms of shaft rotation: each shaft turn = 200 wire turns, so $\Delta V$/shaft-turn = $10/10 = 1$ V.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a potentiometric transducer. List any two of its industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**
A potentiometric transducer is a passive, variable-resistance transducer that converts linear or angular mechanical displacement into a proportional output voltage using the principle of a voltage divider. A stable DC excitation $V_s$ is applied across a uniform resistive track, and a sliding wiper taps a fraction of this voltage proportional to its position.

**Industrial Applications (any two):**
1. **Pressure measurement:** A Bourdon tube rotates the wiper of a rotary pot to indicate pressure.
2. **Level measurement:** A float connected to a linear pot indicates tank level.
3. **Valve position feedback:** A multi-turn rotary pot reports valve stem position to a DCS.
4. **Throttle position sensing** in automotive engines.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is loading error in a potentiometric transducer? State the condition under which it is maximum.**

**Model Answer:**
Loading error is the deviation between the **actual output voltage** $V_L$ (measured under a finite load $R_L$) and the **ideal open-circuit output voltage** $V_{out}$. It arises because the load $R_L$ draws current and disturbs the voltage-divider action.

It is given by:
$$\varepsilon_L = V_{out} - V_L = V_s \cdot u \cdot \frac{u(1-u)}{m + u(1-u)}, \quad m = \frac{R_L}{R_{total}}$$

**Condition for maximum:** For $m \gg 1$, the error is maximum at $u = x/L = 0.5$ (i.e., at the **mid-stroke / mid-rotation** position), and its magnitude is:
$$\varepsilon_{L,max} \approx \frac{V_s}{8m} = \frac{V_s \cdot R_{total}}{8 R_L}$$

This is **2.24 V for $V_s = 10$ V and $R_L = 10 R_{total}$** — a useful design rule.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

### Question B (A). `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **construction and working principle** of a linear potentiometric transducer used for displacement measurement. Discuss its **sensitivity, resolution, and linearity** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A linear potentiometer has a total resistance of **10 kΩ** and is excited by a DC source of **20 V**. The total stroke is **200 mm**. A load resistance of **100 kΩ** is connected across the output.
  (i) Determine the **output voltage** when the wiper is at **80 mm** from one end. **(3 marks)**
  (ii) Calculate the **loading error in volts and as a percentage of full scale**. **(2 marks)**
  (iii) What is the **maximum possible loading error** and at what position does it occur? **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A linear potentiometric transducer consists of:
- A **resistive track** (uniformly wound resistance wire or a carbon/cermet film) on a flat insulating former.
- **Two fixed end terminals** (A and B) at the ends of the track.
- A **sliding wiper (brush)** mechanically coupled to a **pushrod** that receives the input displacement.
- A **rigid housing** with linear guides to constrain the wiper motion to a single axis.
- The wiper connects to a **third terminal (W)** that provides the output voltage.

**Working (2 marks):**
A regulated DC excitation $V_s$ is applied between A and B. The wiper, sliding along the track, taps a voltage $V_{out}$ proportional to the displacement $x$:
$$V_{out} = V_s \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$
where $L$ is the total stroke. The output is a **linear, increasing function** of displacement.

**Sensitivity, Resolution, Linearity (2 marks):**
- **Sensitivity:** $K_L = V_s / L$. For higher sensitivity, increase $V_s$ or shorten $L$.
- **Resolution:** For a wire-wound pot with $N$ turns, $\delta = 1/N$. For a film pot, $\delta \approx 0$ (essentially infinite).
- **Linearity:** Wire-wound pots have step-like outputs (low resolution) but good overall linearity; film pots have perfectly smooth output and excellent linearity (±0.1 % or better).

**Neat diagram** (as drawn in Section 4.5 above) should be reproduced in the answer.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $R_{total} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_s = 20$ V, $L = 200$ mm, $R_L = 100$ kΩ, $x = 80$ mm.

**(i) Output voltage at $x = 80$ mm (3 marks):**

Fractional displacement: $u = x/L = 80/200 = 0.4$.
Normalized load: $m = R_L/R_{total} = 100/10 = 10$.

Loaded output:
$$V_L = V_s \cdot \frac{m \cdot u}{m + u(1-u)} = 20 \cdot \frac{10 \times 0.4}{10 + 0.4 \times 0.6}$$
$$= 20 \cdot \frac{4}{10.24} = 20 \times 0.3906 = \mathbf{7.813 \text{ V}}$$

Open-circuit (ideal) output: $V_{out} = V_s \cdot u = 20 \times 0.4 = 8.0$ V.

**(ii) Loading error (2 marks):**
$$\varepsilon_L = 8.0 - 7.813 = \mathbf{0.187 \text{ V}}$$
As a percentage of full scale ($V_s = 20$ V):
$$\varepsilon_L\,\% = \frac{0.187}{20} \times 100 = \mathbf{0.94\,\%} \text{ of FS}$$

**(iii) Maximum loading error (2 marks):**
At $u = 0.5$:
$$V_L(0.5) = 20 \cdot \frac{10 \times 0.5}{10 + 0.25} = 20 \cdot \frac{5}{10.25} = 20 \times 0.4878 = 9.756 \text{ V}$$
$$\varepsilon_{L,max} = 10 - 9.756 = \mathbf{0.244 \text{ V} = 1.22\,\%} \text{ of FS}$$

(Using the approximation $\varepsilon_{L,max} \approx V_s/(8m) = 20/80 = 0.25$ V ≈ 1.25 % — close enough to verify.)

---

### Question B (B). `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Describe the **construction and working of a rotary (angular) potentiometric transducer**. Explain how it is used as a **pressure transducer** in combination with a Bourdon tube. Derive the expression for its **angular sensitivity**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rotary potentiometer is used in a pressure gauge. The pot has $\theta_{max} = 270°$, $V_s = 12$ V, $R_{total} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, and is connected to a voltmeter of input impedance **200 kΩ**.
  (i) Calculate the **angular sensitivity** in V/° and V/rad. **(2 marks)**
  (ii) For an applied pressure of **6 bar**, the wiper rotates through **135°**. Find the **voltmeter reading** (loaded output). **(3 marks)**
  (iii) Calculate the **loading error at this position** as a percentage of full scale. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A rotary potentiometric transducer has a **circular (arc-shaped) resistive track** — either wire-wound on a circular mandrel or a film deposited on a curved ceramic substrate. A **rotating shaft** carries the **wiper (brush)**, which slides along the track as the shaft turns. Two **fixed end terminals** are at the start and end of the track, and a **third wiper terminal** provides the output. The shaft is supported by precision bearings within a sealed housing.

**Working (1 mark):**
DC excitation $V_s$ is applied across the two fixed terminals. As the shaft rotates by angle $\theta$, the wiper taps a fractional resistance $R_x = R_{total} \cdot \theta / \theta_{max}$, producing:
$$V_{out} = V_s \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$$

**Use as pressure transducer (Bourdon tube) (2 marks):**
A **Bourdon tube** is a curved metal tube that tends to **straighten** when internal pressure increases. The free end of the Bourdon tube is mechanically linked (via a lever or gear train) to the shaft of the rotary pot. As pressure rises:
- The Bourdon tube tip **displaces**,
- The linkage **rotates** the pot shaft by an angle $\theta$ proportional to pressure,
- The pot produces an **output voltage proportional to pressure**.

The pressure sensitivity is $K_P = V_s / P_{max}$ (V/bar), and the system gives a direct, readable, electrical pressure signal suitable for display, recording, or control.

**Angular sensitivity derivation (2 marks):**
$$V_{out}(\theta) = V_s \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}} \implies K_\theta = \frac{dV_{out}}{d\theta} = \frac{V_s}{\theta_{max}}$$
In V/rad if $\theta_{max}$ is in radians, and in V/° if $\theta_{max}$ is in degrees.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $\theta_{max} = 270°$, $V_s = 12$ V, $R_{total} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 200 \text{ k}\Omega$, $\theta = 135°$.

**(i) Angular sensitivity (2 marks):**
$$K_\theta = \frac{V_s}{\theta_{max}} = \frac{12 \text{ V}}{270°} = \mathbf{0.0444 \text{ V/°}}$$
In radians: $\theta_{max} = 270° \times \pi/180 = 4.712$ rad, so:
$$K_\theta = \frac{12}{4.712} = \mathbf{2.546 \text{ V/rad}}$$

**(ii) Voltmeter reading (loaded output) at $\theta = 135°$ (3 marks):**
Fractional rotation: $u = 135/270 = 0.5$.
Normalized load: $m = R_L/R_{total} = 200/2 = 100$.

Loaded output:
$$V_L = V_s \cdot \frac{m \cdot u}{m + u(1-u)} = 12 \cdot \frac{100 \times 0.5}{100 + 0.5 \times 0.5}$$
$$= 12 \cdot \frac{50}{100.25} = 12 \times 0.49875 = \mathbf{5.985 \text{ V}}$$

**(iii) Loading error as % of full scale (2 marks):**
Open-circuit output: $V_{out} = V_s \cdot u = 12 \times 0.5 = 6.0$ V.
Loading error: $\varepsilon_L = 6.0 - 5.985 = 0.015$ V.
$$\varepsilon_L\,\% = \frac{0.015}{12} \times 100 = \mathbf{0.125\,\%} \text{ of FS}$$

(Consistent with the design rule $1/(8m) = 1/800 = 0.125\%$.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the loaded vs. open-circuit distinction:** Many students write $V_{out} = V_s \cdot x/L$ and stop, even when $R_L$ is given. Always check whether the problem specifies a load resistance — if it does, use the **full loaded formula**.
> 2. **Sign error in loading error:** Loading error is $V_{out} - V_L$ (ideal minus actual), which is **always positive** because $V_{out} \geq V_L$ for a passive load.
> 3. **Unit confusion:** Sensitivity in V/mm vs. V/m vs. V/° vs. V/rad — **always match units** in numerator and denominator.
> 4. **Reporting percent loading error as "of reading" instead of "of full scale":** KTU expects "% of FS" — using the formula $\varepsilon_L / V_s \times 100\%$.
> 5. **Forgetting the resolution statement for wire-wound pots:** When the question says "wire-wound", you **must** state that resolution is finite ($\delta = 1/N$) and explain the consequence.
> 6. **Confusing transducer with rheostat:** A **potentiometer** uses **three terminals** (voltage divider); a **rheostat** uses **two terminals** (variable resistor). The transducer action requires the three-terminal configuration.
> 7. **Not mentioning the need for high-impedance readout:** Examiners specifically look for the phrase "the readout device must have an input impedance much greater than $R_{total}$" to avoid loading error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Potentiometric transducer** = **passive** displacement-to-voltage converter using a **voltage divider** with a sliding wiper.
- Two main configurations: **Linear (straight track)** and **Rotary (circular track, single-turn or multi-turn)**.
- **Open-circuit output:** $V_{out} = V_s \cdot (x/L)$ for linear, $V_{out} = V_s \cdot (\theta/\theta_{max})$ for rotary.
- **Sensitivities:** $K_L = V_s/L$ (V/m or V/mm); $K_\theta = V_s/\theta_{max}$ (V/rad or V/°).
- **Loading error formula (loaded output):** $V_L = V_s \cdot \dfrac{m \cdot u}{m + u(1-u)}$, where $m = R_L/R_{total}$ and $u = x/L$.
- **Maximum loading error** occurs at **mid-stroke ($u = 0.5$)** and equals $\approx V_s/(8m) = V_s \cdot R_{total}/(8 R_L)$.
- **Design rule:** For loading error $\leq 1\%$, use $R_L \geq 12.5 \, R_{total}$. For $\leq 0.1\%$, use $R_L \geq 125 \, R_{total}$.
- **Resolution:** Wire-wound pots have $\delta = 1/N$; film pots have effectively infinite resolution.
- **Always** use a **high-input-impedance** readout (voltmeter, ADC, op-amp buffer) to minimize loading error.
- **Wire-wound** = discrete steps, high power, robust; **Carbon/Cermet/Conductive-plastic film** = smooth, infinite resolution, lower power, longer life in dynamic applications.
- **Industrial applications:** valve position, level (float), pressure (Bourdon), throttle/pedal position, robotic joint angle, joystick.
- **Advantages:** simple, cheap, high output amplitude, no excitation electronics at sensor, bidirectional.
- **Limitations:** contact wear, friction loading, finite resolution (wire-wound), temperature drift, loading error, not for very high-speed dynamic measurements.
- **Multi-turn helical pots** improve resolution by a factor equal to the number of turns (e.g., 10-turn pot = 10× finer).
- **For 14-mark KTU questions:** Always draw a **neat diagram**, state the **output equation**, derive the **sensitivity**, and address **loading error** if any load resistance is mentioned.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT865 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Piezoelectric Effect** (derived from the Greek word *piezein*, meaning "to press" or "to squeeze") is the fundamental electromechanical phenomenon exhibited by certain anisotropic, non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials wherein an applied **mechanical stress** directly produces a proportional **electrical charge** (or voltage) across the crystal faces. This is termed the **Direct Piezoelectric Effect**, formally discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie in **1880**.

The converse phenomenon, where an applied **electric field** induces a proportional **mechanical strain** (deformation) in the crystal, is called the **Inverse (or Converse) Piezoelectric Effect**, predicted by Lippmann in 1881 and verified by the Curies the same year.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The piezoelectric transducer is classified as an **active transducer** (or self-generating transducer) because it does *not* require an external excitation voltage/power supply to generate an output signal. The mechanical energy itself is directly converted into electrical energy, unlike resistive/capacitive/inductive passive transducers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

**Plain-English Analogy — "The Crystal Sponge":**

Imagine a special, slightly stretchy "sponge" made of crystal. When you press this sponge with your finger (apply mechanical force), it doesn't just squash — it simultaneously pushes tiny electrical charges out of its two flat faces, like squeezing water out of a real sponge. The harder you press, the more charges appear on the faces.

Now flip the analogy: if you connect a small battery to the two flat faces of this crystal sponge, the sponge physically *stretches* or *contracts* on its own. You have just converted an electrical signal into a precise mechanical movement.

**Real-world examples of piezoelectric materials:**
- **Natural Crystals:** Quartz (SiO₂), Rochelle Salt, Tourmaline
- **Synthetic Ceramics (most widely used):** PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate), Barium Titanate (BaTiO₃)
- **Polymers:** PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride)

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Property:** A crystal must be **non-centrosymmetric** (lacking a center of symmetry) to exhibit the piezoelectric effect. Cubic crystals like NaCl cannot be piezoelectric because their symmetric structure cancels out the induced charges. This is a frequent KTU short-answer question.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
