This amplifies the readable displacement, enabling accurate measurement of low pressures. **Functional diagram:** ``` ___________ | | <- Inclined tube | \ | (angle theta to horizontal) | \ | | \ | | \ | Reads L (along incline) | \ | |__________\|___________ \ <- Sealing/Manometric Fluid Interface \ \ Reservoir (well) of area A_w \____________________________ | | <- Pressure P1 (high side) |____________________________ P2 (low side) at the other end of the inclined tube ``` **Part (b): Calculation (7 marks)** **Step 1:** Apply the inclined manometer equation: $$\Delta P = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot L \cdot \sin(\theta)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute values: $$\Delta P = 850 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.120 \cdot \sin(20°)$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate: $$\sin(20°) \approx 0.3420$$ $$\Delta P = 850 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.120 \cdot 0.3420$$ $$\Delta P = 850 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.04104$$ $$\Delta P = 850 \cdot 0.4027 \approx 342.3 \text{ Pa}$$ **Final Answer:** $\Delta P \approx 342.3 \text{ Pa}$ (or $\approx 34.9 \text{ mmWC}$). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully!** > > 1. **Forgetting unit conversions:** $h$ must be in **meters**, not mm or cm, when using SI units. Converting only at the end is dangerous; convert at the input stage. > 2. **Using $\rho_f$ alone in liquid-on-liquid U-tubes:** Always subtract $\rho_s$ when both limbs contain a process liquid (like water). This costs **2–3 marks easily**. > 3. **Forgetting $\sin(\theta)$ in inclined-manometer problems:** Many students write $\Delta P = \rho_f g L$ and lose the entire 7-mark calculation. > 4. **Ignoring reservoir correction in well-type manometers:** When $A_w$ is comparable to $A_t$, the $A_t/A_w$ term must be included. > 5. **Sign convention:** $P_1 - P_2$ is **positive** when $P_1$ is the high side. State this explicitly in derivations. > 6. **Using $g = 10$ instead of $9.81$:** KTU prefers $9.81$. Use $9.81$ unless the problem states otherwise. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **U-Tube Manometer:** Standard equation is $\Delta P = \rho_f g h$; use $(\rho_f - \rho_s)$ when liquid-on-liquid. - [ ] **Inclined Manometer:** Use $\Delta P = \rho_f g L \sin(\theta)$; magnification factor is $K = 1/\sin(\theta)$. - [ ] **Ring-Balanced Manometer:** Self-balancing via gravity counter-torque; output is **linear in angle $\phi$**. - [ ] **Units:** $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $h$ in m, giving $\Delta P$ in Pa. - [ ] **Conversion:** $1 \text{ mmWC} \approx 9.81 \text{ Pa}$; $1 \text{ atm} = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$. - [ ] **Continuity principle:** Volume displaced from one limb = volume received in the other (uniform U-tube: $x = y$). - [ ] **Well-type correction:** True height $h = L[\sin\theta + A_t/A_w]$; often approximated for large wells. - [ ] **Low-pressure measurement:** Always choose inclined manometer with low-density fluid (oil). - [ ] **Continuous measurement / signal output:** Choose ring-balanced manometer (or DP transmitter). - [ ] **Standard calibration:** Lab U-tube manometers using mercury serve as **primary standards** for pressure calibration. - [ ] **Exam trick:** Always identify whether the problem is **gas-on-mercury** (use $\rho_f g h$) or **liquid-on-mercury** (use $(\rho_f - \rho_s) g h$). - [ ] **Sketch before you solve:** KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** for a clean functional diagram in long-answer questions. ---

Level measurement Bubblers-Capacitance level sensors-Level switches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT865 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Level measurement Bubblers-Capacitance level sensors-Level switches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — LEVEL MEASUREMENT: BUBBLERS, CAPACITANCE SENSORS & LEVEL SWITCHES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Bubbler System (Purge-Type Level Measurement)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A *bubbler* (also called a **purge-type level sensor** or **hydrostatic bubbler system**) is a non-contact level measurement device that infers the liquid level inside a vessel by measuring the **back-pressure** required to maintain a constant, slow flow of gas (typically air or nitrogen) through a **dip tube** submerged in the liquid.

**Working Principle (Intuitive Analogy):**
Imagine you are drinking a beverage through a straw submerged deep in a glass. To push air through the straw, you must blow harder when the straw is dipped *deeper* into the liquid. The pressure you need to blow is exactly equal to the weight of the liquid column sitting above the straw's outlet. The bubbler system uses this exact physical principle.

- The **dip tube** is the "straw."
- The **regulated gas supply** is the "blowing action."
- The **back-pressure measured at the top of the tube** is the "blowing effort."
- The **liquid level** is what we want to find.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The system measures **hydrostatic head pressure** using the fundamental relationship:
> $$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$
> where **P = 9.81 kPa per meter of water column (≈ 9.81 kPa/m H₂O)**, ρ = liquid density, g = 9.81 m/s², and h = liquid height above the tube outlet.

---

### 1.2 Capacitance Level Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A *capacitance level sensor* is an electronic level measuring device that operates on the principle of variation of **electrical capacitance** between two conducting elements (a probe and a reference—usually the tank wall) as the liquid level changes the dielectric medium between them.

**Working Principle (Intuitive Analogy):**
Think of a capacitor as a "sandwich" of two metal plates separated by an insulating material (a *dielectric*). Now, imagine slowly filling the gap with water instead of air. Water has a much higher dielectric constant (εᵣ ≈ 80) than air (εᵣ ≈ 1.0006). So the capacitor's ability to store charge (its *capacitance*) increases dramatically. The capacitance level sensor simply *watches* this change and translates it into a liquid height.

There are two engineering variants:
- **Non-conductive liquids (oil, solvents, deionized water):** The liquid acts as the *dielectric*. Capacitance increases with rising level.
- **Conductive liquids (water, acids, molten metals):** The liquid itself acts as one of the capacitor plates. The probe must be **insulated with a non-conductive coating** (PTFE, PVDF, etc.).

---

### 1.3 Level Switches (Point-Level Devices)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A *level switch* is a binary (ON/OFF) instrument that detects whether a liquid or solid (bulk material) has reached a **specific preset level** in a vessel. Unlike continuous transmitters, it does not provide a proportional reading — it simply gives an alarm, trip, or pump-control signal when the material reaches the actuation point.

**Working Principle (Intuitive Analogy):**
A level switch is like a **limit switch on a door** — it does not tell you *how far* the door is open, only whether it has crossed a particular position. In industry, level switches are used to say "Tank is FULL — stop filling" or "Tank is EMPTY — start pump."

> [!TIP]
> Common KTU-mandated types are:
> 1. **Float switch** (mechanical buoyancy)
> 2. **Conductivity / Resistive level switch**
> 3. **Capacitance level switch** (point version of capacitance sensor)
> 4. **Vibrating / Tuning-fork level switch**
> 5. **Optical level switch**
> 6. **Diaphragm / Paddle-wheel switch**

---

### 1.4 Universal Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Industry Use |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity, **g** | **9.81 m/s²** | All hydrostatic calculations |
| Density of water, **ρ_w** | **1000 kg/m³** at 4 °C | Reference for level spans |
| Dielectric constant of air, **εᵣ,air** | **1.0006** | Reference for capacitance baseline |
| Dielectric constant of water, **εᵣ,water** | **≈ 80** | Conductive vs. non-conductive cases |
| Purge gas flow (typical) | **1–5 L/min** | Bubbler low-flow rotameter setting |
| Standard bubble rate | **1 bubble per second** (visual indicator) | Field commissioning |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bubbler System — Operating Theory

A bubbler system consists of these functional blocks:

1. **Air/N₂ supply** (filtered, regulated, dry) — typically from instrument air manifold at **1.4 bar (20 psi)**.
2. **Pressure regulator** — drops the supply pressure to a set purge pressure (slightly above the maximum expected hydrostatic head).
3. **Rotameter / flow indicator** — maintains a very low, constant purge flow (e.g., 1–5 L/min) so the gas column weight is negligible.
4. **Dip tube** — a vertical tube extending down into the liquid. Tube diameter is small (typically **6–12 mm**).
5. **Differential pressure (DP) transmitter** — measures the back-pressure at the top of the dip tube.

**Physical logic — step by step:**

- Step 1: Gas flows into the dip tube at a very low, metered rate.
- Step 2: Inside the tube, a small column of gas pushes against the liquid at the outlet.
- Step 3: For gas to *escape* as bubbles, the **internal gas pressure must exactly balance the hydrostatic head** of liquid above the tube's outlet point.
- Step 4: This balancing pressure is measured by the DP cell at the top of the tube.
- Step 5: Using the hydrostatic equation, the height of liquid is recovered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a constant low flow is mandatory:** If flow is too high, gas column *friction* and *dynamic pressure* add error. If flow is too low, the bubbling may be intermittent and unstable. The "low-but-positive" rule ensures the gas column is essentially static and the measured pressure equals the hydrostatic head.

---

### 2.2 Capacitance Level Sensor — Operating Theory

For a **cylindrical coaxial probe** (rod inside a concentric tube or tank wall), the capacitance is:

$$C = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r L}{\ln(D/d)}$$

where:
- **L** = length of probe immersed in the active dielectric
- **D** = outer electrode diameter
- **d** = inner electrode diameter
- **ε₀** = permittivity of free space = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- **εᵣ** = relative permittivity of the dielectric

For a **rod-in-tank** geometry (using the tank wall as the second electrode), the formula is similar but uses an effective diameter. When the liquid rises, the air is replaced by liquid (or the liquid is sensed via its conductivity through the insulation):

**Case A — Non-conductive liquid:**
The capacitance has two regions:
- **Air portion (dry):** $C_{air} = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0(1) L_{air}}{\ln(D/d)}$
- **Liquid portion (wet):** $C_{liq} = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r L_{liq}}{\ln(D/d)}$

Total: $C = C_{air} + C_{liq}$. This is a linear function of level.

**Case B — Conductive liquid (insulated probe):**
The probe has a thin dielectric sheath (εᵣ,sheath). The capacitance depends on the *thickness of the insulation* and the **effective area covered by liquid**:
$$C = C_0 + K \cdot h$$
where **C₀** is the empty-tank (air) capacitance, and **K** is the calibration constant.

---

### 2.3 Level Switches — Operating Theory

| Switch Type | Sensing Element | Actuation Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **Float switch** | Buoyant float with magnetic / mercury / reed contact | Float lifts/lowers with level past pivot |
| **Conductivity switch** | Two electrodes + low-voltage AC source | Liquid completes the circuit (only for conductive liquids) |
| **Capacitance switch** | Single probe + reference (tank wall) | Capacitance crosses user-set threshold |
| **Vibrating-fork switch** | Two piezo-driven tuning forks | Fork frequency drops when liquid/solid dampens vibration |
| **Optical switch** | LED + photodetector in a prism | Light refracts differently when liquid touches the prism |
| **Diaphragm switch** | Snap-action disc pressed by hydrostatic head | Pressure deflection triggers microswitch |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hysteresis** is a critical design parameter. A level switch must have a small "dead-band" between the ON and OFF points to prevent *chattering* (rapid on-off cycling) when the surface ripples or waves.

---

### 2.4 🔥 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic head pressure | Pa (or kPa) |
| 2 | $h = P / (\rho g)$ | Level from measured pressure | m |
| 3 | $\Delta P = \rho g \Delta h$ | Differential head for two taps | Pa |
| 4 | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r L}{\ln(D/d)}$ | Cylindrical capacitance | F (farads) |
| 5 | $C = C_0 + K h$ | Linearised capacitance probe (conductive) | F |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_r = \varepsilon / \varepsilon_0$ | Definition of relative permittivity | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_{total} = C_{air} + C_{liq}$ | Two-segment capacitance (non-conductive) | F |
| 8 | $F_b = \rho_{liq} g V_{float}$ | Buoyancy force on float | N |
| 9 | $f_{fork} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency of tuning fork (design eqn.) | Hz |
| 10 | $I = V / R_{liquid}$ | Conductivity switch current | A |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, note that for **water**: **1 m of water column ≈ 9.81 kPa ≈ 0.0981 bar ≈ 1.422 psi**. This is the most common unit-conversion mistake in KTU papers.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bubblers** dominate in **wastewater, corrosive chemicals, and slurries** where wetted electronics would fail. Common in *sewage lift stations* and *open-channel flow measurement*.
- **Capacitance sensors** are the workhorse of the **process industries** — fuels, oils, acids, plastics, food & beverages, cement, and pharmaceuticals — because they have **no moving parts** and can detect *interface levels* (e.g., oil on water).
- **Level switches** protect every **pump, heater, and reactor** in the plant. They are the *safety-critical* devices, triggering **high-level alarms, low-level trips, and pump-control interlocks**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Head Equation for Bubbler

**Starting principle:** For a static fluid, the pressure at a depth *h* below the free surface equals the weight per unit area of the fluid column above that point.

**Step 1:** Consider an elemental horizontal disc of cross-sectional area *A* and thickness *dh* at depth *h*.
Mass of element: $dm = \rho \cdot A \cdot dh$

**Step 2:** Weight of element (force pulling down): $dW = dm \cdot g = \rho g A \, dh$

**Step 3:** This weight is supported by pressure *P* acting over area *A*:
$dP \cdot A = \rho g A \, dh$

**Step 4:** Cancel area *A* and integrate from surface (P = P_atm, h = 0) to depth h (P = P_h):
$\int_{P_{atm}}^{P_h} dP = \int_0^h \rho g \, dh$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integral:
$$P_h - P_{atm} = \rho g h$$

**Step 6:** Define **gauge pressure** (P_gauge = P_h − P_atm):
$$\boxed{P_{gauge} = \rho g h}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for level h:
$$h = \dfrac{P_{gauge}}{\rho g}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The bubbler DP transmitter reads **gauge pressure** at the top of the dip tube. This reading, divided by (ρg), directly gives the level *h* above the tube outlet. If the dip tube outlet is mounted at the *bottom* of the tank, *h* equals total liquid level.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capacitance Variation with Level (Non-Conductive Liquid)

**Step 1:** Define the geometry: rod of diameter *d* centred in a concentric cylinder of diameter *D*. Total length *L_total*. The portion *h* is submerged.

**Step 2:** Length of dry (air) portion:
$$L_{air} = L_{total} - h$$

**Step 3:** Capacitance of the dry portion (εᵣ = 1 for air):
$$C_{air} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 (1) (L_{total} - h)}{\ln(D/d)}$$

**Step 4:** Capacitance of the wet portion (εᵣ = ε_liq):
$$C_{liq} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r h}{\ln(D/d)}$$

**Step 5:** Because the two portions act as capacitors *in parallel* (sharing the same pair of electrodes, side-by-side along the probe), add them:
$$C_{total} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(D/d)} \left[ (L_{total} - h) + \varepsilon_r h \right]$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange into an explicit function of *h*:
$$C_{total} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 L_{total}}{\ln(D/d)} + \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1)}{\ln(D/d)} \cdot h$$

**Step 7:** Recognise the linear form $C = C_0 + K h$:
$$\boxed{C_0 = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 L_{total}}{\ln(D/d)}, \quad K = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1)}{\ln(D/d)}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
Given: Probe length $L_{total} = 1.5$ m, inner diameter $d = 10$ mm, outer diameter $D = 40$ mm, liquid εᵣ = 2.5 (oil).

Calculate constants:
- $\ln(D/d) = \ln(40/10) = \ln(4) = 1.3863$
- $C_0 = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1.5}{1.3863} = \dfrac{8.342 \times 10^{-11}}{1.3863} = 6.018 \times 10^{-11}$ F = **60.18 pF**
- $K = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot (2.5 - 1)}{1.3863} = \dfrac{8.342 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 1.5}{1.3863} = 9.025 \times 10^{-11}$ F/m = **90.25 pF/m**

**For h = 0.8 m:**
$$C_{total} = 60.18 + 90.25 \times 0.8 = 60.18 + 72.20 = 132.38 \text{ pF}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Buoyancy Force on a Float Switch

**Step 1:** A float of volume *V_float* is fully submerged in liquid of density *ρ_liq*.

**Step 2:** By Archimedes' principle, the buoyant force equals the weight of displaced liquid:
$$F_b = \rho_{liq} \cdot g \cdot V_{float}$$

**Step 3:** The float's own weight is $W = \rho_{float} \cdot g \cdot V_{float}$.

**Step 4:** For the float to *float* (rise with the level), buoyancy must exceed weight:
$$\rho_{liq} \cdot g \cdot V_{float} > \rho_{float} \cdot g \cdot V_{float}$$
$$\rho_{liq} > \rho_{float}$$

**Step 5:** When level drops below the float, the float is no longer fully supported — it falls, tipping the switch. The actuation point is geometric, set by the float's *pivot height* and *stem length*.

**Numerical Example:**
Float volume = 50 cm³ = $5 \times 10^{-5}$ m³, liquid ρ = 1200 kg/m³.
$$F_b = 1200 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-5} = 0.5886 \text{ N}$$
This is the *net upward force* available to actuate the magnetic reed switch.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Selection Guide)

| Feature | Bubbler | Capacitance Sensor | Float Switch | Conductivity Switch |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output | Continuous (4–20 mA) | Continuous (4–20 mA) | Discrete (relay) | Discrete (relay) |
| Wetted parts | Tube only | Probe (insulated) | Float | Electrodes |
| Corrosive liquids | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Good (with coating) | ⚠ Limited | ⚠ Limited |
| Conductive liquids required? | No | No (works with both) | No | **Yes** |
| Foam / vapor issues | ⚠ May error | ⚠ Foam changes εᵣ | ⚠ May float on foam | ❌ Conducts through foam |
| Maintenance | Low (filter air) | Very low | Medium (mechanical) | Medium (electrode fouling) |
| Typical cost | Medium | Medium | Low | Low |
| Hazardous area suitability | ✅ Pneumatic-safe | ✅ Intrinsically safe versions | ✅ Pneumatic-safe | ⚠ Limited |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bubbler System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Instrument Air Supply 1.4 bar] --> A2[Filter Regulator]
    A2 --> A3[Rotameter Low Flow 1 to 5 L per min]
    A3 --> A4[Dip Tube Outlet at Tank Bottom]
    A4 --> A5[Liquid Level h meters]
    A3 --> A6[DP Transmitter Measures Back Pressure]
    A6 --> A7[Signal Conditioner 4 to 20 mA]
    A7 --> A8[PLC DCS Indicator]
    A5 -. hydrostatic head P equals rho g h .-> A6
```

> [!TIP]
> The dotted feedback arrow shows the **physical link** between level *h* and the back-pressure reading — this is the heart of the bubbler principle.

---

### 4.2 Capacitance Level Sensor — Sensing Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NonConductive["Case A Non Conductive Liquid Oil Solvent"]
        NA[Rod Probe]
        NB[Tank Wall acts as Second Plate]
        NA --- NB
        NC[Liquid is the Dielectric]
        NC -. replaces air .-> NA
    end
    subgraph Conductive["Case B Conductive Liquid Water Acid"]
        CA[Insulated Rod Probe PTFE Coating]
        CB[Tank Wall Liquid acts as Second Plate]
        CA --- CB
        CC[Dielectric is the Insulation Layer]
        CC -. wraps .-> CA
    end
```

---

### 4.3 Level Switch — Decision Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Process Liquid Reaches Probe Location] --> S2{Switch Type}
    S2 -- Float --> F1[Buoyancy Lifts Float]
    S2 -- Conductivity --> F2[Liquid Completes Electrode Circuit]
    S2 -- Capacitance --> F3[Capacitance Crosses Setpoint]
    S2 -- Vibrating Fork --> F4[Fork Frequency Drops Below Threshold]
    F1 --> O1[Reed Contact Closes]
    F2 --> O2[Relay Coil Energises]
    F3 --> O3[Electronics Trip Relay]
    F4 --> O4[Piezo Detector Senses Damping]
    O1 --> X[Discrete Output NO NC COM]
    O2 --> X
    O3 --> X
    O4 --> X
    X --> A[High or Low Level Alarm Pump Trip Interlock]
```

---

### 4.4 Typical Mounting Diagram (Bubbler)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1[Tank Free Surface at height H] --> T2[Liquid Column of height H]
    T2 --> T3[Dip Tube Outlet at depth D below surface]
    T3 --> T4[Gas Pressure equals rho g D]
    T4 --> T5[Measured at DP Cell mounted above tank]
    T5 --> T6[Transmitter Computes Level equals D plus stand off height]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three advantages of a bubbler-type level measurement system over a direct-contact hydrostatic pressure sensor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The dip tube is the only wetted part — ideal for **corrosive, slurry, or sewage** applications (1 mark).
2. No electrical components are in contact with the process — safe in **hazardous/explosive** areas (1 mark).
3. Can be installed in **open vessels** without modification — only needs a small dip tube entry (1 mark).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "dielectric constant" and state its value for air and water at 25 °C.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The dielectric constant (relative permittivity, εᵣ) is the ratio of the permittivity of a material to the permittivity of free space, ε/ε₀. It indicates a material's ability to store electrical energy in an electric field (1 mark).
- For **air**: εᵣ ≈ **1.0006** (1 mark).
- For **water**: εᵣ ≈ **78–80** (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 3]**

> **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a **bubbler-type level measurement system**. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A bubbler system is used in a tank of liquid with density **1200 kg/m³**. The dip tube outlet is **0.5 m above the tank bottom**. The DP transmitter reads a gauge pressure of **14.715 kPa**. Find the **liquid level** above the tank bottom. Take g = 9.81 m/s². **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle (7 marks):**
- A regulated, low flow of clean gas (air/N₂) is fed into a dip tube immersed in the liquid (1 mark).
- Gas escapes as bubbles at the tube's outlet. The pressure required to push the gas out equals the hydrostatic pressure of the liquid above the outlet (2 marks).
- This back-pressure is sensed by a differential pressure transmitter mounted at the top of the tube (1 mark).
- The output is linear with liquid level: $P = \rho g h$ (1 mark).
- **Advantages:** No moving parts in the tank, no electrical wetted parts, ideal for corrosive/abrasive liquids, suitable for open tanks (1 mark).
- **Limitations:** Requires clean dry gas supply, not suitable for liquids that foam heavily, accuracy depends on density constancy (1 mark).

*(Neat sketch: same as Section 4.1 — Bubbler block diagram is acceptable.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
- Given: ρ = 1200 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s², P_gauge = 14.715 kPa = 14715 Pa, stand-off h₀ = 0.5 m.
- Hydrostatic head above outlet: $h = P/(\rho g)$

$$h = \frac{14715}{1200 \times 9.81} = \frac{14715}{11772} = 1.25 \text{ m}$$

- Total level above tank bottom: $H = h + h_0 = 1.25 + 0.5 = 1.75$ m (2 marks for this step).
- **Answer: Liquid level = 1.75 m above tank bottom** (1 mark for units, 1 mark for the boxed answer).

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3 Alternative]**

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **capacitance-type level sensor** for both **conductive** and **non-conductive** liquids. Compare the two cases. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cylindrical capacitance probe has an inner rod of diameter **8 mm** and a concentric outer sheath of diameter **32 mm**. The active length is **2 m**. The tank contains an oil of εᵣ = **2.4**. Calculate the capacitance when the oil level is **1.2 m** and **0 m** (empty). Take ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**
- A capacitance probe uses the liquid (and/or air) as the dielectric between two electrodes. As level changes, capacitance changes linearly (1 mark).
- **Non-conductive case (oil, solvents):** The liquid is the dielectric. Probe and tank wall are the electrodes. Capacitance increases as level rises. C = C_air + C_liq. Both segments are in parallel (2 marks).
- **Conductive case (water, acids):** The liquid *conducts*, so it acts as the second plate. The probe must be **insulated** (PTFE sheath). The dielectric becomes the insulation layer. Capacitance changes with the *wetted area* of the probe (2 marks).
- **Comparison:** Non-conductive is simpler (no insulation needed) but offers small ΔC for low-ε liquids. Conductive gives larger signals but requires a coated probe (1 mark).
- **Sketch:** See Section 4.2 (1 mark).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
- Given: d = 8 mm = 0.008 m, D = 32 mm = 0.032 m, L_total = 2 m, εᵣ,oil = 2.4, ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m.
- Geometric factor:
$$\ln(D/d) = \ln(32/8) = \ln(4) = 1.3863 \text{ (1 mark)}$$

- Constant C₀ (empty, air εᵣ = 1):
$$C_0 = \frac{2\pi \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1 \cdot 2}{1.3863} = \frac{1.1126 \times 10^{-10}}{1.3863} = 8.025 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F} = 80.25 \text{ pF (1 mark)}$$

- Linear coefficient K:
$$K = \frac{2\pi \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot (2.4 - 1)}{1.3863} = \frac{7.7879 \times 10^{-11}}{1.3863} = 5.618 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F/m} = 56.18 \text{ pF/m (1 mark)}$$

- **Case h = 1.2 m (oil level):**
$$C_{1.2} = 80.25 + 56.18 \times 1.2 = 80.25 + 67.42 = 147.67 \text{ pF (2 marks)}$$

- **Case h = 0 m (empty):**
$$C_{0} = 80.25 + 0 = 80.25 \text{ pF (1 mark)}$$

- **Result: C_empty = 80.25 pF, C_filled(1.2 m) = 147.67 pF** (1 mark for boxed answer with units).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalise heavily:**
> 1. **Unit blunders:** Writing 9.81 kPa/m for water column but forgetting to **multiply by density** for non-water liquids (oil, acid, etc.). The factor is **ρ × g**, not just g.
> 2. **Confusing gauge vs. absolute pressure** in bubbler problems — always use **gauge pressure** in $P = \rho g h$.
> 3. **Forgetting stand-off height (h₀)** in bubbler problems where the dip tube outlet is raised above the tank floor.
> 4. **Insulation requirement missed:** A bare rod in conductive liquid will *short-circuit* the capacitor — students lose 2 marks if they don't mention the **PTFE/PVDF sheath** for conductive liquids.
> 5. **Forgetting to use ln(D/d) in cylindrical capacitance** — many students write the parallel-plate formula and lose 3–4 marks.
> 6. **Level switches are discrete, not continuous** — describing them as "transmitters" is an instant 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- **Bubblers** = hydrostatic head measurement via gas-purge back-pressure. Equation: $P = \rho g h$. Only the dip tube touches the process.
- **Purge flow** must be **low and constant** (1–5 L/min) — too high adds dynamic error, too low loses bubbling stability.
- **Capacitance sensors** rely on **change in dielectric** (non-conductive) or **change in effective electrode area** (conductive).
- **Conductive liquids need an insulated probe**; non-conductive liquids use a bare probe.
- Cylindrical capacitance formula: $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r L}{\ln(D/d)}$ — note the **natural logarithm**, not log₁₀.
- Capacitance vs. level is **linear** in both cases (after calibration): $C = C_0 + K h$.
- **Level switches** are **binary** devices for alarms and trips; they do not give continuous readings.
- **Hysteresis (dead-band)** is mandatory to prevent relay *chattering*.
- **Common switches:** Float, Conductivity, Capacitance, Vibrating fork, Optical, Paddle-wheel.
- **Vibrating fork** senses **damping** of piezo-driven resonance when material touches the fork tines.
- **Conductivity switch** works **only with conductive liquids** (e.g., water) — fails with hydrocarbons.
- **Safety note:** Bubblers and pneumatic float switches are inherently safe in **hazardous/explosive** areas; capacitance and optical switches require **intrinsic safety (IS)** or **flameproof (Ex d)** certification.
- **Water column conversion to remember:** **1 m H₂O ≈ 9.81 kPa ≈ 0.0981 bar ≈ 1.422 psi**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Module 3: Level Measurement Instrumentation — Comprehensive Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Level Measurement

> [!NOTE]
> **Level Measurement** is the determination of the position of the surface (interface) of a liquid, solid, or multiphase fluid within a vessel (tank, silo, reactor) relative to a fixed reference point. It is one of the most critical measurements in process industries — petrochemical, pharmaceutical, water treatment, food & beverage, power, and nuclear plants.

Level measurement directly influences **inventory control, safety interlocks (overflow prevention), mass balance calculations, and regulatory compliance**. KTU 2024 Scheme classifies level sensors broadly by physical principle:

| Category | Operating Principle | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Point (Discrete) Level** | Detects presence/absence at a set point | Field-effect, Diaphragm, Float switch |
| **Continuous Level** | Provides proportional output over a range | DP transmitter, Displacer, Laser |
| **Visual / Gauging** | Direct sight or assisted reading | Flat glass, Magnetic, Reflex gauge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes the *electro-mechanical/optical* level devices that bridge simple switches with sophisticated continuous transmitters.

---

### 1.2 Field-Effect Switch (Capacitance Type Point Level Switch)

**Formal Definition:** A field-effect level switch is a **point (discrete) level detector** in which the approach of a conductive liquid (or granular solid) alters the **electric field between a probe electrode and the tank wall**, causing a measurable change in capacitance. An electronic oscillator/relay circuit detects this capacitance shift and switches a relay contact.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a coaxial wire: the inner conductor is the probe, the outer shield is the tank wall. The liquid/solid acts as the *dielectric* between them. When the material rises and "touches" the probe's electric field, it changes the effective dielectric constant — exactly like your finger altering the capacitance of a touch-screen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants & Standards:** Typical switch sensitivity: **1 pF** change; supply: **24 V DC**; switching output: **SPDT relay, 5 A @ 230 V AC**.

---

### 1.3 Diaphragm Switch

**Formal Definition:** A diaphragm switch is a **point level detector** consisting of a flexible metallic diaphragm that mechanically actuates a microswitch when the hydrostatic pressure of the rising liquid exceeds a calibrated threshold.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a wall-mounted soap dispenser — you push it, the diaphragm moves, the lever clicks. In a diaphragm level switch, the *liquid's weight (pressure)* pushes the diaphragm instead of your finger.

- **Operating Pressure Range:** up to **100 bar**
- **Switch Differential:** typically **± 12 mm** of level

---

### 1.4 Differential Pressure (D/P) Level Detector

**Formal Definition:** A differential pressure level detector infers liquid level by measuring the **differential hydrostatic pressure** between the bottom of the vessel and a reference point (usually the vapor space at the top).

**Intuitive Analogy:** Submerge a drinking straw into a glass of water, seal the top with your finger, and lift it — the water column height inside the straw equals the depth you submerged it. The taller the water in the glass, the higher the water stays inside the straw. A D/P transmitter does precisely the same thing electronically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** D/P transmitters are the **workhorse** of level measurement in refineries and chemical plants. They handle pressurized vessels, foaming liquids, and corrosive media when fitted with chemical seals.

---

### 1.5 Electronic D/P Transmitter

**Formal Definition:** An electronic D/P transmitter is a **two-wire, 4–20 mA current loop** instrument that senses the difference between two pressures (high-pressure tap at the tank bottom, low-pressure tap at the vapor space) and converts it into a proportional standardized signal.

**Key Specifications:**
- Accuracy: **± 0.075 %** of calibrated span
- Turndown ratio: **100 : 1**
- Output: **4–20 mA DC** with HART protocol
- Static pressure rating: up to **420 bar**

---

### 1.6 Displacer Level Detector & Torque Tube

**Formal Definition:** A displacer level detector measures level by the **Archimedes buoyancy principle**. A cylindrical displacer of fixed volume is partially submerged in the liquid; the buoyant force (which is proportional to submerged volume) acts on the displacer. This force is transmitted to a **torque tube** — a precision torsion shaft — whose angular twist (≈ ±4°) is converted into a pneumatic (3–15 psi) or electronic (4–20 mA) output signal.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Drop a cork into a bucket of water — it floats because water pushes it up. Now tie the cork to a string held by your hand. The harder you pull, the more submerged the cork becomes. The tension in the string tells you how deep the water is. The torque tube is your "string," and the displacer is the "cork."

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** A torque tube is *not* a spring. It is a **helically-wound torsion bar** that provides rotational stiffness without axial loading — this isolates pressure seals from the measured force.

---

### 1.7 Interface Measurement

**Formal Definition:** Interface measurement is the determination of the **boundary between two immiscible liquids of different densities** (e.g., oil over water, hydrocarbon over caustic) inside the same vessel.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Pour salad oil into a glass of water — the oil floats on top because it is lighter. The flat line between them is the *interface*. To find this line automatically, we use a buoyancy sensor (displacer) whose apparent weight changes sharply as it crosses the boundary, or a capacitance probe with two dielectric regions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Rule:** The displacer's specific gravity (S.G.) MUST lie between the S.G. of the heavy (lower) liquid and the light (upper) liquid. If S.G.displacer < S.G.light → it always floats. If S.G.displacer > S.G.heavy → it always sinks.

---

### 1.8 Float Level Devices & Float Level Switch

**Formal Definition:** A float level device uses a **buoyant float** (hollow metal/plastic sphere or cylinder) that rides on the liquid surface and mechanically actuates a switch, gauge pointer, or transmitter coupling.

**Sub-Types:**
1. **Float Level Switch** — discrete output (on/off), commonly used in sump pumps and high-level alarms.
2. **Float & Cable Transmitter** — continuous output; a counterweighted float pulls a perforated tape.
3. **Magnetic Float Switch** — float carries a permanent magnet that actuates a sealed reed switch.

**Intuitive Analogy:** A child's rubber duck floats on a bathtub — push it down, it pops back up. Now imagine the duck has a magnet on its belly and a reed switch on the tub wall; as the duck rises, the magnet triggers the switch. That is exactly how a magnetic float switch works.

---

### 1.9 Laser Level Detectors

**Formal Definition:** A laser level detector is a **non-contact, optical continuous level sensor** that uses a coherent laser beam to determine the distance from the sensor head to the target surface (liquid or solid).

**Two Principal Methods:**
1. **Triangulation Principle** — geometric distance calculation using the angle of the reflected beam.
2. **Time-of-Reflection (ToF / ToR) Principle** — direct timing of light pulse round-trip.

**Intuitive Analogy (Triangulation):** Hold a flashlight aimed at the floor, then tilt it 30°. The spot moves far away — the *farther* the floor, the *more* the spot moves for the same tilt. A laser triangulation sensor measures how much the spot moves.

**Intuitive Analogy (Time-of-Reflection):** Clap your hand in a canyon and listen for the echo. The longer the echo takes to come back, the farther the canyon wall. Light behaves the same way — except at **299,792,458 m/s**.

---

### 1.10 Level Gauges (Sight Glasses)

**Formal Definition:** Level gauges are **direct-reading instruments** that provide a visual indication of liquid level in a vessel, typically mounted externally via process connections.

**Sub-Types:**
1. **Flat Glass Gauge (Transparent & Reflex)** — glass sections installed in a steel frame with isolating valves.
2. **Magnetic Level Gauge** — non-magnetic chamber with a magnetic float that flips external visual indicators.
3. **Remote Reading Gauge** — same as a magnetic gauge but couples to a transmitter for distant readout.

**Intuitive Analogy:** A flat glass gauge is like a vertical window strip on a pressure cooker — you see the water level directly. A magnetic gauge is like a "lava lamp" tube — colored flaps flip as the float rises.

---

### 1.11 Optical Level Devices

**Formal Definition:** Optical level devices (fiber-optic & laser-based) use **changes in refractive index, light scattering, or fluorescence** to detect liquid level without any metallic parts in contact with the process fluid.

**Sub-Types:**
1. **Prism/Refractive Probe** — total internal reflection (TIR) changes at the liquid interface.
2. **Fiber-Optic Level Switch** — U-shaped probe; light escapes into the liquid when immersed.
3. **Optical Interferometric Sensor** — measures liquid-level via interference fringes.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Put a pencil in a half-filled glass — it appears "broken" at the water line because light bends (refracts) as it crosses from air to water. Optical level devices exploit this *exact* phenomenon.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Field-Effect Switch — Detailed Operating Principle

The field-effect switch operates as a **capacitance proximity detector**.

- **Step 1:** A high-frequency AC voltage (~ 500 kHz – 1 MHz) is applied between the active probe and the vessel wall.
- **Step 2:** The probe + tank wall + dielectric (air, then liquid) form a cylindrical capacitor.
- **Step 3:** As the liquid rises and contacts the probe's electric field, the dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$ increases:
  - Air: $\varepsilon_r \approx 1.0$
  - Water: $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$
  - Oil: $\varepsilon_r \approx 2.0 - 4.0$
- **Step 4:** Capacitance increases proportionally; the oscillator circuit detects the change and triggers the relay.

**Why this matters:** Capacitance switches work for *both* liquids and granular solids — making them ideal for silos, hoppers, and slurry tanks.

### 2.2 Diaphragm Switch — Detailed Theory

- **Step 1:** Hydrostatic pressure $P = \rho g h$ acts on the diaphragm's outer face.
- **Step 2:** When $P > P_{\text{threshold}}$ (set by spring preload), the diaphragm deflects.
- **Step 3:** Deflection pushes a pusher plate against a **snap-action microswitch** (SPDT).
- **Step 4:** The microswitch changes state with a crisp "snap" — eliminating contact bounce.

**Why this matters:** Diaphragm switches tolerate **viscous, slurry, and corrosive** fluids when fitted with coated diaphragms (PTFE, Hastelloy).

### 2.3 Electronic D/P Transmitter — Detailed Theory

The transmitter has **two pressure ports**: HP (high pressure) connected to the bottom tap, LP (low pressure) connected to the top tap.

$$
P_{HP} = P_{\text{vapor}} + \rho g h
$$

$$
P_{LP} = P_{\text{vapor}}
$$

$$
\Delta P = P_{HP} - P_{LP} = \rho g h
$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of the liquid (kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- $h$ = liquid level (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Smart Transmitter Features:** Auto-zero, auto-span, square-root extraction (for open-tank measurement with bubbler), and digital HART communication overlay on the 4–20 mA loop.

### 2.4 Displacer & Torque Tube — Detailed Theory

A displacer is a sealed cylinder (typically S.G. ≈ 0.5 – 2.5) suspended from a torque tube. The buoyant force is:

$$
F_B = \rho_{\text{liq}} \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{submerged}}
$$

The *net downward force* on the torque tube is:

$$
F_{\text{net}} = W_{\text{displacer}} - F_B
$$

As level rises, $V_{\text{submerged}}$ grows → $F_B$ grows → $F_{\text{net}}$ *decreases*. The torque tube twists clockwise, rotating an angle $\theta$:

$$
\theta = k \cdot F_{\text{net}} \quad (k = \text{tube stiffness}, \text{rad/N})
$$

A feedback linkage (in pneumatic models) or a Hall-effect/LVDT (in electronic models) converts $\theta$ to a 3–15 psi or 4–20 mA signal.

### 2.5 Interface Measurement — Differential Buoyancy

For interface detection, the displacer experiences a *weighted* buoyancy from both liquids:

$$
F_B = g \left[ \rho_{\text{heavy}} \cdot V_h + \rho_{\text{light}} \cdot V_{\text{l}} \right]
$$

where $V_h$ and $V_{\text{l}}$ are the volumes submerged in the heavy and light liquids respectively. As the displacer crosses the interface, $F_B$ changes linearly with interface height.

### 2.6 Float Level Switch — Force Balance

For a magnetic float switch, the actuation distance $d_{\text{act}}$ depends on the float's magnetic flux density and the reed switch sensitivity:

$$
d_{\text{act}} \propto \frac{B_r \cdot A_m}{\sqrt{F_{\text{spring}}}}
$$

Where $B_r$ = residual flux density, $A_m$ = magnet cross-section, $F_{\text{spring}}$ = reed-switch restoring force.

### 2.7 Laser Level Detector — Geometric Formulations

**Triangulation Method:**

$$
L = \frac{d}{\tan(\alpha + \beta)}
$$

where $d$ = baseline distance between laser emitter and detector, $\alpha$ = emitted beam angle, $\beta$ = reflected beam angle measured by PSD (Position Sensitive Detector).

**Time-of-Reflection Method:**

$$
L = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
$$

where $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s (speed of light), $t$ = measured round-trip time.

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure | $P = \rho g h$ | $\rho$: kg/m³, $g$: 9.81 m/s², $h$: m, $P$: Pa | D/P transmitter |
| 2 | Differential pressure | $\Delta P = \rho g h$ | Pa | D/P level |
| 3 | Buoyancy force | $F_B = \rho_{\text{liq}} g V_{\text{sub}}$ | N | Displacer |
| 4 | Net displacer force | $F_{\text{net}} = W - F_B$ | N | Torque tube |
| 5 | Torque tube twist | $\theta = k \cdot F_{\text{net}}$ | rad | Angle sensor |
| 6 | Capacitance (cylindrical) | $C = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(D/d)}$ | F | Field-effect |
| 7 | Float buoyancy | $F_B = \rho g \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ (sphere) | N | Float switch |
| 8 | Triangulation distance | $L = \dfrac{d}{\tan(\alpha + \beta)}$ | m | Laser |
| 9 | Time-of-reflection | $L = c \cdot t / 2$ | m | Laser ToF |
| 10 | Level from D/P with elevated/suppressed zero | $h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g} + h_{\text{offset}}$ | m | Smart D/P |
| 11 | Interface from dual D/P | $h_{\text{int}} = \dfrac{\Delta P_1}{\rho_1 g}$ | m | Interface |
| 12 | Snell's law (optical) | $n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$ | — | Optical probe |
| 13 | Critical angle (TIR) | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$ | rad | Refractive probe |
| 14 | Magnetic force on reed | $F \propto B^2 A / \mu_0$ | N | Magnetic float |
| 15 | Sight glass visibility | $h_{\text{read}} = h_{\text{actual}} \cdot (\rho_w / \rho_{\text{liq}})$ | m | Reflex gauge |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries:** D/P transmitters + chemical seals for hydrocracker reactors operating at 200 bar & 400 °C.
- **Water Treatment:** Float switches in sumps, magnetic gauges on clarifier tanks.
- **Pharma:** Laser triangulation for hygienic CIP/SIP vessels (no contact with product).
- **Food & Beverage:** Optical probes detect foam interface in milk tanks.
- **Power Plants:** Sight glasses on boiler drums (up to 200 bar steam).
- **LNG Carriers:** Displacer + torque tube for cryogenic liquefied gas level.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Level from D/P Transmitter Output

**Problem Setup:** A closed cylindrical tank of diameter $D = 2$ m contains oil of density $\rho = 850$ kg/m³. Vapor pressure $P_v = 1.2$ bar. The D/P transmitter has range 0 – 1000 mm H₂O and outputs 4–20 mA. Find (a) the current when level = 4.0 m, and (b) the level when current = 12 mA.

**Given data:**
- $\rho = 850$ kg/m³
- $h = 4.0$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Transmitter range: $\Delta P_{\min} = 0$, $\Delta P_{\max} = \rho_w g h_{\max} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1 = 9810$ Pa

**Step (a):** Calculate $\Delta P$ at $h = 4.0$ m:

$$
\Delta P = \rho g h = 850 \times 9.81 \times 4.0
$$

Multiplying $850 \times 9.81$:

$$
850 \times 9.81 = 8338.5
$$

Multiplying by $h = 4.0$:

$$
\Delta P = 8338.5 \times 4.0 = 33354 \; \text{Pa}
$$

**Convert to mm H₂O** (since the range is given in mm water column; 1 mm H₂O = 9.81 Pa):

$$
\Delta P_{\text{mmH2O}} = \frac{33354}{9.81} = 3400 \; \text{mm H}_2\text{O}
$$

But transmitter range is only **0 – 1000 mm H₂O**, so this exceeds range. Practical solution: **use elevated/suppressed zero calibration** or **use a different range**. Let us re-specify with a calibrated span matching $h_{\max}$.

Assume the transmitter is re-ranged to **0 – 4000 mm H₂O** (i.e., to match 0–4 m oil column).

$$
I = 4 + (20 - 4) \times \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta P_{\max}} = 4 + 16 \times \frac{3400}{4000}
$$

$$
I = 4 + 16 \times 0.85 = 4 + 13.6 = 17.6 \; \text{mA}
$$

**Step (b):** Solve for level when $I = 12$ mA:

$$
\% \text{ of span} = \frac{12 - 4}{20 - 4} = \frac{8}{16} = 0.5 \equiv 50\%
$$

Therefore $\Delta P = 0.5 \times 4000 = 2000$ mm H₂O = $2000 \times 9.81 = 19620$ Pa.

$$
h = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = \frac{19620}{850 \times 9.81} = \frac{19620}{8338.5}
$$

$$
h = 2.353 \; \text{m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Always convert $\Delta P$ units carefully — Pa vs. mm H₂O vs. bar vs. psi. **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 10197 mm H₂O ≈ 14.5 psi.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Displacer Buoyancy at Various Levels

**Problem Setup:** A displacer of length $L_d = 0.5$ m, diameter $D_d = 0.05$ m, mass $m_d = 4.0$ kg, displacer S.G. = 0.95. Liquid S.G. = 1.10. Compute buoyant force, net force, and torque tube angle for $h = 0.1, 0.3, 0.5$ m. (Torque tube stiffness $k = 0.04$ rad/N.)

**Step 1:** Compute volume of displacer:

$$
V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} D_d^2 \cdot L_d = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.05)^2 \times 0.5
$$

$$
V_d = 0.7854 \times 0.0025 \times 0.5 = 9.817 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2:** Compute weight (W):

$$
W = m_d \cdot g = 4.0 \times 9.81 = 39.24 \; \text{N}
$$

**Step 3:** For each level, submerged volume:

$$
V_{\text{sub}} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_d^2 \cdot h_{\text{sub}}
$$

Case 1: $h_{\text{sub}} = 0.1$ m:

$$
V_{\text{sub}} = 0.7854 \times 0.0025 \times 0.1 = 1.963 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3
$$

Buoyant force:

$$
F_B = \rho_{\text{liq}} \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{sub}} = 1100 \times 9.81 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
F_B = 10791 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-4} \approx 2.118 \; \text{N}
$$

Net force:

$$
F_{\text{net}} = W - F_B = 39.24 - 2.118 = 37.12 \; \text{N}
$$

Torque tube twist:

$$
\theta = k \cdot F_{\text{net}} = 0.04 \times 37.12 = 1.485 \; \text{rad} \; (\approx 85^\circ)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> That exceeds the typical torque tube range of **± 4°**! The displacer is too heavy for this torque tube. **Engineering fix:** Either reduce displacer mass or use a stiffer torque tube.

Case 2: $h_{\text{sub}} = 0.3$ m → $V_{\text{sub}} = 5.89 \times 10^{-4}$ m³ → $F_B = 6.355$ N → $F_{\text{net}} = 32.89$ N → $\theta = 1.316$ rad.

Case 3: $h_{\text{sub}} = 0.5$ m (fully submerged):

$$
V_{\text{sub}} = V_d = 9.817 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3
$$

$$
F_B = 1100 \times 9.81 \times 9.817 \times 10^{-4} \approx 10.59 \; \text{N}
$$

$$
F_{\text{net}} = 39.24 - 10.59 = 28.65 \; \text{N}
$$

$$
\theta = 0.04 \times 28.65 = 1.146 \; \text{rad}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** As level rises, $\theta$ decreases (twist reduces). Output signal decreases correspondingly. **Proper calibration** aligns $\theta_{\max}$ to level $= 0$ and $\theta_{\min}$ to level $= L_d$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Laser Triangulation Distance

**Problem Setup:** Laser emitter is at distance $d = 50$ mm from PSD. Emitted beam makes angle $\alpha = 30°$ with the normal. The reflected beam strikes the PSD at angle $\beta = 25°$. Find target distance $L$.

$$
L = \frac{d}{\tan(\alpha + \beta)} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{\tan(30° + 25°)} = \frac{0.05}{\tan(55°)}
$$

$$
\tan(55°) = 1.4281
$$

$$
L = \frac{0.05}{1.4281} = 0.0350 \; \text{m} = 35.0 \; \text{mm}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Time-of-Flight Distance Resolution

**Problem Setup:** The electronics can resolve time intervals of $\Delta t = 1$ ns. Compute the distance resolution.

$$
\Delta L = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{2.998 \times 10^8 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}}{2}
$$

$$
\Delta L = \frac{0.2998}{2} = 0.1499 \; \text{m} \approx 150 \; \text{mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is too coarse for short-range level measurement — hence **phase-shift detection** (modulated light, measure phase) is preferred for ranges < 50 m. Direct ToF is reserved for large silos (50 – 1000 m).

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Interface Level using Dual-Weighted Displacer

**Problem Setup:** A displacer of mass 5 kg is partially in oil (S.G. = 0.85) and partially in water (S.G. = 1.0). Volume in oil = $V_o = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³; volume in water = $V_w = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³. Compute net torque tube force.

$$
F_B = g(\rho_{\text{oil}} V_o + \rho_{\text{water}} V_w)
$$

$$
F_B = 9.81 \times (850 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} + 1000 \times 5 \times 10^{-4})
$$

$$
F_B = 9.81 \times (0.170 + 0.500) = 9.81 \times 0.670 = 6.573 \; \text{N}
$$

Weight:

$$
W = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05 \; \text{N}
$$

Net force:

$$
F_{\text{net}} = 49.05 - 6.573 = 42.48 \; \text{N}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> As interface rises (more volume in water), $F_B$ increases, $F_{\text{net}}$ decreases — same direction of signal change as in single-liquid operation. Calibration must use **two known interface levels** for accurate linearity.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Sensor | Input | Output | Range | Error Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Field-effect switch | Material presence | Relay contact | One point | Coating, foam |
| Diaphragm switch | Hydrostatic P | Microswitch | One point | Density variation |
| D/P transmitter | $\Delta P$ | 4–20 mA | Continuous | Density, fouling |
| Displacer | Buoyancy | Pneumatic / Electronic | Continuous | Coating, S.G. change |
| Float switch | Buoyancy | Reed contact | One point | Turbulence, fouling |
| Laser triangulation | Reflected angle | 0–10 V / 4–20 mA | 30 – 1000 mm | Surface reflectivity |
| Laser ToF | Light travel time | Digital | 0.5 – 500 m | Foam, dust, vapor |
| Sight glass | Visual | Operator | Local | Dirty glass |
| Magnetic gauge | Float position | Visual / 4–20 mA | Up to 6 m | Magnet decay |
| Optical probe | Refractive index | Switch | One point | Coating |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 D/P Transmitter with Chemical Seal — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tankV[Tank Vessel]
    hp[HP Tap - Bottom]
    lp[LP Tap - Top Vapor]
    cap1[Capillary A - filled with silicone oil]
    cap2[Capillary B - filled with silicone oil]
    cs1[Chemical Seal 1 - diaphragm + oil fill]
    cs2[Chemical Seal 2 - diaphragm + oil fill]
    dxr[Differential Pressure Sensor<br/>Capacitive Cell]
    a2d[Sigma-Delta ADC + Microprocessor]
    hart[HART Modem on 4-20 mA Loop]
    out[4-20 mA Output to DCS / PLC]

    tankV --> hp
    tankV --> lp
    hp --> cs1
    lp --> cs2
    cs1 --> cap1
    cs2 --> cap2
    cap1 --> dxr
    cap2 --> dxr
    dxr --> a2d
    a2d --> hart
    a2d --> out
```

> **Interpretation:** The two diaphragms of the chemical seals isolate the process fluid from the D/P cell. Silicone-oil-filled capillaries transmit pressure faithfully.

---

### 4.2 Displacer with Torque Tube — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    displ[Displacer Cylinder]
    rod[Displacer Rod]
    tt[Torque Tube - Helical Torsion Bar]
    wheel[Feedback Lever Wheel]
    noz[Nozzle Flapper]
    fb[Feedback Bellow]
    amp[Pneumatic Amplifier / Relay]
    psupply[Air Supply 20 psig]
    out3[3-15 psi to Recorder]

    displ --> rod
    rod --> tt
    tt --> wheel
    wheel --> noz
    fb --> wheel
    noz --> amp
    psupply --> amp
    amp --> out3
    fb -.->|back pressure| amp
```

> **Interpretation:** Force-balance principle — the torque tube's twist moves the flapper relative to the nozzle, changing back-pressure; the pneumatic amplifier restores equilibrium by re-pressurizing the feedback bellow. Output is proportional to level.

---

### 4.3 Float Level Switch — Reed Type Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    proc[Process Vessel]
    stk[Vertical Stem / Guide]
    flo[Foat with Internal Magnet]
    reed[Reed Switch - Sealed in Stem]
    wire[Cable to Junction Box]
    dcs[DCS / Alarm Lamp]

    proc --> stk
    stk --> flo
    flo --> reed
    reed --> wire
    wire --> dcs
```

> **Interpretation:** The float slides along the stem as level changes; the magnet's flux closes the reed contact at the preset height.

---

### 4.4 Laser Triangulation — Optical Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lsrc[Laser Diode 650 nm]
    lens1[Projection Lens]
    tgt[Target Surface]
    lens2[Receiver Lens]
    psd[Position Sensitive Detector PSD]
    adc[ADC + Microprocessor]
    disp[Distance Display]

    lsrc --> lens1
    lens1 -->|Beam strikes| tgt
    tgt -->|Diffuse reflection| lens2
    lens2 --> psd
    psd --> adc
    adc --> disp
```

> **Interpretation:** The spot's position on the PSD shifts linearly with target distance — geometry yields distance.

---

### 4.5 Laser Time-of-Reflection — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clk[High-Stability Clock]
    pdrv[Pulse Driver]
    lsrc[Pulsed Laser Diode]
    tgt[Target]
    apd[Avalanche Photodiode APD]
    tdc[Time-to-Digital Converter TDC]
    calc[Distance Calculator]

    clk --> pdrv
    pdrv --> lsrc
    lsrc -->|Light pulse| tgt
    tgt -->|Reflected pulse| apd
    apd --> tdc
    clk --> tdc
    tdc --> calc
```

> **Interpretation:** A 10 ns laser pulse travels to the target and back. TDC measures the elapsed time with picosecond resolution.

---

### 4.6 Magnetic Level Gauge — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tank[Process Tank]
    chamber[Non-Magnetic Chamber]
    magf[Float with Alnico Magnet]
    flaps[External Visual Flag Indicators Red/White]
    trans[External Magnetostrictive or Reed-Chain Transmitter]
    out[4-20 mA Output]

    tank --> chamber
    chamber --> magf
    magf --> flaps
    magf --> trans
    trans --> out
```

> **Interpretation:** The float's magnet flips colored flags (yellow/red) as it rises. The same magnet actuates the transmitter's external sensors.

---

### 4.7 Optical Refractive Level Switch — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    src[LED 940 nm]
    ptip[Polished Glass Tip]
    liq[Liquid Contact]
    pd[Photodiode Receiver]
    thr[Threshold Comparator]
    relay[Output Relay]

    src --> ptip
    ptip -->|Air - TIR occurs| pd
    ptip -->|Liquid - light refracts away| liq
    pd --> thr
    thr --> relay
```

> **Interpretation:** In air, light undergoes Total Internal Reflection inside the glass prism and returns to the photodiode. When liquid touches the tip, refractive index matching destroys TIR — light escapes, photodiode signal drops, relay trips.

---

### 4.8 Field-Effect (Capacitance) Level Switch — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    osc[RF Oscillator 1 MHz]
    prb[Probe Electrode]
    wall[Tank Wall Reference]
    det[Detector - Capacitance Bridge]
    comp[Comparator]
    relay[Relay Output SPDT]

    osc --> prb
    wall --> det
    prb --> det
    det --> comp
    comp --> relay
```

> **Interpretation:** Probe + tank wall + dielectric form a capacitor. Rising liquid changes capacitance → unbalances bridge → detector triggers relay.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the working of a torque tube used in displacer-type level measurement.*

**Model Answer:**
A torque tube is a precision torsion element used in displacer-type level instruments. It is essentially a hollow shaft with helical winding or slots cut into it, providing rotational stiffness about its axis while being axially flexible.

**Working Principle:**
1. The displacer hangs from one end of the torque tube; the other end is fixed and sealed to the tank.
2. As liquid level changes, the buoyant force on the displacer changes.
3. The resulting force difference twists the torque tube through a small angle (typically ± 4°).
4. This angular twist is mechanically linked to a pneumatic nozzle-flapper or an electronic LVDT/Hall sensor, producing a 3–15 psi or 4–20 mA output.
5. **Key advantage:** The torque tube seals the process from the outside while transmitting motion — eliminating the need for sliding seals.

**Advantages:** No friction, no hysteresis, leak-tight, suitable for high-pressure and hazardous service.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List any three advantages and two limitations of laser-based level detectors.*

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Non-contact measurement** — ideal for corrosive, hot, or hygienic fluids.
2. **High accuracy** — sub-millimetre possible with triangulation.
3. **Fast response** — suitable for dynamic level measurement (≤ 10 ms).

**Limitations:**
1. Performance is degraded by **foam, dust, vapor, and transparent fluids**.
2. Higher cost compared with mechanical float or sight-glass methods.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Displacer & D/P Transmitter Focus*

**(a)** Explain, with a block diagram, the working of an **electronic differential pressure transmitter** used for level measurement in a closed tank. How is the dry calibration performed when the process fluid has S.G. = 0.85 and the range is 0–4 m? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A displacer of length 0.6 m and diameter 50 mm, weighing 5 kg, is suspended in a liquid of S.G. = 1.05. Compute the buoyant force when (i) the tank is empty and (ii) the tank is full. Take torque tube stiffness as 0.025 rad/N. *(7 marks)*

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
A D/P transmitter has two pressure ports — HP (high pressure, connected to tank bottom) and LP (low pressure, connected to vapor space). The differential pressure is:

$$
\Delta P = \rho g h
$$

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HP[HP Tap Bottom] --> cap[Capacitive Sensor Cell]
    LP[LP Tap Vapor] --> cap
    cap --> a2d[Sigma-Delta ADC]
    a2d --> cpu[Microsprocessor]
    cpu --> hart[HART Modem]
    cpu --> loop[4-20 mA Loop]
```

**Working Steps:**
1. Process pressures enter the HP and LP capsules.
2. The differential pressure deflects the central diaphragm between the two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$.
3. The capacitance ratio $C_1 / C_2$ is measured by an AC bridge.
4. The microprocessor converts the ratio into a digitally linearized 4–20 mA signal.
5. HART protocol superimposes a digital signal at 1200 bps on the 4–20 mA loop.

**Dry Calibration for S.G. = 0.85 and Range = 0–4 m:**

Since the transmitter is calibrated in mm H₂O, we calculate the equivalent water column for 4 m of oil:

$$
h_{\text{water}} = h_{\text{oil}} \times \text{S.G.}_{\text{oil}} = 4 \times 0.85 = 3.4 \; \text{m H}_2\text{O} = 3400 \; \text{mm H}_2\text{O}
$$

The transmitter is pressurized at HP port with **3400 mm H₂O** (or **33.36 kPa** or **0.3336 bar**) while LP is vented to atmosphere. Set LRV = 4 mA, URV = 20 mA. This dry calibration eliminates the need for actual fluid in the field.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students forget that the dry calibration uses the **specific gravity correction** — a common mistake is using $h$ directly without multiplying by S.G.

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L_d = 0.6$ m, $D_d = 0.05$ m, $m_d = 5$ kg
- S.G. liquid = 1.05 → $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³
- $k = 0.025$ rad/N, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Displacer volume:**

$$
V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D_d^2 \times L_d = 0.7854 \times (0.05)^2 \times 0.6
$$

$$
V_d = 0.7854 \times 0.0025 \times 0.6 = 1.178 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Displacer weight:**

$$
W = m_d g = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05 \; \text{N}
$$

**Case (i) — Tank Empty:** No buoyancy, $F_B = 0$.

$$
F_{\text{net}} = W - 0 = 49.05 \; \text{N}
$$

Torque tube twist:

$$
\theta_{\text{empty}} = k \cdot F_{\text{net}} = 0.025 \times 49.05 = 1.226 \; \text{rad} \; (\approx 70.3°)
$$

**Case (ii) — Tank Full:** Displacer fully submerged, $V_{\text{sub}} = V_d$:

$$
F_B = \rho g V_{\text{sub}} = 1050 \times 9.81 \times 1.178 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
F_B = 10300.5 \times 1.178 \times 10^{-3} = 12.13 \; \text{N}
$$

$$
F_{\text{net}} = 49.05 - 12.13 = 36.92 \; \text{N}
$$

$$
\theta_{\text{full}} = 0.025 \times 36.92 = 0.923 \; \text{rad} \; (\approx 52.9°)
$$

**Span of twist:**

$$
\Delta \theta = \theta_{\text{empty}} - \theta_{\text{full}} = 1.226 - 0.923 = 0.303 \; \text{rad} \; (17.4°)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The usable span of twist is only the *difference*, not the absolute angle. The signal conditioning must subtract the empty-tank weight (zero-suppression).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Laser & Float Focus*

**(a)** Describe the **triangulation principle** of laser level detection with a labeled diagram. Compare it with the **time-of-reflection** method in terms of range, accuracy, and application. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **float-type level switch** using a reed switch. A float of diameter 100 mm and mass 0.3 kg is used in water. Determine (i) the maximum depth to which it can be submerged without sinking, and (ii) the buoyant force when fully submerged. *(7 marks)*

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Triangulation Principle:**
A laser beam is projected onto the target surface at an angle $\alpha$ from the normal. The diffuse-reflected beam is collected by a lens and projected onto a Position Sensitive Detector (PSD) at angle $\beta$. The spot's position on the PSD shifts linearly with target distance:

$$
L = \frac{d}{\tan(\alpha + \beta)}
$$

**Labeled Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Laser Diode]
    Tx[Target at Distance L]
    Lens[Receiver Lens]
    PSD[Position Sensitive Detector]

    L -->|Beam at angle alpha| Tx
    Tx -->|Diffuse reflection at angle beta| Lens
    Lens --> PSD
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Triangulation | Time-of-Reflection |
|---|---|---|
| Range | 30 – 1000 mm | 0.5 – 500 m |
| Accuracy | ± 0.1 mm (short range) | ± 10 mm (long range) |
| Beam divergence | Narrow cone | Narrow cone |
| Cost | Moderate | High (precision timing) |
| Best application | Small tanks, hygienic | Silos, bulk solids |
| Sensitivity to surface | High (needs diffuse reflection) | Moderate |
| Foam/dust immunity | Poor | Moderate |

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Working of Float Level Switch with Reed:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Tank[Process Tank]
    Stem[Non-Magnetic Stem]
    Float[Float with Magnet]
    Reed[Reed Switch inside Stem]
    Wire[Cable]
    Alarm[DCS / Alarm]

    Tank --> Stem
    Stem --> Float
    Float --> Reed
    Reed --> Wire
    Wire --> Alarm
```

**Working:**
1. The float, containing a permanent magnet, slides along a non-magnetic guide stem.
2. As liquid level rises, the float rises with it.
3. When the float reaches the reed switch position, the magnet's field closes the reed contacts.
4. The contact closure triggers an alarm or pump cut-off.

**Given:**
- Float diameter $D = 100$ mm = 0.1 m
- Float mass $m = 0.3$ kg
- Liquid = water → $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³

**Float volume:**

$$
V_{\text{float}} = \frac{\pi}{6} D^3 = \frac{\pi}{6} \times (0.1)^3 = 5.236 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Float weight:**

$$
W = m g = 0.3 \times 9.81 = 2.943 \; \text{N}
$$

**Maximum Submerged Depth (just before sinking):**
At this point, buoyant force equals weight:

$$
F_B = W
$$

$$
\rho g V_{\text{sub,max}} = W
$$

$$
V_{\text{sub,max}} = \frac{W}{\rho g} = \frac{2.943}{1000 \times 9.81} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3
$$

Assuming cylindrical submergence cross-section equal to float's largest area:

$$
V_{\text{sub}} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot h_{\text{sub}}
$$

$$
h_{\text{sub,max}} = \frac{V_{\text{sub,max}}}{\frac{\pi}{4} D^2} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.7854 \times 0.01}
$$

$$
h_{\text{sub,max}} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0382 \; \text{m} = 38.2 \; \text{mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The float is in stable equilibrium as long as its center of buoyancy stays above its center of gravity. Spherical floats are intrinsically stable.

**Buoyant Force When Fully Submerged:**

$$
F_B = \rho g V_{\text{float}} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 5.236 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
F_B = 5.136 \; \text{N}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "fully submerged" with "submerged to depth h." Fully submerged means the *entire float volume* displaces water. The two answers must be reported separately and clearly.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in Module 3:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch** — Mixing Pa, bar, mm H₂O, and psi without conversion (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 10197 mm H₂O).
> 2. **Forgetting S.G. Correction** — D/P transmitters read water column; you must multiply by specific gravity of process fluid.
> 3. **Submerged vs. Total Volume** — In displacer calculations, distinguish $V_{\text{sub}}$ (actual submerged) from $V_d$ (total).
> 4. **Triangulation Sign Convention** — Always use *sum* of angles (α + β) in denominator — students mistakenly use the difference.
> 5. **Float Stability** — A float must have S.G. < liquid S.G.; otherwise, it sinks. Center of gravity must be below the geometric center.
> 6. **Torque Tube Range** — Don't assume the full force is converted to angle; check whether the twist is within ± 4°.
> 7. **Sight Glass Reflex vs. Transparent** — Reflex glasses have prismatic grooves for better meniscus visibility; transparent glasses show clear liquid.
> 8. **Optical TIR** — Total Internal Reflection only occurs when going from *higher* to *lower* refractive index; otherwise, light refracts away.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Field-Effect Switch:** Capacitance-based; detects change in dielectric; works on liquids *and* solids.
- **Diaphragm Switch:** Pressure-driven; microswitch snap action; ideal for slurries.
- **D/P Transmitter:** Hydrostatic principle $\Delta P = \rho g h$; output 4–20 mA with HART.
- **Displacer + Torque Tube:** Buoyancy principle; output is angular twist; used for interface when S.G. is intermediate.
- **Interface Measurement:** Differential buoyancy; critical design — displacer S.G. between heavy and light liquid S.G.
- **Float Level Switch:** Magnet + reed switch; point-level detection; cylindrical/spherical buoyancy.
- **Laser Triangulation:** Short range, very high accuracy; needs diffuse reflective surface.
- **Laser Time-of-Flight:** Long range, lower accuracy; ideal for silos and bulk solids.
- **Flat Glass Gauge:** Direct visual; reflex type has prismatic grooves for clearer meniscus.
- **Magnetic Level Gauge:** Float flips external flags; safe for toxic/hazardous fluids.
- **Remote Reading Gauge:** Magnetic gauge with coupled transmitter (typically reed-chain).
- **Optical Devices:** TIR (Total Internal Reflection) principle; intrinsically safe (no electricity in tank).
- **Key Constants:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- **Key Formulas:** $\Delta P = \rho g h$, $F_B = \rho g V_{\text{sub}}$, $L = d / \tan(\alpha + \beta)$, $L = c \cdot t / 2$.
- **Standards:** 4–20 mA HART, 3–15 psi pneumatic, IEC 61511 (SIL) for safety applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
