<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: OPTICAL & RADIATION-BASED LEVEL MEASUREMENT

## 1.1 Light Reflection, Transmission and Refraction — Core Definitions

### Reflection
**Reflection** is the optical phenomenon in which a light ray striking a smooth interface between two media bounces back into the same medium from which it originated, without any change in frequency or wavelength. The angle of incidence $\theta_i$ equals the angle of reflection $\theta_r$, both measured from the surface normal (the imaginary line perpendicular to the boundary).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Reflection is the foundational principle behind all optical level switches, retro-reflective sensors, and the operation of the radar/ultrasonic level transmitter's transducer face.

### Transmission
**Transmission** is the passage of a light ray across the boundary and into the second medium. The transmitted ray always lies on the opposite side of the interface and bends (or refracts) according to the optical densities of the two media. A fraction of the incident light intensity is also lost at the interface due to partial reflection; the rest is transmitted.

### Refraction
**Refraction** is the bending of a light ray as it passes obliquely from one transparent medium into another with a different optical density. The bending is governed by **Snell's Law** and quantified by the **refractive index** of each medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refractive Index (n):** A dimensionless number that expresses the ratio of the speed of light in vacuum ($c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s) to the speed of light in a given medium ($v$).
>
> $$n = \frac{c}{v}$$

### Conceptual Analogy — The Swimming Pool Effect
Imagine you are standing at the edge of a swimming pool and looking at the legs of a person standing inside the water. The legs appear *bent* or *displaced* from their actual position. This is refraction in action. The water slows the light ray coming from the submerged foot, so by the time the ray reaches your eye, it has changed direction — fooling your brain into seeing the foot at a slightly different (shallower) location. In industrial instrumentation, the same principle misleads optical level sensors when the medium's refractive index is not compensated for; the *time-of-flight* method exists precisely to bypass this optical ambiguity.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Memorize for KTU)

| Symbol / Term | Value / Definition | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| $c$ (Speed of light in vacuum) | $\mathbf{2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$ | Used in all time-of-flight calculations |
| $n$ (Refractive index of air) | $\approx 1.0003$ | Treated as 1 for most industrial calculations |
| $n$ (Refractive index of water) | $\approx 1.33$ | Common process medium |
| $n$ (Refractive index of oil) | $\approx 1.45$ | Hydrocarbon process medium |
| $\theta_i = \theta_r$ | Law of reflection | Both measured from normal |
| Plane of incidence | Contains incident ray, reflected ray and normal | All angles referenced here |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In every numerical problem involving light or radar, the value of $c$ must be written explicitly. Omitting the constant is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction in the ESE (End Semester Examination).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2.1 Deep Theoretical Analysis of Optical Behaviour

### 2.1.1 The Three Optical Phenomena — Step-by-Step Logic

When a light ray strikes the boundary between two media (say, Medium 1 of refractive index $n_1$ and Medium 2 of refractive index $n_2$), three things can happen, and their *relative proportions* depend on the angle of incidence and the difference in refractive indices.

- **Step 1 — Incident Ray Strikes Interface:** A ray travelling in Medium 1 hits the boundary. The angle between the incident ray and the surface normal is $\theta_1$.
- **Step 2 — Partial Reflection:** A portion of the light energy obeys the *Law of Reflection* and returns into Medium 1. The angle of reflection $\theta_r$ equals $\theta_1$.
- **Step 3 — Partial Transmission with Refraction:** The remaining energy crosses into Medium 2. The transmitted (refracted) ray makes an angle $\theta_2$ with the normal, governed by **Snell's Law**:
  
  $$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$
  
- **Step 4 — The Critical Angle & Total Internal Reflection (TIR):** If light travels from a denser medium ($n_1 > n_2$) towards a less dense medium, beyond a specific angle — called the **critical angle** $\theta_c$ — no refraction occurs and 100 % of the light is reflected back. This is **Total Internal Reflection**.

  $$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$

- **Step 5 — Fresnel Losses:** At every interface, a fraction of energy is always lost to reflection. The **Fresnel equations** quantify this loss; for normal incidence, the reflected power fraction is:
  
  $$R = \left(\frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in instrumentation:** Radar level transmitters use the *reflected* component. The strength of the returning echo is directly related to the dielectric constant ($\varepsilon_r$) of the process medium — a higher dielectric constant gives a *stronger* reflection and a more reliable level reading.

### 2.1.2 Refraction in Process Industries — Why Engineering Care

Refraction is the principal cause of error in *optical* and *float-and-sight-glass* level gauges when the medium's temperature (and therefore its refractive index) changes. Heated oils have a different $n$ than cold oils; this shifts the apparent meniscus position. The radar method **eliminates** this error source because it does not rely on optical bending — it uses precise time measurement of an electromagnetic pulse.

---

## 2.2 Radar Level Transmitter — Theory of Operation

A **radar level transmitter** (also called a microwave level sensor) is a non-contact device that measures the level of liquids, slurries, and bulk solids by emitting a microwave pulse and measuring the time it takes to travel from the sensor to the material surface and back.

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step

- **Step 1 — Pulse Emission:** A Gunn diode or similar microwave source generates a short electromagnetic pulse in the microwave band (typically **6 GHz, 10 GHz, 26 GHz, or 80 GHz** in industrial radar).
- **Step 2 — Propagation:** The pulse travels at the speed of light through the air/vapour space above the process medium.
- **Step 3 — Reflection at Surface:** When the microwave hits the surface of the medium (whose dielectric constant is different from that of air), part of the energy is reflected back.
- **Step 4 — Echo Reception:** The reflected echo is captured by the same antenna (in monostatic radar) acting as a transceiver.
- **Step 5 — Time Measurement:** The elapsed time $t$ is measured by a high-speed electronic counter (time-to-digital converter).
- **Step 6 — Distance Calculation:** The level (or empty distance) is computed as:
  
  $$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$
  
  The factor 2 accounts for the round-trip travel of the pulse.

### 2.2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $n = c/v$ | Refractive index | dimensionless | Optical media analysis |
| $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$ | Snell's Law | dimensionless | Refraction problems |
| $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$ | Critical angle | degrees / radians | TIR analysis |
| $R = \left(\frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$ | Fresnel reflection (normal incidence) | dimensionless | Echo strength estimation |
| $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Distance from time of flight | meters | Radar level measurement |
| $t = \frac{2d}{c}$ | Time of flight for distance $d$ | seconds | Radar time computation |
| $L = H - d$ | Actual level | meters | Where $H$ is total tank height |
| $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | Beer-Lambert radiation attenuation | W/m² | Nuclear level gauge |
| $\mu = \rho \cdot \mu_m$ | Linear attenuation coefficient | 1/m | Radiation calculations |
| $t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda}$ | Half-life of radioisotope | seconds | Source selection |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value in your own notes.** Never use the bare `|` character inside a markdown table row — it breaks the table column structure. Example: write $n_1 \mid \sin\theta_1 \mid$ instead of $n_1 \mid \sin\theta_1 \mid$ (already in TeX-safe form).

### 2.2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Radar level transmitters are ubiquitous in **oil & gas storage tanks, chemical reactors, pharmaceutical fermenters, and food & beverage silos**. They are immune to foam, vapour, dust, and temperature changes — conditions that defeat ultrasonic and capacitance sensors. The 80 GHz radar, the most recent KTU-recognised industrial standard, has a beam divergence of only **±0.2°**, allowing it to measure levels through very narrow nozzles.

---

## 2.3 Radiation Level Sensor — Theoretical Basis

A **radiation (nuclear) level sensor** uses a sealed radioisotope source (commonly **Cobalt-60** or **Caesium-137**) that emits gamma radiation across the vessel, with a detector on the opposite side. The intensity of radiation reaching the detector depends on whether the vessel is empty (long path through air) or full (short path through process medium that absorbs gamma rays).

### 2.3.1 Why Use Radiation? 
When the process medium is **extremely hot, corrosive, abrasive, or under high pressure**, no contact-type sensor can survive. Radiation passes *through* the vessel wall, so the sensor is entirely non-intrusive. This makes it the only option for measuring molten metal, red-hot coke, or aggressive acid levels.

### 2.3.2 Attenuation Principle
Gamma rays are attenuated as they pass through matter. The intensity at the detector follows the **Beer-Lambert Law**:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident intensity (W/m²)
- $I$ = transmitted intensity (W/m²)
- $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient (1/m)
- $x$ = path length through absorbing medium (m)

The detector output is therefore a direct function of the *height* of the absorbing medium inside the vessel.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivations

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Snell's Law (from Fermat's Principle)

Fermat's Principle states that a light ray travels along the path of *minimum time* between two points. Consider a light ray going from point A in Medium 1 to point B in Medium 2, crossing a flat interface at point P.

Let the perpendicular distance from A to the interface be $h_1$ and from B to the interface be $h_2$. Let the horizontal distance between the foots of these perpendiculars be $L$. Let the point P be at horizontal distance $x$ from the foot of the perpendicular from A.

The total time of travel is:

$$T = \frac{\sqrt{x^2 + h_1^2}}{v_1} + \frac{\sqrt{(L-x)^2 + h_2^2}}{v_2}$$

Since $v_1 = c/n_1$ and $v_2 = c/n_2$, substitute:

$$T = \frac{n_1 \sqrt{x^2 + h_1^2}}{c} + \frac{n_2 \sqrt{(L-x)^2 + h_2^2}}{c}$$

For minimum time, differentiate $T$ with respect to $x$ and set $dT/dx = 0$:

$$\frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{n_1}{c} \cdot \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2 + h_1^2}} - \frac{n_2}{c} \cdot \frac{(L-x)}{\sqrt{(L-x)^2 + h_2^2}} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{n_1 \cdot x}{\sqrt{x^2 + h_1^2}} = \frac{n_2 \cdot (L-x)}{\sqrt{(L-x)^2 + h_2^2}}$$

From the geometry, $x / \sqrt{x^2 + h_1^2} = \sin\theta_1$ and $(L-x) / \sqrt{(L-x)^2 + h_2^2} = \sin\theta_2$. Substituting:

$$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$

This is **Snell's Law**, derived rigorously from Fermat's Principle. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Time-of-Flight Distance Equation for Radar

Consider a radar transceiver mounted at the top of a tank at height $H$ above the tank bottom. The sensor emits a microwave pulse that travels down to the liquid surface, reflects, and returns. Let $d$ be the *empty* distance (from sensor to surface) and $t$ be the round-trip time.

The microwave pulse travels at the speed of light $c$. The downward journey covers distance $d$ in time $t/2$:

$$d = c \cdot \frac{t}{2}$$

The return journey covers the same distance $d$ in another $t/2$, so the total time is:

$$t = \frac{2d}{c}$$

Solving for $d$:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

The actual liquid level $L$ is then:

$$L = H - d = H - \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

**Validity check:** If the tank is empty, $d = H$, so $t = 2H/c$. If full, $d \approx 0$, so $t \approx 0$. Both limits are physically consistent. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Beer-Lambert Law for Radiation Level Sensor

Consider a beam of gamma photons of intensity $I_0$ entering a process medium of thickness $dx$. The number of photons absorbed (or scattered) in the slice $dx$ is proportional to the number of incident photons and the slice thickness:

$$dI = -\mu \cdot I \cdot dx$$

where $\mu$ is the linear attenuation coefficient, characteristic of the medium. Separating variables:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \, dx$$

Integrating both sides from $0$ to $x$ and from $I_0$ to $I$:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \int_{0}^{x} dx$$

$$\ln I - \ln I_0 = -\mu x$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu x$$

Exponentiating:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$

This is the **Beer-Lambert Law of attenuation**. $\blacksquare$

For a vessel with process medium of height $L$ and the radiation beam travelling *horizontally* across the vessel of inner diameter $D$, the path through medium is approximately $L$ (if the beam is at the level of the medium) and the detector output is therefore a *step function* of $L$, ideal for point-level detection.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Standard)

### Worked Example 1 — Time of Flight Calculation

**Problem:** A radar level transmitter is installed at the top of a tank of total height 12 m. The measured round-trip time of the microwave pulse is **64 ns**. Calculate the actual liquid level in the tank. (Use $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.)

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the given data:
- $H = 12$ m
- $t = 64$ ns $= 64 \times 10^{-9}$ s
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

Step 2 — Apply the time-of-flight equation to find the empty distance:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{(3 \times 10^8)(64 \times 10^{-9})}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{19.2}{2} = 9.6 \text{ m}$$

Step 3 — Calculate the actual liquid level:

$$L = H - d = 12 - 9.6 = 2.4 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** The actual liquid level is **2.4 m**. $\blacksquare$

---

### Worked Example 2 — Critical Angle and TIR

**Problem:** Light travels from water ($n_1 = 1.33$) into air ($n_2 = 1.00$). Calculate the critical angle. Will a light ray striking the water-air interface at 60° undergo refraction or total internal reflection?

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the critical angle formula:

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right) = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1.00}{1.33}\right)$$

Step 2 — Compute the argument:

$$\frac{1.00}{1.33} = 0.7519$$

Step 3 — Find the angle:

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.7519) = 48.75°$$

Step 4 — Compare with the incident angle: Since $60° > 48.75°$, the incident angle exceeds the critical angle. Therefore, the ray undergoes **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** and does not pass into the air.

**Answer:** $\theta_c = 48.75°$, and at 60° incidence, TIR occurs. $\blacksquare$

---

### Worked Example 3 — Radiation Attenuation

**Problem:** A Co-60 source emits gamma radiation with intensity 1000 counts/s. The radiation passes through a process medium of thickness 0.5 m with linear attenuation coefficient $\mu = 1.386$ /m. Calculate the intensity reaching the detector.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the Beer-Lambert Law:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$

Step 2 — Substitute values:

$$I = 1000 \cdot e^{-(1.386)(0.5)} = 1000 \cdot e^{-0.693}$$

Step 3 — Note that $e^{-0.693} \approx 0.5$ (this is a consequence of $\ln 2 = 0.693$):

$$I = 1000 \times 0.5 = 500 \text{ counts/s}$$

**Answer:** Detector intensity = **500 counts/s** (50 % of incident). $\blacksquare$

## 4.1 Structural Diagrams and Schematics

### Diagram 1 — Behaviour of Light at an Interface (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INCIDENT LIGHT RAY IN MEDIUM 1"] --> B["STRIKES INTERFACE AT ANGLE theta 1"]
    B --> C["PARTIAL REFLECTION"]
    B --> D["PARTIAL TRANSMISSION INTO MEDIUM 2"]
    C --> E["REFLECTED RAY ANGLE theta 1 EQUALS THETA R"]
    D --> F["REFRACTED RAY BENDS TO ANGLE theta 2"]
    F --> G{"IS theta 1 GREATER THAN CRITICAL ANGLE"}
    G -->|NO| H["REFRACTION CONTINUES INTO MEDIUM 2"]
    G -->|YES| I["TOTAL INTERNAL REFLECTION BACK INTO MEDIUM 1"]
    E --> J["DETECTOR OR EYE RECEIVES ECHO"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

```mermaid
    A3 -->|WAVE TRAVELS AT SPEED OF LIGHT| B1["PROCESS MEDIUM SURFACE ACTS AS REFLECTOR"]
    B1 -->|REFLECTED ECHO RETURNS| A3
    A2 --> A4["LOW NOISE RECEIVER AMPLIFIER"]
    A4 --> A5["TIME TO DIGITAL CONVERTER MEASURES T"]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
