## 5.1 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 ESE):** Module 3 in PEMRT865 typically carries 14–18 marks. Expect either a full 14-mark question, a 7-mark sub-part, or a 3-mark definition. The question bank below mirrors the actual KTU paper pattern.

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the terms reflection, transmission, and refraction with respect to light. State Snell's Law.**

**Model Answer:**

**Reflection** is the phenomenon in which a light ray, on striking a smooth interface, returns into the same medium. The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection, both measured from the normal. **Transmission** is the passage of a portion of the light energy across the interface into the second medium. **Refraction** is the bending of this transmitted ray as it crosses the interface, caused by the change in speed of light between media of different optical densities. **Snell's Law** states that the product of the refractive index and the sine of the angle (with the normal) is constant across the interface: $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$. (3 marks)

---

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three advantages of radar level transmitters over ultrasonic level transmitters.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Immunity to vapour, dust, and foam** — microwaves are not attenuated by air-borne contaminants the way ultrasound is.
2. **Higher accuracy** — radar operates at the speed of light, giving nanosecond-level time resolution; ultrasonic is affected by temperature and gas composition (sound speed varies).
3. **No moving parts and truly non-contact** — the antenna is the only wetted boundary, making radar suitable for corrosive, hot, or pressurised media.

(3 marks)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a **radar level transmitter** with a neat block diagram. Derive the time-of-flight equation used for level measurement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A radar level transmitter is a non-contact level measurement device that uses microwave pulses in the GHz range. The operating principle is as follows:

- A microwave source (Gunn diode) generates a short pulse that is launched into the tank through a horn or rod antenna.
- The pulse travels downward at the speed of light $c$ through the air/vapour space above the process medium.
- Upon striking the surface of the medium (whose dielectric constant is different from that of air), a portion of the energy is reflected back as an echo.
- The same antenna receives the echo, and the elapsed round-trip time $t$ is measured by a high-speed time-to-digital converter.

**Derivation of time-of-flight equation:**

Let $d$ be the empty distance from the sensor to the surface. The pulse travels down a distance $d$ in time $t/2$ and returns the same distance in another $t/2$:

$$\text{Downward journey: } d = c \cdot \frac{t}{2}$$

$$\text{Total round trip: } t = \frac{2d}{c}$$

Solving for $d$:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

The actual level is $L = H - d$, where $H$ is the total height of the tank from the sensor mounting to the tank bottom. (7 marks)

---

**(b)** A radar level transmitter is fitted at the top of a tank of total height 8 m. The dielectric constant of the stored liquid is 2.2. The measured round-trip time is 40 ns. Determine: **(i)** the empty distance, **(ii)** the actual level of the liquid, and **(iii)** the percentage of the tank that is filled. Take $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $H = 8$ m, $t = 40$ ns $= 40 \times 10^{-9}$ s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**(i) Empty distance:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{(3 \times 10^8)(40 \times 10^{-9})}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{12}{2} = 6 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Actual liquid level:**

$$L = H - d = 8 - 6 = 2 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Percentage filled:**

$$\% \text{Filled} = \frac{L}{H} \times 100 = \frac{2}{8} \times 100 = 25\%$$

**Answer:** Empty distance = 6 m, Level = 2 m, Tank is 25 % full. (7 marks)

---

#### Question B: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern] — *Internal Choice*

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a **radiation (nuclear) level sensor**. State Beer-Lambert Law and explain its significance in level measurement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A radiation level sensor uses a sealed radioisotope source (typically Cobalt-60 with half-life 5.27 years, or Caesium-137 with half-life 30.1 years) that emits a collimated beam of gamma radiation. The beam is directed horizontally across the vessel, and a detector (scintillation counter or ionization chamber) is placed on the opposite side.

- When the vessel is empty, the beam travels only through air (low attenuation), and a *high* intensity reaches the detector.
- As the level of the process medium rises and intercepts the beam, the medium absorbs gamma photons according to its density and atomic number. Detector intensity *drops*.
- This change in intensity is processed by a comparator; when it falls below a preset threshold, a level alarm or output is generated.

**Beer-Lambert Law:**

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$

where $I_0$ is the incident intensity, $I$ is the intensity after travelling distance $x$ through the medium, and $\mu$ is the linear attenuation coefficient. The exponential dependence on $x$ makes radiation sensors ideal for point-level detection: a small change in level produces a sharp change in detector output. (7 marks)

---

**(b)** A Cs-137 source emits gamma rays with intensity 2000 counts/s. The radiation passes through a process medium of thickness 0.4 m. If the linear attenuation coefficient of the medium is 1.732 /m, calculate the intensity reaching the detector. Also determine the count rate if the thickness increases to 0.8 m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $I_0 = 2000$ counts/s, $x_1 = 0.4$ m, $\mu = 1.732$ /m.

**Case 1 — Thickness 0.4 m:**

$$I_1 = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x_1} = 2000 \cdot e^{-(1.732)(0.4)}$$

$$I_1 = 2000 \cdot e^{-0.6928}$$

Note that $0.6928 \approx \ln 2$, so $e^{-0.6928} \approx 0.5$:

$$I_1 = 2000 \times 0.5 = 1000 \text{ counts/s}$$

**Case 2 — Thickness 0.8 m:**

$$I_2 = 2000 \cdot e^{-(1.732)(0.8)} = 2000 \cdot e^{-1.3856}$$

Since $1.3856 = 2 \times 0.6928 \approx 2 \ln 2 = \ln 4$:

$$I_2 = 2000 \times e^{-\ln 4} = \frac{2000}{4} = 500 \text{ counts/s}$$

**Answer:** At 0.4 m thickness, intensity = 1000 counts/s. At 0.8 m thickness, intensity = 500 counts/s. The exponential relationship means *doubling the thickness* (in this special case) halved the intensity — a direct consequence of $\mu x = \ln 2$ at the first thickness. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the time-of-flight equation. The pulse travels *down* and *back*, so the distance is $ct/2$, not $ct$. This single error loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Unit errors in $t$** — always convert nanoseconds to seconds before substituting. $1 \text{ ns} = 10^{-9}$ s. Mixing units gives numerically absurd answers and zero marks.
> 3. **Wrong sign in level calculation** — students often write $L = d - H$ instead of $L = H - d$. Memorise: *Empty distance* $d$ is the space *above* the liquid; *Level* $L$ is the space *below*.
> 4. **Snell's law angle confusion** — both angles are measured from the *normal*, not from the surface. Drawing a clear diagram with the normal is worth 1 mark by itself in the ESE.
> 5. **Confusing refractive index with dielectric constant** — they are *related* ($n^2 \approx \varepsilon_r$ for non-magnetic media) but are not the same. In radar problems, use $\varepsilon_r$; in optics, use $n$.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Reflection:** $\theta_i = \theta_r$ (measured from the normal). All angles in Snell's law and the law of reflection are with respect to the **normal**, not the surface.
- **Refraction:** $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$. Refractive index $n = c/v$.
- **Critical Angle:** $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$ when light travels from a denser to a rarer medium. Beyond $\theta_c$, **Total Internal Reflection** occurs.
- **Fresnel Reflection (normal incidence):** $R = \left(\frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$. A higher dielectric constant medium reflects *more* microwave energy — this is why water and oil are easy radar targets, but low-dielectric hydrocarbons are harder.
- **Radar Time-of-Flight:** $d = ct/2$. Multiply by 2 to get round-trip time $t = 2d/c$.
- **Actual Level:** $L = H - d$ where $H$ is the sensor-to-bottom distance.
- **Radar Frequencies in Industry:** 6 GHz, 10 GHz, 26 GHz, 80 GHz. Higher frequency = narrower beam, better resolution, smaller antenna.
- **Beer-Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$. Used in radiation level sensors where $\mu$ depends on medium density and atomic number.
- **Half-Life:** $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / \lambda$. Cs-137 (30.1 y) and Co-60 (5.27 y) are the most common industrial sources.
- **Common Industrial Radar Equation:** $L = H - \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$. Memorise this exactly.
- **Snell's Law and Refraction apply to the optical level sensor family (refractometric switches, sight glass with compensation). Radar and radiation sensors are *immune* to refraction-based errors.**
- **Numerical Safety Check:** A 10 m round trip corresponds to $t = 2 \times 10 / (3 \times 10^8) = 66.7$ ns. Use this as a sanity check for any radar numerical.
- **Beam Divergence:** 80 GHz radar has $\pm 0.2°$ beam divergence vs. 26 GHz's $\pm 8°$. This is why 80 GHz radar can be mounted on long nozzles and still measure reliably in narrow tanks.
- **KTU Frequency (Dec 2024):** Expect at least one 7-mark question on radar time-of-flight, and one 3-mark definitional question on reflection/refraction/Snell's law. A radiation sensor question appears roughly every alternate exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: continuous level measurement, Ultrasonic level measurement, Resistance tape level measurement, Rotating paddle switch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT865 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** continuous level measurement, Ultrasonic level measurement, Resistance tape level measurement, Rotating paddle switch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Continuous Level Measurement — Formal Definition

**Continuous Level Measurement** refers to a class of instrumentation techniques designed to provide a real-time, proportional, and uninterrupted measurement of the surface height (level) of a process material — which may be a liquid, slurry, or bulk solid — contained within a vessel, tank, silo, or open reservoir. The output is a continuous analog or digital signal that varies in direct proportion to the level, as opposed to point level devices which only indicate whether the material has reached a predefined threshold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Continuous level measurement devices are evaluated against parameters like accuracy, range, contact vs. non-contact operation, and compatibility with the process medium. The four major sub-classifications are: (1) **Hydrostatic**, (2) **Electrical**, (3) **Acoustic**, and (4) **Radiation-based**.

### 1.2 The Big Picture — Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bathroom Scale vs. The Thermometer:**
> Imagine you have two ways of knowing how much water is in a bathtub:
> - A **point sensor** is like a switch that beeps when water touches it — it only tells you "wet" or "dry."
> - A **continuous level sensor** is like drawing a ruler on the inside of the tub and reading the exact height at any moment.
>
> In a chemical plant, knowing *exactly* how many liters are in a tank (not just "full" or "empty") is critical for inventory, control, and safety — this is what continuous level measurement provides.

### 1.3 Ultrasonic Level Measurement — Formal Definition

**Ultrasonic Level Measurement** is a **non-contact**, continuous level measurement technique that uses high-frequency acoustic waves (typically in the range of **20 kHz to 200 kHz**) to determine the distance between a transducer mounted at the top of a vessel and the surface of the process material. The fundamental principle is the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** method, where the time taken for an emitted ultrasonic pulse to travel to the surface and return as an echo is measured and converted to a level reading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU Expected Answer):** "Ultrasonic level measurement determines the level of a process material by measuring the time required for an ultrasonic pulse to travel from a transducer to the material surface and back, using the known velocity of sound in the medium."

### 1.4 Ultrasonic Level Measurement — Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Bats and Submarines:** A bat hunting insects in the dark is *exactly* using ultrasonic level measurement. The bat screams (emits a pulse), the sound bounces off the moth (echoes from the surface), and the bat's brain instantly calculates distance from the round-trip time. Submarines do the same thing underwater (called SONAR). In a tank, the ultrasonic sensor is the "bat" and the liquid surface is the "moth."

### 1.5 Resistance Tape Level Measurement — Formal Definition

**Resistance Tape Level Measurement** is a **contact-type**, continuous level measurement technique that uses a long, precision resistance element (a "tape") suspended vertically inside a vessel. As the liquid level rises, more of the tape becomes submerged. Because the submerged portion is short-circuited by the conductive liquid, the effective resistance of the tape changes proportionally with the level. This resistance change is measured using a **Wheatstone bridge** or an ohmmeter circuit to give a direct reading of liquid height.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation Note:** Resistance tape measurement requires the process liquid to be **electrically conductive** (typically > 1 μS/cm). It is unsuitable for hydrocarbons, oils, or ultra-pure water.

### 1.6 Resistance Tape — Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Dipstick with a Brain:** Imagine a regular dipstick, but it is made of a special material whose electrical resistance decreases by a fixed amount for every centimeter that gets wet. You attach a multimeter to the top, and as the liquid rises, the resistance reading drops. The resistance value directly translates to a level in millimeters. This is, in essence, a resistance tape.

### 1.7 Rotating Paddle Switch — Formal Definition

> [!WARNING]
> **Categorical Clarification:** The **Rotating Paddle Switch** is strictly a **POINT level measurement** device (not continuous). It is included in this module because it is typically discussed in the same chapter on level instrumentation in the KTU syllabus, and students must understand the contrast.

A **Rotating Paddle Switch** (also called a *Rotating Paddle Level Switch* or *Motorized Paddle Switch*) is a point-level detection device used primarily for **bulk solids** (powders, granules, chips, etc.). It consists of a small low-power electric motor that continuously rotates a paddle (shaft with blades) at a fixed speed. When the bulk solid material rises and physically impedes the rotation of the paddle, the motor encounters a torque overload. This stall condition is detected by a mechanical clutch and a microswitch, which triggers an alarm or a control relay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Applications:** Grain silos, cement storage, plastic pellet hoppers, fly-ash bunkers, and coal bins.

### 1.8 Rotating Paddle — Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Ceiling Fan in a Sandstorm:** Imagine a small fan spinning freely in an empty container. Now pour sand into the container. When the sand level reaches the fan blades, the sand physically jams the rotation, and the motor feels a sudden resistance. A smart mechanism inside the motor detects this jam and flips a switch. That switch can be wired to a red warning light that says "SILO FULL." This is exactly the rotating paddle principle.

### 1.9 Key Physical Constants and Units (Bold for Emphasis)

| Parameter | Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of sound in air at **20 °C** | **c = 343 m/s** | Critical for ultrasonic ToF calculations |
| Standard ultrasonic frequency | **20 kHz – 200 kHz** | Above human hearing range |
| Conductive liquid threshold | **> 1 μS/cm** | Minimum for resistance tape to function |
| Paddle rotation speed | **1 – 5 RPM** | Low to prevent damage to material |
| Typical accuracy (ultrasonic) | **±0.25% of full scale** | Industry standard |
| Typical accuracy (resistance tape) | **±1 mm** | High resolution, contact type |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Continuous Level Measurement Methods

- **A. Hydrostatic Methods:** Use pressure at the bottom of the liquid column to infer level (e.g., differential pressure transmitter).
- **B. Electrical Methods:** Use the electrical properties of the medium (resistance, capacitance).
- **C. Acoustic Methods:** Use sound waves (ultrasonic).
- **D. Radiation Methods:** Use gamma rays (nuclear).
- **E. Mechanical Methods:** Use float, displacer, or tape.

The focus of this module (Module 3) is on **Electrical (Resistance Tape)**, **Acoustic (Ultrasonic)**, and **Mechanical (Paddle as a comparator)**.

---

### 2.2 Ultrasonic Level Measurement — Operational Theory

**Why does it work?** Sound travels at a known, predictable speed through a medium. If we send a pulse and time the echo, we can calculate distance.

**How does it work?** Step-by-step logic:

- **Step 1 — Pulse Emission:** A piezoelectric crystal in the transducer is excited by a high-voltage pulse, generating an ultrasonic wave.
- **Step 2 — Propagation:** The wave travels through the gas/vapor space above the material at the **speed of sound (c)**.
- **Step 3 — Reflection:** The wave hits the surface of the material. A portion is reflected back toward the transducer (acoustic impedance mismatch causes reflection).
- **Step 4 — Echo Reception:** The transducer (acting now as a receiver) converts the returning mechanical vibration back into a small electrical signal.
- **Step 5 — Time Measurement:** The instrument's electronics precisely measure the round-trip time **t**.
- **Step 6 — Distance Calculation:** The distance **D** from the transducer face to the surface is calculated as: **D = (c × t) / 2** (the factor of 2 accounts for the round trip).
- **Step 7 — Level Calculation:** If the total empty distance (from transducer to bottom) is **H**, then the level **L = H − D**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the divisor 2?** Because the time *t* is the time taken for the pulse to go *down to the surface* AND come *back up*. The total path length is *c × t*, and this path is the *down* leg plus the *up* leg (which are equal). So the *one-way* distance is half the total path.

**Factors Affecting Accuracy (Why & How):**
- **Temperature variation:** The speed of sound in air changes by approximately **0.6 m/s per °C**. Compensation is done using a built-in temperature sensor.
- **Gas composition:** Different gases have different acoustic velocities. Calibration for the specific vapor space is essential.
- **Foam, vapors, dust:** These can absorb or scatter the ultrasonic beam, reducing echo strength.
- **Turbulence:** Agitated surfaces can deflect the echo away from the transducer, causing signal loss.
- **Mounting nozzle:** The nozzle should be smooth and ideally of the same diameter as the transducer to avoid false echoes.

**Real-World Engineering Utility:** Ultrasonic level sensors are widely used in:
- Municipal water treatment (open channel flow measurement)
- Chemical storage tanks (corrosive liquids, since there is no contact)
- Food & beverage industry (hygienic, wash-down rated)
- Wastewater sumps and lift stations

---

### 2.3 Resistance Tape Level Measurement — Operational Theory

**Why does it work?** A conductor immersed in a conductive liquid is effectively "shorted out" by the liquid. The remaining un-submerged resistance is proportional to the empty height.

**How does it work?** Step-by-step logic:

- **Step 1 — Construction:** A precision resistance wire is wound on a slender, insulated support, enclosed in a protective perforated stainless-steel sheath. Total resistance **R_total** is fixed (e.g., 5 kΩ over 10 m).
- **Step 2 — Installation:** The tape is suspended vertically from the top of the tank, with its lower end connected (via a ground/reference rod) to the conductive liquid.
- **Step 3 — Liquid Contact:** As the conductive liquid rises, it enters through the perforations and contacts the resistance wire at the current liquid level.
- **Step 4 — Effective Resistance:** The portion of the tape *below* the liquid surface is short-circuited by the highly conductive liquid. The portion *above* the liquid remains the only contributor to measured resistance.
- **Step 5 — Resistance to Level Conversion:** The effective resistance **R_eff** is linearly related to the empty height **H_e**:
  **R_eff / R_total = H_e / H_total**
  Therefore, level **L = H_total − H_e = H_total × (1 − R_eff/R_total)**
- **Step 6 — Signal Conversion:** R_eff is measured using a **Wheatstone bridge** or a precision ohmmeter, and the output is converted to a standard 4–20 mA signal.

**Mathematical Derivation of the Resistance Change:**

For a uniform resistance wire of total resistance $R_{total}$ and total length $L_{total}$:

$$R_{total} = \rho \cdot \frac{L_{total}}{A}$$

where $\rho$ is the resistivity and $A$ is the cross-sectional area.

If the liquid is at level $L$ from the bottom, the submerged length is $L$, and the dry (effective) length is $L_{total} - L$. The effective resistance is:

$$R_{eff} = \rho \cdot \frac{(L_{total} - L)}{A}$$

Since $R_{total} = \rho \cdot L_{total}/A$, we can write:

$$R_{eff} = R_{total} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{L}{L_{total}}\right)$$

Solving for level $L$:

$$L = L_{total} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{R_{eff}}{R_{total}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The relationship between level and resistance is LINEAR** — this is a major advantage of the resistance tape method, as it eliminates the need for complex linearization tables.

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- Hydrocarbon storage tanks (using a stilling well)
- Boiler drum level measurement
- Cooling water reservoirs
- Industrial process tanks in chemical plants

---

### 2.4 Rotating Paddle Switch — Operational Theory

**Why does it work?** A free-spinning motor in air draws a small, constant current. When the paddle is jammed by solid material, the motor stalls, and the current (or torque) changes drastically. This change is mechanically/electrically detected and used to switch a relay.

**How does it work?** Step-by-step logic:

- **Step 1 — Mounting:** The switch is installed at the desired high-level (or low-level) point on the side of the silo.
- **Step 2 — Free Rotation:** Under normal conditions (material below the paddle), a small synchronous or gear motor rotates the paddle at a fixed low speed (typically 1–5 RPM).
- **Step 3 — Material Contact:** When the bulk solid reaches the paddle, the material packs around the blades, creating a mechanical load.
- **Step 4 — Stall Detection:** A **mechanical slip clutch** disconnects the motor shaft from the paddle, preventing motor damage. A **torque spring** is deflected, and a **microswitch** is actuated.
- **Step 5 — Output:** The microswitch changes state, opening or closing a relay contact wired to an alarm (horn, beacon) or to a control system (PLC/DCS) to stop a feeder conveyor.
- **Step 6 — Auto-Reset (Optional):** When the level drops below the paddle, the spring returns the switch to its normal state, and the motor resumes rotation.

**Why not continuous?** Because the paddle only provides a binary output (rotating = no material, stalled = material present). It cannot measure *how much* material is there — only *whether* the material has reached the paddle's position.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every key formula required for the KTU 2024 ESE for this topic. Master these — they are highly likely to appear in Part A or Part B questions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Distance to surface (ultrasonic) — *one-way distance* | meters |
| 2 | $c = c_0 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{T_0}}$ | Speed of sound as a function of temperature | m/s |
| 3 | $L = H_{total} - D$ | Liquid level (ultrasonic, top-mounted) | meters |
| 4 | $L = H_{total} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{R_{eff}}{R_{total}}\right)$ | Liquid level (resistance tape) | meters |
| 5 | $R_{total} = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L_{total}}{A}$ | Total resistance of tape wire | ohms (Ω) |
| 6 | $\Delta R = R_{total} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_{total}}$ | Change in resistance per unit level change | ohms/m |
| 7 | $c_{air}(20°C) = 343$ **m/s** | Reference speed of sound in air | m/s |
| 8 | $c_{air}(T) = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | Empirical linear approx. (T in °C) | m/s |
| 9 | $\Delta c / °C \approx 0.6$ **m/s** | Temperature coefficient of sound speed | m/s/°C |
| 10 | $N_{pulses} = \dfrac{f_{PRF}}{2 \cdot D/c}$ | Number of pulses per measurement cycle | dimensionless |

**Boundary Conditions & Engineering Limits:**

- **Ultrasonic:** Minimum measurable distance (blanking zone) is typically **0.25 m – 0.5 m** below the transducer face. Maximum range in air is **~15 m**.
- **Resistance Tape:** Maximum tape length typically **30 m**. Conductivity must be **> 1 μS/cm**.
- **Paddle Switch:** Maximum particle size **< 50 mm** (for standard paddles). Bulk density **> 100 g/L** for reliable operation.

---

### 2.6 Comparative Matrix — At a Glance

| Feature | Ultrasonic | Resistance Tape | Rotating Paddle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Continuous | Continuous | Point |
| **Contact** | Non-contact | Contact | Contact |
| **Medium** | Liquids & slurries | Conductive liquids only | Bulk solids |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.25% FS | ±1 mm | N/A (binary) |
| **Cost** | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Maintenance** | Low (clean face) | Medium (fouling) | Medium (motor wear) |
| **Hazardous Area** | Safe (no contact) | Safe | Requires Ex-proof motor |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Ultrasonic Level from Time-of-Flight

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** An ultrasonic level sensor is mounted at the top of an open tank. The total height of the tank from the sensor face to the bottom is **H = 5.0 m**. The sensor measures a round-trip echo time of **t = 22.5 milliseconds**. The temperature of the air above the liquid is **T = 30 °C**. Calculate the liquid level in the tank.

**Given:**
- Total tank height: $H = 5.0$ m
- Round-trip time: $t = 22.5 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- Temperature: $T = 30$ °C = 303.15 K

**Step 1 — Calculate the speed of sound at T = 30 °C:**

Using the linear approximation:

$$c = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T_{°C} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot 30$$

$$c = 331 + 18 = 349 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the one-way distance from the sensor to the liquid surface:**

$$D = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

$$D = \frac{349 \text{ m/s} \times 22.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}{2}$$

$$D = \frac{7.8525}{2} = 3.92625 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Convert the empty distance to a level reading:**

$$L = H - D = 5.0 - 3.92625$$

$$L = 1.07375 \text{ m} \approx 1.074 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion:** The liquid level in the tank is **L ≈ 1.074 m** (or **1074 mm**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Many students forget to divide by 2 in Step 2. Always remember: the measured time is for a *round trip*, but you need the *one-way* distance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Resistance Tape Sensitivity & Linearity

**Problem Setup:** A resistance tape has a total length of **L_total = 8 m** and a total resistance of **R_total = 4000 Ω**. The wire is made of constantan (a low-temperature-coefficient alloy). Calculate:
- (a) The sensitivity (ΔR per cm of level change).
- (b) The effective resistance when the level is at L = 3 m.
- (c) The level when the effective resistance is R_eff = 1500 Ω.

**Given:**
- $L_{total} = 8$ m = 800 cm
- $R_{total} = 4000$ Ω

**Part (a) — Sensitivity:**

The sensitivity is the change in resistance per unit change in level:

$$S = \frac{R_{total}}{L_{total}} = \frac{4000 \text{ Ω}}{8 \text{ m}} = 500 \text{ Ω/m}$$

In other words, for every 1 cm of liquid rise, the resistance decreases by:

$$S_{cm} = \frac{4000 \text{ Ω}}{800 \text{ cm}} = 5 \text{ Ω/cm}$$

**Part (b) — Effective resistance at L = 3 m:**

Using the formula:

$$R_{eff} = R_{total} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{L}{L_{total}}\right)$$

$$R_{eff} = 4000 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{3}{8}\right) = 4000 \cdot \frac{5}{8}$$

$$R_{eff} = 4000 \cdot 0.625 = 2500 \text{ Ω}$$

**Part (c) — Level at R_eff = 1500 Ω:**

Rearranging the formula for L:

$$L = L_{total} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{R_{eff}}{R_{total}}\right)$$

$$L = 8 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1500}{4000}\right) = 8 \cdot \left(1 - 0.375\right)$$

$$L = 8 \cdot 0.625 = 5.0 \text{ m}$$

**Verification of Linearity:**

| Level L (m) | R_eff (Ω) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 4000 |
| 2 | 3000 |
| 4 | 2000 |
| 6 | 1000 |
| 8 | 0 |

The relationship is perfectly **linear**, which is a key advantage of this method.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Temperature Compensation for Ultrasonic Sensors

**Problem Setup:** An ultrasonic sensor is calibrated at **20 °C** (c₀ = 343 m/s). The same sensor is now used at **50 °C**. If the sensor assumes c = 343 m/s but the actual speed is different, calculate the **percentage error** in the distance measurement.

**Step 1 — Calculate actual speed of sound at 50 °C:**

$$c_{50} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot 50 = 331 + 30 = 361 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Express the actual distance using the actual speed:**

$$D_{actual} = \frac{c_{50} \cdot t}{2} = \frac{361 \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Express the measured (erroneous) distance using the assumed speed:**

$$D_{measured} = \frac{c_{20} \cdot t}{2} = \frac{343 \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the percentage error:**

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{D_{measured} - D_{actual}}{D_{actual}} \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{(343 - 361)}{361} \times 100\% = \frac{-18}{361} \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{Error} = -4.99\% \approx -5\%$$

**Conclusion:** Without temperature compensation, a 30 °C temperature rise causes approximately a **5% under-reading** in the measured distance. This is why every modern ultrasonic sensor has a built-in temperature sensor and automatic compensation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For Exam Use)

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix-style comparison is a high-yield topic. KTU frequently asks for tabular comparisons in Part B (7-mark sub-questions).

| Parameter | Ultrasonic Sensor | Resistance Tape | Rotating Paddle Switch |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Measured Variable** | Time of flight | Effective resistance | Motor torque / rotation state |
| **Output Signal** | 4–20 mA / HART | Resistance (Ω) or 4–20 mA | Relay contact (NO/NC) |
| **Power Supply** | 24 V DC (loop powered) | 24 V DC | 110/230 V AC or 24 V DC |
| **Installation** | Top of vessel | Top of vessel (suspended) | Side of vessel (at point) |
| **Material State** | Liquid / slurry | Conductive liquid | Bulk solid (powder/granules) |
| **Pressure Rating** | Typically atmospheric / low pressure | Up to 100 bar | Atmospheric to medium |
| **Temperature Rating** | −40 °C to +80 °C | Up to 250 °C (with care) | Up to 200 °C |
| **Failure Mode** | Loss of echo (safe failure) | Open circuit (predictable) | Motor failure (safe failure) |
| **Ingress Protection** | IP65 / IP67 | IP65 | IP65 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Ultrasonic Level Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[24V DC Power Supply] --> B[Microcontroller Unit]
    B --> C[Pulse Generator Circuit]
    C --> D[Piezoelectric Transducer]
    D --> E[Ultrasonic Wave Emission]
    E --> F[Process Material Surface]
    F --> G[Echo Reflection]
    G --> H[Echo Reception at Transducer]
    H --> I[Amplifier and Filter Stage]
    I --> J[Time of Flight Measurement]
    J --> K[Temperature Compensation Unit]
    K --> B
    B --> L[Display and 4-20mA Output]
    L --> M[PLC or DCS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The microcontroller triggers a pulse generator, which excites the transducer. The transducer emits an ultrasonic wave, which hits the liquid surface and reflects back. The same transducer receives the echo, which is amplified and filtered. The time of flight is measured, corrected for temperature, and converted to a level reading. The output is sent to a controller.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Resistance Tape Measurement Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Resistance Tape - Top Terminal] --> B[Wheatstone Bridge]
    C[Reference Resistor R1] --> B
    D[Reference Resistor R2] --> B
    E[Reference Resistor R3] --> B
    B --> F[Bridge Amplifier]
    F --> G[Linearization and Scaling]
    G --> H[4-20mA Current Loop Output]
    I[Conductive Liquid - Ground] --> A
    J[Tank Wall] --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Note:** The resistance tape forms one arm of a Wheatstone bridge. The conductive liquid (grounded via the tank wall) effectively short-circuits the submerged portion of the tape. The bridge becomes unbalanced in proportion to the level, and the amplifier converts this imbalance into a standard current signal.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Rotating Paddle Switch Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC or DC Mains Supply] --> B[Step Down Transformer]
    B --> C[Rectifier and Filter]
    C --> D[Low Power AC Motor]
    D --> E[Paddle Shaft and Blades]
    E --> F{Solid Material Present?}
    F -->|No| G[Paddle Rotates Freely]
    G --> H[Normal Status LED]
    F -->|Yes| I[Mechanical Stall and Slip Clutch]
    I --> J[Torque Spring Deflection]
    J --> K[Microswitch Actuation]
    K --> L[Alarm or Relay Output]
    K --> M[Stop Conveyor Signal]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Flow:** Power → Motor → Paddle. If the material is not in contact, the paddle spins and the system is in "normal" state. The instant material jams the paddle, a slip clutch protects the motor, a spring deflects, and a microswitch fires. This triggers an alarm and can stop the upstream feed conveyor.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Comparative Architecture of Three Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuous Level Measurement] --> B[Contact Type]
    A --> C[Non Contact Type]
    B --> D[Resistance Tape]
    B --> E[Rotating Paddle Switch]
    C --> F[Ultrasonic Sensor]
    D --> G[Wheatstone Bridge Circuit]
    D --> H[Liquid Conductivity Required]
    E --> I[Motor and Paddle Assembly]
    E --> J[Point Level Detection Only]
    F --> K[Time of Flight Measurement]
    F --> L[Acoustic Wave Propagation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Summary View:** This diagram consolidates the three devices covered in this module. Notice that resistance tape and rotating paddle are *contact* methods, while ultrasonic is *non-contact*. Also note the categorical split: resistance tape is *continuous*, but rotating paddle is *point* (binary).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define ultrasonic level measurement. State the formula relating distance to time of flight and explain why a divisor of 2 is used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Ultrasonic level measurement is a non-contact, continuous level measurement technique that uses high-frequency acoustic pulses to determine the distance from a transducer to the surface of a process material.

The distance is calculated using the formula:

$$D = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

where $D$ is the distance from the transducer to the surface, $c$ is the velocity of sound in the medium (typically 343 m/s in air at 20 °C), and $t$ is the measured round-trip time.

**The divisor of 2 is used because the measured time $t$ corresponds to a *round trip* (down to the surface and back up), whereas we require only the *one-way* distance.** Dividing by 2 correctly gives the one-way distance from the transducer to the reflecting surface.

---

> **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **List any three advantages and three disadvantages of the rotating paddle switch for level detection in bulk solids.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Advantages:**
1. **Simple and rugged construction** — no complex electronics, suitable for harsh industrial environments.
2. **Low cost** — one of the most economical point-level devices for solids.
3. **Independent of material electrical properties** — works regardless of conductivity, dielectric constant, or moisture content.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Moving parts** — the motor and bearings can wear out, requiring periodic maintenance.
2. **Only point-level detection** — does not provide a continuous level reading.
3. **Limited to bulk solids** — not suitable for liquids or very fine powders (which can pack into the bearings).
4. (Additional) Requires the material bulk density to be above a threshold (~100 g/L) for reliable operation.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an **ultrasonic level measurement system**. Discuss the factors that affect its accuracy. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A tank of total height **5.5 m** has an ultrasonic sensor mounted on its top. The speed of sound in the air above the liquid is **348 m/s**. The sensor measures a round-trip echo time of **24 milliseconds**. Calculate: (i) the empty distance, and (ii) the liquid level in the tank. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Working Principle (7 marks):**

The ultrasonic level measurement system consists of the following functional blocks (refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Pulse Generator:** Produces high-voltage electrical pulses (typically 50–500 V peak) at a pulse repetition frequency of 1–10 Hz.
2. **Piezoelectric Transducer:** Converts the electrical pulse into a mechanical ultrasonic wave (and conversely, the returning echo back into an electrical signal). The transducer operates at its resonant frequency (e.g., 50 kHz, 75 kHz, or 200 kHz).
3. **Propagation Path:** The ultrasonic wave travels through the gas/vapor space above the material.
4. **Reflection at Surface:** The wave reflects off the material surface due to acoustic impedance mismatch.
5. **Echo Reception & Amplification:** The weak returning echo is amplified by a low-noise amplifier and filtered to remove noise.
6. **Time Measurement:** A precision timer measures the round-trip time with microsecond resolution.
7. **Computation:** The microcontroller computes distance using $D = (c \cdot t) / 2$ and applies temperature compensation.
8. **Output:** The result is linearized and output as 4–20 mA, HART, or a digital protocol.

**Factors Affecting Accuracy:**
- **Temperature variation** of the air/vapor space (0.6 m/s per °C change in air).
- **Gas composition** (different gases have different c).
- **Foam, dust, and vapors** that absorb or scatter the acoustic beam.
- **Turbulence** of the liquid surface.
- **False echoes** from tank internals (agitators, ladders, heating coils).
- **Mounting** — improper nozzle geometry can cause reflections.
- **Transducer aging** and **temperature drift** of the transducer itself.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Total tank height: $H = 5.5$ m
- Speed of sound: $c = 348$ m/s
- Round-trip time: $t = 24 \text{ ms} = 24 \times 10^{-3}$ s

**(i) Empty distance D:**

$$D = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{348 \times 24 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$D = \frac{8.352}{2} = 4.176 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Liquid level L:**

$$L = H - D = 5.5 - 4.176 = 1.324 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion:** The empty distance is **4.176 m** and the liquid level is **1.324 m**.

---

> **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3 — Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **resistance tape method** of continuous liquid level measurement with a neat diagram. Derive the relationship between the effective resistance and the liquid level. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A resistance tape of total length **6 m** has a total resistance of **3000 Ω**. Find: (i) the sensitivity in Ω/cm, (ii) the effective resistance when the level is at **2.5 m**, and (iii) the level when the effective resistance is **1250 Ω**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**

The resistance tape method (refer to Mermaid schematic in Section 4.2) uses a precision resistance wire wound on an insulated core and enclosed in a perforated stainless-steel sheath. It is suspended vertically in the tank. The conductive liquid, grounded via the tank wall, enters through the perforations and contacts the resistance wire.

**Derivation:**

For a uniform resistance wire of total length $L_{total}$ and total resistance $R_{total}$:

$$R_{total} = \rho \cdot \frac{L_{total}}{A}$$

When the liquid is at level $L$, the submerged portion (length $L$) is short-circuited. The dry portion (length $L_{total} - L$) contributes the entire effective resistance:

$$R_{eff} = \rho \cdot \frac{(L_{total} - L)}{A}$$

Dividing the two:

$$\frac{R_{eff}}{R_{total}} = \frac{L_{total} - L}{L_{total}} = 1 - \frac{L}{L_{total}}$$

Solving for level $L$:

$$L = L_{total} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{R_{eff}}{R_{total}}\right)$$

This is a **linear relationship** between level and the complement of resistance.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $L_{total} = 6$ m = 600 cm
- $R_{total} = 3000$ Ω

**(i) Sensitivity:**

$$S = \frac{R_{total}}{L_{total}} = \frac{3000}{600} = 5 \text{ Ω/cm}$$

**(ii) Effective resistance at L = 2.5 m = 250 cm:**

$$R_{eff} = R_{total} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{L}{L_{total}}\right) = 3000 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2.5}{6}\right)$$

$$R_{eff} = 3000 \cdot \left(1 - 0.4167\right) = 3000 \cdot 0.5833$$

$$R_{eff} = 1750 \text{ Ω}$$

**(iii) Level at R_eff = 1250 Ω:**

$$L = L_{total} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{R_{eff}}{R_{total}}\right) = 6 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1250}{3000}\right)$$

$$L = 6 \cdot \left(1 - 0.4167\right) = 6 \cdot 0.5833 = 3.5 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion:** Sensitivity = **5 Ω/cm**, $R_{eff}$ at 2.5 m = **1750 Ω**, and Level at 1250 Ω = **3.5 m**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the divisor of 2 in the ultrasonic formula.** This is the single most common error. If a student writes $D = c \cdot t$ instead of $D = (c \cdot t)/2$, they lose a full mark even if the rest is correct.
> 2. **Confusing continuous and point level measurement.** Rotating paddle is a *point* device. If a student calls it a continuous measurement device, they will lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using the wrong reference point for level.** The "level" in a top-mounted ultrasonic sensor is calculated as $L = H - D$ (tank height minus empty distance), not as $D$ itself. Many students mistakenly report the empty distance as the level.
> 4. **Ignoring temperature compensation.** In numerical problems, if the speed of sound is not given, the student must use $c = 331 + 0.6T$ (in °C) or 343 m/s (at 20 °C). Not stating the assumed temperature is a minor mark loss.
> 5. **In resistance tape, confusing R_eff with ΔR.** $R_{eff}$ is the *remaining* (un-submerged) resistance, not the change. The change $\Delta R = R_{total} - R_{eff}$.
> 6. **In resistance tape, not specifying that the liquid must be conductive.** This is a frequently-asked conceptual question and is worth 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **The High-Density Bullet Summary — Read this the night before the exam:**

- **Continuous Level Measurement** provides a proportional output for the entire range, unlike point devices.
- **Ultrasonic Level Measurement** is **non-contact**, uses **time-of-flight** principle, and the governing formula is **D = (c × t) / 2**.
- The **divisor of 2** in the ultrasonic formula is because the measured time is for a *round trip*.
- The **speed of sound in air at 20 °C** is **c = 343 m/s** and changes by **0.6 m/s per °C**.
- Ultrasonic sensors are **affected by temperature, gas composition, foam, dust, and turbulence**.
- Modern ultrasonic sensors have a **built-in temperature sensor** for automatic compensation.
- The **blanking zone** (minimum measurable distance) of an ultrasonic sensor is typically **0.25–0.5 m**.
- **Resistance Tape Level Measurement** is a **contact, continuous** method for **conductive liquids only** (conductivity > 1 μS/cm).
- The resistance tape works by **short-circuiting** the submerged portion of a precision resistance wire.
- The **key formula** for resistance tape is **L = L_total × (1 − R_eff / R_total)** — a **linear** relationship.
- The **sensitivity** of a resistance tape is **S = R_total / L_total** (in Ω per unit length).
- The **Wheatstone bridge** is commonly used to measure the resistance change.
- **Rotating Paddle Switch** is a **POINT level** device for **bulk solids** — it is **NOT continuous**.
- The paddle is driven by a **small electric motor** at **1–5 RPM**.
- A **mechanical slip clutch** protects the motor from stall damage.
- A **microswitch** is actuated when the paddle is jammed by the material.
- Typical applications: **grain silos, cement hoppers, plastic pellet bins, fly-ash bunkers**.
- The paddle switch requires **bulk density > 100 g/L** and **particle size < 50 mm**.
- All three devices are **instruments of level** but differ in: contact type, output type, and application (liquid vs. solid).
- KTU expects you to know the **block diagram** of each — practice drawing them from memory.
- Numerical problems on **ultrasonic ToF** and **resistance tape sensitivity** are **high-probability** questions in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotational
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT865 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rotational

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Definition

In the context of **Industrial Instrumentation**, the term **"Rotational"** measurement refers to the comprehensive set of techniques, transducers, and instrument systems used to quantify parameters associated with **rotary motion** of a mechanical shaft, rotor, or rotating body. The primary measured quantities include:

1. **Rotational (Angular) Speed** – measured in **revolutions per minute (rpm)** or **radians per second (rad/s)**.
2. **Angular Displacement** – measured in degrees (°) or radians (rad).
3. **Angular Acceleration** – measured in rad/s².
4. **Torque (Rotational Force)** – measured in N·m.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Rotational measurement instrumentation encompasses non-contact and contact-type transducers that convert rotary mechanical motion (speed, displacement, acceleration) into proportional electrical signals (analog voltage, frequency, or digital pulses) for display, control, and feedback purposes in industrial automation."*

## 1.2 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|:------:|:---------|:--------------|
| $N$ | Rotational speed | **rpm** |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity | **rad/s** |
| $\theta$ | Angular displacement | **rad** |
| $\alpha$ | Angular acceleration | **rad/s²** |
| $T$ | Torque | **N·m** |
| $f$ | Output frequency | **Hz** |

**Conversion Relationships:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

$$f = \frac{N}{60} = \frac{\text{pulses per revolution} \times N}{60} \quad \text{[Hz]}$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Speedometer vs. Tachometer**
>
> Think of a **car dashboard**: the *speedometer* tells you how fast the **wheels (linear translation)** are moving, while the *tachometer* tells you how fast the **engine crankshaft (rotational motion)** is spinning. In industry, we cannot see the inside of a high-speed turbine or motor, but we *must* know its rotational health. **Rotational instruments act as the "speedometers" of industrial rotating machinery** — they let engineers "see" how fast, how stable, and how accurately a shaft is rotating, even when the machine is sealed inside a casing or running at 50,000 rpm.

**Key Industrial Applications:**
- **Electric motor** speed monitoring & control
- **Gas turbine & jet engine** RPM measurement
- **CNC machine spindle** feedback
- **Conveyor & pump** speed regulation
- **Gyroscopic navigation** in aircraft & ships
- **Torque** monitoring in dynamometers

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Rotational Measurement Instruments

Rotational instruments are classified based on the **output signal type** and **operating principle**:

```
ROTATIONAL MEASUREMENT INSTRUMENTS
├── Speed Measurement (Tachometers)
│   ├── Mechanical Tachometers
│   │   └── Centrifugal (Governor-type) Tachometer
│   ├── Electrical Tachometers
│   │   ├── DC Tachogenerator
│   │   ├── AC Tachogenerator (Induction type)
│   │   ├── Magnetic Pickup (Variable Reluctance)
│   │   └── Photoelectric / Optical Tachometer
│   └── Electronic Tachometers
│       ├── Frequency-to-Voltage Converter
│       └── Stroboscope
├── Angular Displacement Measurement
│   ├── Rotary Encoder (Incremental)
│   ├── Rotary Encoder (Absolute)
│   ├── Resolver
│   └── Synchro (Transmitter–Transformer pair)
├── Angular Acceleration
│   └── Angular Accelerometer (Piezoelectric / MEMS)
└── Torque Measurement
    ├── Prony Brake
    ├── Eddy-Current Dynamometer
    └── Strain-Gauge Torque Transducer
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why so many types?** The **KTU examiner expects** students to understand that *no single instrument* fits all applications — high-speed turbines favor non-contact optical devices, while low-speed heavy machinery may use centrifugal governors.

## 2.2 Operating Principle of Each Major Instrument

### 2.2.1 Centrifugal Tachometer (Mechanical)

- Uses **centrifugal force** on flyweights mounted on a vertical shaft.
- As speed increases, weights fly outward, lifting a sleeve.
- The sleeve motion is converted to pointer deflection via a lever linkage.

$$F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \quad \text{[N]}$$

where $m$ = mass of flyweight, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $r$ = radius of rotation.

### 2.2.2 DC Tachogenerator

- A small **DC generator** mechanically coupled to the rotating shaft.
- Output EMF is **directly proportional** to speed.

$$E_g = K \cdot \phi \cdot N \quad \text{[V]}$$

where $K$ = machine constant, $\phi$ = flux per pole (constant), $N$ = rpm.

- **Linear** output, simple, but **brush wear** is a limitation.

### 2.2.3 AC Tachogenerator (Induction Type)

- Two-phase induction generator: **reference (excitation) winding** + **output (quadrature) winding**.
- Output voltage is **proportional to speed**, **frequency equals rotation frequency**.

$$V_{out} = K \cdot \omega \quad \text{[V]}$$

- No brushes → **high reliability** → preferred in **servos, CNC, military**.

### 2.2.4 Magnetic Pickup (Variable Reluctance / Magnetic Tachometer)

- A **permanent magnet** with a coil; a **ferrous gear tooth** or notch passes near it.
- Each tooth generates a **pulse** in the coil (Faraday's law of induction).

$$e = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt} \quad \text{[V]}$$

- If gear has $Z$ teeth, **pulses per second** = $\frac{Z \cdot N}{60}$.

### 2.2.5 Photoelectric (Optical) Tachometer

- A **light beam** + **photodetector** + **reflective mark** on the shaft.
- Each reflection produces a **pulse**; pulses per unit time = rotational frequency.
- **Non-contact**, very high accuracy, suitable for small shafts.

### 2.2.6 Stroboscope

- A **flashing light** whose frequency is **adjusted to match** the rotation.
- When flash frequency **equals** rotation frequency, the shaft **appears stationary**.
- Can measure **very high speeds** (up to 1,00,000 rpm) without contact.

$$N = 60 \times f_{flash} \quad \text{[rpm]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The stroboscope works on the **"Persistence of Vision"** principle of the human eye. The KTU board often asks a 3-mark question on this.

### 2.2.7 Rotary Encoders

- **Incremental Encoder**: Outputs a **pulse train**; speed = pulse frequency / PPR.
- **Absolute Encoder**: Outputs a **unique binary (Gray) code** for every angular position; position is retained even after power-off.

**Resolution:**

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{\text{PPR}} \quad \text{[degrees per pulse]}$$

### 2.2.8 Resolver

- A **rotary transformer** with **two stator windings** at 90° electrical separation.
- Rotor is excited by a carrier signal; the two stator outputs are:

$$V_1 = V_m \sin(\omega_c t) \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

$$V_2 = V_m \sin(\omega_c t) \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

- $\theta$ = angular position of rotor. **Demodulation** recovers $\theta$.

### 2.2.9 Synchro (Transmitter–Transformer Pair)

- **Synchro Transmitter (CX)**: Rotor angle $\theta_1$ → induced stator EMFs.
- **Synchro Control Transformer (CT)**: Rotor angle $\theta_2$ → error voltage.
- **Error signal** drives motor until $\theta_1 = \theta_2$ (remote angular position transmission).

$$V_{error} = K \cdot \sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2)$$

### 2.2.10 Gyroscope

- A **spinning rotor** with high angular momentum exhibits **rigidity in space**.
- Precession rate:

$$\omega_p = \frac{T}{I \cdot \omega_s} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

where $T$ = applied torque, $I$ = moment of inertia, $\omega_s$ = spin velocity.

- Used in **inertial navigation**, **aircraft attitude indicators**, **missiles**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All KTU ESE questions on this module require these formulas. Memorize them!**

| # | Instrument | Key Formula | Output Type | Unit |
|:-:|:-----------|:------------|:-----------|:-----|
| 1 | Centrifugal tachometer | $F_c = m \omega^2 r$ | Mechanical displacement | N |
| 2 | DC tachogenerator | $E_g = K \phi N$ | DC voltage | V |
| 3 | AC tachogenerator | $V_{out} = K \omega$ | AC voltage (constant freq.) | V |
| 4 | Magnetic pickup | $e = -N \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$ | Pulse train | V (pulse) |
| 5 | Photoelectric tachometer | $f = \dfrac{Z \cdot N}{60}$ | Pulse train | Hz |
| 6 | Stroboscope | $N = 60 \cdot f_{flash}$ | Visual | rpm |
| 7 | Incremental encoder | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{\text{PPR}}$ | Digital pulses | ° / pulse |
| 8 | Resolver | $V_1 = V_m \sin(\omega_c t) \cos\theta$ | Modulated AC | V |
| 9 | Synchro | $V_{err} = K \sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2)$ | AC error voltage | V |
| 10 | Gyroscope | $\omega_p = \dfrac{T}{I \omega_s}$ | Angular rate | rad/s |
| 11 | Speed conversion | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular speed | rad/s |
| 12 | Torque (general) | $T = F \cdot r$ | Rotational force | N·m |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application | Preferred Instrument |
|:----------------|:------------|:---------------------|
| Power plants | Turbine RPM monitoring | Magnetic pickup + Stroboscope |
| CNC / Robotics | Spindle speed & position | AC Tachogenerator + Encoder |
| Automotive | Engine RPM, crankshaft | Magnetic pickup on flywheel teeth |
| Aerospace | Aircraft heading, attitude | Ring Laser Gyroscope, Fiber Optic Gyro |
| Process plants | Mixer, agitator speed | DC Tachogenerator / Hall-effect sensor |
| Servo systems | Closed-loop speed control | AC Tachogenerator in feedback |
| Shipbuilding | Stabilization, navigation | Mechanical / Optical Gyroscope |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Centrifugal Tachometer Working Equation

We derive the **deflection characteristic** — i.e., how pointer deflection relates to shaft speed.

### Setup

Consider two flyweights of mass $m$ each, pivoted at distance $r$ from the shaft axis. As the shaft rotates with angular speed $\omega$, the weights swing outward by an angle $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Identify forces on the flyweight:**

- Centrifugal force (outward, radial): $F_c = m \, \omega^2 \, (r + l \sin\alpha)$ where $l$ = length of arm.
- Weight component: $W = m g$.
- Spring restoring force: $F_s = k \cdot s$ (where $s$ = spring stretch).

**Step 2 — Take moments about the pivot:**

Equating the moment of centrifugal force to the restoring moment:

$$m \, \omega^2 \, r \cdot l \cos\alpha = K_{spring} \cdot \alpha$$

**Step 3 — For small $\alpha$ (small deflection), $\cos\alpha \approx 1$:**

$$m \, r \, l \, \omega^2 = K_{spring} \cdot \alpha$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$:**

$$m \, r \, l \cdot \left( \frac{2\pi N}{60} \right)^2 = K_{spring} \cdot \alpha$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\alpha$:**

$$\alpha = \underbrace{\left( \frac{4\pi^2 \, m \, r \, l}{3600 \, K_{spring}} \right)}_{\text{instrument constant } C} \cdot N^2$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = C \cdot N^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The pointer deflection is **proportional to the square of the speed**. Hence, the scale must be **non-linear** (compressed at low speed, expanded at high speed).

## 3.2 Derivation: DC Tachogenerator EMF Equation

**Step 1 — EMF induced in one conductor of length $L$ moving with velocity $v$ in flux density $B$:**

$$e_1 = B \, L \, v$$

**Step 2 — Total conductors = $Z$ (in the armature), connected in $A$ parallel paths:**

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$

**Step 3 — For a wave-wound machine, $A = 2$:**

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{120}$$

**Step 4 — Defining the machine constant** $K_a = \dfrac{\Phi Z P}{120}$:

$$\boxed{E_g = K_a \cdot N \quad [\text{V}]}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to angular speed form using** $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$E_g = \frac{60 \, K_a}{2\pi} \cdot \omega = K_b \cdot \omega$$

**Conclusion:** $E_g$ is **linearly proportional to speed** — this is the key advantage over centrifugal tachometers.

## 3.3 Derivation: Resolver Output Equations

The resolver has two stator windings (S1, S2) at 90° spatial separation and one rotor winding (R) excited by $V_r = V_m \sin(\omega_c t)$.

**Step 1 — Flux linking S1 (rotor at angle $\theta$):**

$$\phi_1 = \phi_0 \cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Flux linking S2:**

$$\phi_2 = \phi_0 \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Faraday's law on each stator winding:**

$$V_1 = -N_s \frac{d\phi_1}{dt} = -N_s \frac{d}{dt}\left(\phi_0 \cos\theta \cdot \sin(\omega_c t)\right)$$

Assuming $\theta$ is **slowly varying** (quasi-static) compared to $\omega_c$:

$$V_1 = -N_s \, \phi_0 \, \omega_c \cos\theta \cos(\omega_c t)$$

$$\boxed{V_1 = K \, \cos\theta \cdot \cos(\omega_c t)}$$

By similar reasoning for S2 (90° spatial shift):

$$\boxed{V_2 = K \, \sin\theta \cdot \cos(\omega_c t)}$$

**Step 4 — Recovering $\theta$:**

$$\tan\theta = \frac{V_2}{V_1}$$

This is how a resolver decodes **angular position**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Problem]**
>
> *A DC tachogenerator produces 100 V at 1500 rpm. Determine (a) the output voltage at 3000 rpm, (b) the tachogenerator constant, and (c) the output at 750 rpm.*

**Given:** $E_{g1} = 100$ V at $N_1 = 1500$ rpm.

**Find:** $E_{g2}$ at $N_2 = 3000$ rpm, $K_a$, $E_{g3}$ at 750 rpm.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Use linear relation** $E_g = K_a \cdot N$:

$$K_a = \frac{E_{g1}}{N_1} = \frac{100}{1500} = 0.0667 \text{ V/rpm}$$

**Step (b) — At 3000 rpm:**

$$E_{g2} = 0.0667 \times 3000 = \boxed{200 \text{ V}}$$

**Step (c) — At 750 rpm:**

$$E_{g3} = 0.0667 \times 750 = \boxed{50 \text{ V}}$$

**Alternative check using ratio** $\dfrac{E_{g2}}{E_{g1}} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$:

$$E_{g2} = 100 \times \frac{3000}{1500} = 200 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.5 Worked Example: Stroboscope Frequency

> **[KTU Numerical Practice]**
>
> *A stroboscope is used to view a rotating shaft. The shaft appears stationary when the flash frequency is set to 250 Hz. Find the shaft speed in rpm.*

**Solution:**

$$N = 60 \times f_{flash} = 60 \times 250 = \boxed{15{,}000 \text{ rpm}}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Tachometers

| Parameter | Centrifugal | DC Tachogen. | AC Tachogen. | Magnetic Pickup | Photoelectric | Stroboscope |
|:----------|:-----------:|:------------:|:------------:|:---------------:|:-------------:|:-----------:|
| Output | Mechanical | DC voltage | AC voltage | Pulses | Pulses | Visual |
| Linearity | **Non-linear** ($N^2$) | Linear | Linear | Linear | Linear | Linear |
| Contact / Non-contact | Contact | Contact | Contact | **Non-contact** | **Non-contact** | **Non-contact** |
| Speed Range (rpm) | 100 – 5000 | up to 10,000 | up to 20,000 | up to 50,000 | up to 100,000 | up to 1,00,000 |
| Direction Sense | No | Yes (polarity) | Yes (phase) | No (can add) | Yes (with 2 ch.) | No |
| Maintenance | Medium | High (brushes) | Low | None | None | None |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Medium–High | Low | Medium | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification Hierarchy of Rotational Instruments

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ROTATIONAL MEASUREMENT] --> B[SPEED]
    A --> C[ANGULAR DISPLACEMENT]
    A --> D[ANGULAR ACCELERATION]
    A --> E[TORQUE]

    B --> B1[Mechanical: Centrifugal Governor]
    B --> B2[Electrical: DC Tachogenerator]
    B --> B3[Electrical: AC Tachogenerator]
    B --> B4[Non-contact: Magnetic Pickup]
    B --> B5[Non-contact: Photoelectric]
    B --> B6[Stroboscope]

    C --> C1[Incremental Encoder]
    C --> C2[Absolute Encoder]
    C --> C3[Resolver]
    C --> C4[Synchro Transmitter Transformer]

    D --> D1[Piezoelectric Accelerometer]
    D --> D2[MEMS Gyroscope]

    E --> E1[Prony Brake]
    E --> E2[Eddy Current Dynamometer]
    E --> E3[Strain Gauge Torque Transducer]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Speed Control Using AC Tachogenerator

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Reference Speed Input] --> B[Summing Junction]
    B --> C[Amplifier and Power Stage]
    C --> D[DC Servo Motor]
    D --> E[Rotating Shaft Output]
    E --> F[Load]
    E --> G[AC Tachogenerator coupled to shaft]
    G --> H[Feedback Voltage]
    H --> B
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Magnetic Pickup (Variable Reluctance) Working

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Rotating Shaft with Gear Teeth] --> B[Tooth approaches Magnet]
    B --> C[Magnetic Flux Rises]
    C --> D[Faraday Induces EMF Pulse]
    D --> E[Coil Output Pulse]
    E --> F[Frequency Counter]
    F --> G[Display rpm = 60 f divided by Z]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Synchro Remote Angular Position Transmission

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Shaft at Remote Location] --> B[Synchro Transmitter CX]
    B --> C[Three wire Stator Link]
    C --> D[Synchro Control Transformer CT]
    D --> E[Error Voltage Amplifier]
    E --> F[Servo Motor]
    F --> G[Local Shaft]
    G --> H[Mechanical Feedback to CT Rotor]
    H --> D
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Resolver Signal Conditioning

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Resolver Rotor Position theta] --> B[Excitation Vm sin omega_c t]
    A --> C[Stator 1 Output V1]
    A --> D[Stator 2 Output V2]
    C --> E[Demodulator 1]
    D --> F[Demodulator 2]
    B --> E
    B --> F
    E --> G[Arctan Computed: tan theta equals V2 by V1]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Digital Display of theta]
```

## 4.6 Block Diagram: Gyroscope Precession Principle

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Spinning Rotor at omega_s] --> B[High Angular Momentum H]
    B --> C[Rigidity in Space]
    C --> D[External Torque T applied]
    D --> E[Precession at omega_p]
    E --> F[omega_p equals T divided by I omega_s]
    F --> G[Used for: Navigation, Stabilization, Attitude Control]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above are block-level functional representations. For physical shape and mechanical construction, students are expected to draw **labeled sketches** (which appear in the KTU answer scripts) — the system-level understanding conveyed here is what examiners look for in the **"Explain with block diagram"** questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the working principle of a stroboscope. State one industrial application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A stroboscope is a **non-contact rotational speed measuring instrument** that uses a **variable-frequency flashing light** directed at the rotating shaft.

**Working:** When the flash frequency $f_{flash}$ exactly **equals** the shaft's rotation frequency, the shaft appears **stationary** to the human eye (due to **persistence of vision**). The shaft speed is computed as:

$$N = 60 \times f_{flash} \quad \text{[rpm]}$$

**Industrial Application:** Measurement of **high-speed rotating machinery** such as **gas turbine blades, fan blades, motor rotors** in power plants and aerospace — without any physical contact with the shaft, eliminating loading effects.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for principle, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for application)** ✓

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Distinguish between an Incremental encoder and an Absolute encoder (any 3 points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|:-----:|:--------------------|:-----------------|
| 1 | Outputs a **pulse train** proportional to rotation | Outputs a **unique binary/Gray code** for every angle |
| 2 | **Loses position** on power failure (needs homing) | **Retains position** after power-off |
| 3 | Lower cost, simpler circuitry | Higher cost, complex decoding |
| 4 | Resolution = $360°$/PPR | Resolution = $360°$/$2^n$ for n-bit code |
| 5 | Used in speed measurement, CNC feedback | Used in robotics, antenna positioning |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often **forget to write the working formula** before substituting values → lose 1–2 marks.
> - **Block diagrams are mandatory** in 7-mark sub-questions; drawing only a text description loses 2 marks.
> - In Synchro/Resolver questions, students confuse **transmitter (CX)** with **control transformer (CT)** — know the difference!
> - Always state the **principle of operation** in the **first sentence** of the answer.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **DC tachogenerator** for speed measurement. Derive its **EMF equation** and state two advantages over a centrifugal tachometer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a **synchro transmitter–control transformer pair** for **remote angular position indication**. State the role of the **error voltage**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Principle (1 mark):**
A DC tachogenerator is a small **DC generator** mechanically coupled to the shaft whose speed is to be measured. It works on **Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction** — the EMF generated at the brushes is **directly proportional** to the rotational speed of the armature.

**Construction (1 mark):**
- Permanent magnet field (or separate field winding with constant current).
- Armature winding on a slotted rotating core.
- Brush and commutator assembly for DC output.
- Output terminals connected to a voltmeter (calibrated in rpm).

**Working (2 marks):**
- When the shaft rotates, the armature conductors cut the magnetic flux.
- An EMF is induced, collected by the commutator and delivered as a DC voltage.
- The voltmeter reads a voltage proportional to speed: $E_g = K_a \cdot N$.
- The polarity of the output **reverses** if the rotation direction reverses — so the instrument can **sense direction**.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

Starting from the basic EMF equation of a DC machine:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$

For a wave-wound machine, $A = 2$:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, P}{120} \cdot N = K_a \cdot N$$

where $K_a = \dfrac{\Phi Z P}{120}$ is the tachogenerator constant (V/rpm).

**Two Advantages over Centrifugal Tachometer (1 mark):**
1. **Linear** output vs. **non-linear** ($N^2$) in centrifugal.
2. **Electrical** output — easily transmitted, recorded, and used in **closed-loop control**.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Construction (1.5 marks):**

A **synchro system** has two units:

- **Synchro Transmitter (CX):** Rotor is driven by the **remote shaft** whose angle $\theta_1$ is to be transmitted. Stator has a **3-phase winding** (Y-connected).
- **Synchro Control Transformer (CT):** Stator winding is connected to the transmitter's stator via a **3-wire link**. Its rotor is mounted on the **local shaft**.

**Working (3 marks):**

- The transmitter's rotor is excited by an **AC reference voltage**.
- As the rotor turns, **EMFs are induced in the 3 stator windings** — these are functions of $\theta_1$.
- The stator EMFs cause **currents** in the 3-wire link, which flow into the CT's stator and produce a **magnetic field in the CT** equivalent to angle $\theta_1$.
- The CT rotor, set at angle $\theta_2$, cuts this field and produces an **error voltage** at its terminals:

$$V_{err} = K \cdot \sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2)$$

- This error signal is **amplified** and drives a **servo motor**, which rotates the local shaft (and CT rotor) until $\theta_2 = \theta_1$.
- At that point, the error voltage = 0, and the system is in **null balance**.

**Role of Error Voltage (1 mark):**
The error voltage is the **control signal** that drives the servo motor to make the local shaft **follow** the remote shaft, enabling **remote angular position transmission** (used in radar antennas, missile guidance, large valve control).

**Block Diagram (1.5 marks):** *(Refer Section 4.4 of these notes.)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram, the working of an **incremental rotary encoder** for angular displacement measurement. A 500-PPR encoder is mounted on a shaft. Calculate the (i) angular resolution, (ii) output frequency at 1200 rpm, and (iii) total pulses in one complete rotation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the construction and working of a **mechanical gyroscope**. Derive the **precession equation** and explain its use in an **aircraft attitude indicator**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Principle (1 mark):**
An **incremental rotary encoder** converts angular displacement of a shaft into a **train of digital pulses**. It uses an **optical (or magnetic) code disc** with alternating transparent/opaque (or magnetic) sectors.

**Construction (1 mark):**
- A **rotating code disc** with PPR equally spaced slots.
- A **light source (LED)** on one side and a **photodetector** on the other side.
- Output channels A and B (in **quadrature**) for direction sensing.

**Working (1.5 marks):**
- As the disc rotates, light passes through the slots → produces **pulses** in the photodetector.
- Each pulse corresponds to a known angular increment.
- A **counter** totals pulses; **frequency** of pulses gives **speed**; **total count** gives **displacement**.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to encoder block structure in Section 4.)*

**Numerical Solution (2.5 marks):**

Given: PPR = $Z = 500$, $N = 1200$ rpm.

**(i) Angular resolution:**

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{Z} = \frac{360}{500} = \boxed{0.72° \text{ per pulse}}$$

**(ii) Output frequency:**

$$f = \frac{Z \cdot N}{60} = \frac{500 \times 1200}{60} = \boxed{10{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 10 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(iii) Pulses per revolution:**

$$\text{Pulses per rev} = Z = \boxed{500 \text{ pulses}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Principle (1 mark):**
A gyroscope is a **spinning rotor** mounted in gimbals. It exploits the **rigidity of a spinning body in space** and the **precession phenomenon** to sense orientation and angular velocity.

**Construction (1 mark):**
- A **heavy symmetrical rotor** (spinning mass) driven at high speed by a motor.
- The rotor is mounted on an **inner gimbal**, which is mounted on an **outer gimbal**, allowing rotation about **3 axes**: spin, inner gimbal (pitch), outer gimbal (roll/yaw).

**Working (2 marks):**
- The rotor spins at high $\omega_s$ → high angular momentum $H = I \omega_s$.
- Due to **conservation of angular momentum**, the spin axis **resists change** in direction → **rigidity in space**.
- If an **external torque $T$** is applied (e.g., aircraft turns), the spin axis does **not** tilt in the torque direction — it **precesses 90° ahead** in the rotation direction.

**Derivation of Precession (2 marks):**

Rate of change of angular momentum = applied torque:

$$T = \frac{dH}{dt} = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p$$

Solving for precession rate:

$$\boxed{\omega_p = \frac{T}{I \cdot \omega_s}}$$

This shows: **higher spin rate → lower precession** (more stable).

**Aircraft Attitude Indicator Use (1 mark):**
- The rotor spin axis is set **fixed in space** (vertical reference).
- The aircraft body rotates around the gyroscope.
- The gimbal mechanism transfers the **relative angle** between aircraft and the stable vertical reference to a **display dial**, showing **pitch and roll** to the pilot.
- Used in **artificial horizon instruments**, **heading indicators**, and **turn coordinators**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Rotational Measurement**

- [x] **Speed** is measured in **rpm**; **angular velocity** in **rad/s**; convert using $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.
- [x] **Centrifugal tachometer** → **non-linear** ($N^2$ relationship) → **square-law scale**.
- [x] **DC Tachogenerator** → **linear** → EMF $E_g = K_a N$ → **direction-sensing** by polarity.
- [x] **AC Tachogenerator** → no brushes → used in **servo feedback loops**.
- [x] **Magnetic pickup** → **non-contact** → works on **variable reluctance** → output is a **pulse train**.
- [x] **Photoelectric tachometer** → uses **light + photodiode** + reflective mark → **high accuracy**.
- [x] **Stroboscope** → **persistence of vision** → $N = 60 f_{flash}$ → **highest speed range**, no contact.
- [x] **Incremental encoder** → **pulses** → loses absolute position on power-off.
- [x] **Absolute encoder** → **Gray/binary code** → **retains position** even after power loss.
- [x] **Resolver** → two outputs: $V_1 \propto \cos\theta$, $V_2 \propto \sin\theta$ → recover via $\tan\theta = V_2/V_1$.
- [x] **Synchro pair (CX–CT)** → error voltage $V_{err} = K \sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2)$ → **remote angular position transmission**.
- [x] **Gyroscope** → precession rate $\omega_p = \dfrac{T}{I \omega_s}$ → used for **navigation and attitude reference**.
- [x] Always draw a **block diagram** for 7-mark questions — the KTU examiner awards up to **2 marks** for a clean, labeled diagram.
- [x] Always **state the working principle first** in your answer.
- [x] **Numerical problems** demand units in every step — a missing unit may cost 0.5–1 mark.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. Confusing **resolver** (rotary transformer) with **synchro** (3-stator winding) — their equations look similar but constructions differ.
> 2. Forgetting to convert **rpm to rad/s** or vice versa in numerical problems.
> 3. Writing $E_g = K \phi N$ without defining the constants.
> 4. Stating "stroboscope measures speed" without mentioning **persistence of vision** — examiner will deduct marks.
> 5. Confusing **precession direction** — precession is **90° ahead** of the applied torque, not in the same direction.

---

**End of Module 4 — Rotational Measurement Notes** ✅
